With `SN1` in place, the anode-cathode waveform on **Channel 1** shows a soft, exponential rise; without it, the same waveform shows a sharp vertical spike limited only by the device's breakover voltage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a snubber circuit and list its two main functions.

> **Model Answer:**
> A snubber circuit is an auxiliary network connected across a power semiconductor switching device to protect it from switching transients. Its two main functions are:
> 1. To limit the rate of rise of voltage ($dv/dt$) across the device during turn-off.
> 2. To limit the rate of rise of current ($di/dt$) through the device during turn-on and to absorb switching losses.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the purpose of connecting a resistor in series with the snubber capacitor?

> **Model Answer:**
> The series resistor $R_s$ provides a **discharge path** for the snubber capacitor $C_s$ between successive switching cycles. Without $R_s$, the energy $\frac{1}{2} C_s V_s^2$ stored in the capacitor at turn-off would dump back into the switch as a destructive inrush current at the next turn-on. The resistor dissipates this energy as heat in a controlled manner.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Turn-off Snubber Design**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]** A chopper circuit feeds an inductive load drawing $I_L = 20 \, A$ from a DC source of $V_s = 200 \, V$. The switching frequency is $f_{sw} = 2 \, kHz$ and the SCR has a maximum tolerable $dv/dt$ of $100 \, V/\mu s$.

**(a)** Design the values of $C_s$ and $R_s$ for a turn-off RC snubber. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Calculate the power that the snubber resistor must dissipate and recommend a suitable standard wattage. (7 Marks)

---

> **Model Solution for Part (a):**
> 
> **Step 1: Snubber Capacitor Sizing**
> 
> $$C_s \ge \frac{I_L}{\left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)_{max}}$$
> 
> Substituting the given values:
> 
> $$C_s \ge \frac{20}{100 \times 10^6}$$
> 
> $$C_s \ge 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \, F$$
> 
> $$\boxed{C_s = 0.2 \, \mu F}$$
> 
> **Step 2: Minimum Off-Time of the Chopper**
> 
> $$T = \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{1}{2000} = 500 \, \mu s$$
> 
> Assume a 50 % duty cycle, so the off-time is:
> 
> $$t_{off} = 0.5 \times 500 = 250 \, \mu s$$
> 
> **Step 3: Snubber Resistor for 90 % Discharge**
> 
> $$R_s = \frac{t_{off}}{2.3026 \times C_s}$$
> 
> $$R_s = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{2.3026 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-6}}$$
> 
> $$R_s = \frac{250}{0.4605} = 542.9 \, \Omega$$
> 
> Use the nearest standard E12 value:
> 
> $$\boxed{R_s = 560 \, \Omega}$$
> 
> **Verification:** Time constant $\tau = R_s C_s = 560 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-6} = 112 \, \mu s$, which is less than the off-time of 250 μs ✓

> **Model Solution for Part (b):**
> 
> **Step 1: Energy per Switching Cycle**
> 
> $$E_s = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_s^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \times (200)^2$$
> 
> $$E_s = 0.5 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 \times 10^4 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \, J$$
> 
> **Step 2: Power Dissipated in the Resistor**
> 
> $$P_s = E_s \times f_{sw} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^3 = 8 \, W$$
> 
> **Step 3: Apply Safety Factor of 2**
> 
> $$P_{rating} = 2 \times P_s = 16 \, W$$
> 
> $$\boxed{\text{Use a 25 W wirewound resistor for long-term reliability.}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Turn-on Snubber and Overvoltage Clamp**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]** A DC-DC buck converter using an IGBT switches $V_s = 400 \, V$ at $f_{sw} = 10 \, kHz$ into a load that draws $I_L = 15 \, A$. The IGBT's maximum $di/dt$ rating is $50 \, A/\mu s$ and the leakage inductance of the commutation loop is measured to be $L_{leak} = 5 \, \mu H$.

**(a)** Design a turn-on (series) snubber inductor $L_s$ to limit $di/dt$ to a safe value. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Design an overvoltage clamp capacitor such that the peak voltage across the switch never exceeds $V_{clamp} = 600 \, V$. (7 Marks)

---

> **Model Solution for Part (a):**
> 
> **Step 1: Apply the di/dt Formula**
> 
> $$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_s}{L_s} \le \left( \frac{di}{dt} \right)_{max}$$
> 
> **Step 2: Solve for $L_s$**
> 
> $$L_s \ge \frac{V_s}{\left( \frac{di}{dt} \right)_{max}}$$
> 
> $$L_s \ge \frac{400}{50 \times 10^6}$$
> 
> $$L_s \ge 8 \times 10^{-6} \, H$$
> 
> $$\boxed{L_s = 8 \, \mu H}$$
> 
> **Step 3: Sanity Check Using the L/R Time Constant**
> 
> Assuming load resistance $R_{load} = V_s / I_L = 400/15 = 26.7 \, \Omega$:
> 
> $$\tau_L = \frac{L_s}{R_{load}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{26.7} = 0.3 \, \mu s$$
> 
> This is much less than the switching period $T = 1/10000 = 100 \, \mu s$, so the inductor behaves as a wire during steady state ✓
> 
> **Step 4: Practical Recommendation**
> 
> Use an air-core or ferrite-core inductor rated for at least $I_L = 15 \, A$ continuous current, with a saturation current of 30 A or more.

> **Model Solution for Part (b):**
> 
> **Step 1: Energy Equating Method**
> 
> The magnetic energy stored in the leakage inductance at the instant of turn-off is:
> 
> $$E_L = \frac{1}{2} L_{leak} I_L^2$$
> 
> The extra electrical energy that the clamp capacitor must absorb to raise the voltage from $V_s$ to $V_{clamp}$ is:
> 
> $$E_C = \frac{1}{2} C_s \left( V_{clamp}^2 - V_s^2 \right)$$
> 
> **Step 2: Equate and Solve for $C_s$**
> 
> $$\frac{1}{2} L_{leak} I_L^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_s \left( V_{clamp}^2 - V_s^2 \right)$$
> 
> $$C_s = \frac{L_{leak} \, I_L^2}{V_{clamp}^2 - V_s^2}$$
> 
> **Step 3: Substitute the Numerical Values**
> 
> $$C_s = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times (15)^2}{(600)^2 - (400)^2}$$
> 
> $$C_s = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 225}{360000 - 160000}$$
> 
> $$C_s = \frac{1.125 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^5}$$
> 
> $$C_s = 5.625 \times 10^{-9} \, F$$
> 
> $$\boxed{C_s = 5.6 \, nF \text{ (use a 6.8 nF standard value for safety margin)}}$$
> 
> **Step 4: Discharge Resistor for the Clamp**
> 
> Allow 5 time-constants for full discharge within the off-period $t_{off} = 50 \, \mu s$:
> 
> $$R_{clamp} \le \frac{t_{off}}{5 C_s} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 5.6 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.78 \, k\Omega$$
> 
> Use the standard value $R_{clamp} = 1.5 \, k\Omega, \, 2 \, W$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Students often write $C_s = 0.2$ instead of $0.2 \, \mu F$ when answering. This is treated as a **3-mark deduction** by the examiner because it changes the physical meaning by 6 orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Missing Safety Factor:** If you give a resistor wattage *equal* to the calculated $P_s$ without a 1.5× to 2× safety margin, you lose **2 marks**. The resistor will thermally run away and burn out within hours.
> 3. **Confusing Turn-on and Turn-off Snubbers:** A turn-on snubber is in **series** with the load (inductor), and a turn-off snubber is in **parallel** with the switch (RC). Mixing these up costs **5 marks** — the entire "understanding" credit.
> 4. **Forgetting the Discharge Path:** A common viva trap — if you specify $C_s$ but forget $R_s$, the examiner will mark the snubber design as incomplete and deduct **4 marks**.
> 5. **Using Non-Standard Values:** Always quote the **nearest E12 standard resistor value** and the **nearest E24 standard capacitor value**. Non-standard values are physically unobtainable in the lab.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Snubber Circuits (PCEIL607 – Experiment 2):**

- A **snubber circuit** is a protective auxiliary network across a power semiconductor switch.
- Its **two main functions** are: (1) limit $dv/dt$ at turn-off and (2) limit $di/dt$ at turn-on.
- The **turn-off RC snubber** is connected in **parallel** with the switch.
- The **turn-on RL snubber** is connected in **series** with the load.
- The **minimum snubber capacitor** is given by $C_s \ge \frac{I_L}{(dv/dt)_{max}}$.
- The **snubber resistor** is sized so that $C_s$ is **at least 90 % discharged** between cycles: $R_s = \frac{t_{off}}{2.3026 \, C_s}$.
- **Snubber power dissipation** is $P_s = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_s^2 f_{sw}$; always apply a 2× safety factor on resistor wattage.
- The **overvoltage clamp** capacitor size is found by equating magnetic energy in leakage inductance to additional electrical energy in the capacitor: $C_s = \frac{L_{leak} I_L^2}{V_{clamp}^2 - V_s^2}$.
- In the lab, the CRO channel is connected across the **anode–cathode** of the SCR to observe the $V_{ak}$ waveform with and without the snubber.
- A **smooth exponential rise** of $V_{ak}$ confirms proper snubber action; a **sharp vertical spike** indicates absence or mis-design of the snubber.
- Standard lab values: $R_s \approx 470 \, \Omega$ and $C_s \approx 0.1 \, \mu F$ are typical starting points for a 1 A, 400 V SCR.
- Always use a **wirewound or metal-oxide resistor** for the snubber $R_s$ to handle the repetitive pulse loading.
- **Viva-favourite questions:** "What happens if $R_s$ is too large?" (capacitor does not discharge, energy accumulates, switch overheats) and "What happens if $R_s$ is too small?" (capacitor dumps energy back into the switch at the next turn-on, causing a current spike).
- The **RC time constant** of the snubber must be **less than the minimum off-time** of the converter; otherwise, the capacitor never discharges fully.
- Snubbers do not "eliminate" switching losses — they **transfer** the loss from the semiconductor die to a passive component that can tolerate it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Three-Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier** (also called a **6-Pulse Uncontrolled Rectifier** or **Graetz Bridge**) is a power electronic converter topology that uses **six uncontrolled power diodes** arranged in a three-phase bridge configuration to convert a balanced three-phase AC supply into a controlled-magnitude DC output. The output voltage magnitude is fixed by the supply voltage and transformer turns ratio since diodes are passive switching elements with no gate control. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A 6-pulse diode bridge rectifier produces an output with **6 pulses per fundamental cycle** of the supply frequency. With a standard **50 Hz** supply, the fundamental ripple frequency at the DC output is **6 × 50 = 300 Hz**, making it inherently smoother than single-phase or three-phase half-wave rectifiers. ### Circuit Topology Summary | Parameter | Specification | |---|---| | Number of Diodes | **6** (D1–D6) | | AC Supply Phases | **3** (R, Y, B) | | Output Pulses per Cycle | **6** | | Most Conductive Diode Pair | One from +ve group, one from –ve group | | Each Diode Conduction Angle | **120° (2π/3 rad)** | | Number of Diodes Conducting Simultaneously | **2** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 6 Pulses?** > Each phase of the 3-phase supply feeds two diodes (one for positive half-cycle to the +DC rail, one for negative half-cycle to the –DC rail). Across one full 360° cycle, **6 commutation events** occur — this is why it is called a **6-pulse rectifier**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Water Wheel" Imagine a three-river confluence (R, Y, B rivers) feeding a common water tank. The water tank is your DC load. You have **six sluice gates** (the six diodes) installed in the channels: - **Three "uphill" gates (D1, D3, D5)** at the top that connect the highest-pressure river to the tank's top inlet. - **Three "downhill" gates (D2, D4, D6)** at the bottom that connect the lowest-pressure river to the tank's bottom outlet. At any instant, the **river with the highest water level** automatically opens its top gate (its diode becomes forward-biased), while the **river with the lowest water level** automatically opens its bottom gate. The tank's water level (DC output voltage) is always the difference between the highest and lowest rivers' levels — never the average. This is exactly how a 3-phase diode bridge behaves electrically: > **Highest phase voltage** → feeds the **+DC terminal** > **Lowest phase voltage** → feeds the **–DC terminal** --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Constants (MUST MEMORIZE):** > - Line-to-line RMS voltage: $V_L$ (volts) > - Phase RMS voltage: $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ > - Peak phase voltage: $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{ph} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} \cdot V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ > - Peak line-to-line voltage: $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m = \sqrt{6} \cdot V_{ph}$ > - Output ripple fundamental frequency: $f_r = 6f$ where $f$ is supply frequency > - Supply frequency (India): $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ → Ripple = **300 Hz** # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Phase-by-Phase Analysis A balanced 3-phase supply has three sinusoidal voltages displaced by 120°: $$V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$V_Y = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$V_B = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ ### Conduction Logic (The "Highest & Lowest" Rule) > The diode that connects the **most positive phase** to the **+DC rail** is forward-biased and conducts. > The diode that connects the **most negative phase** to the **–DC rail** is forward-biased and conducts. > All other 4 diodes are reverse-biased and OFF. ### Conduction Sequence (KTU High-Yield Table) | Interval | Conduction Duration | Conducting Diodes | Phase Feeding +DC | Phase Feeding –DC | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0° to 60° | **D1, D6** | R (most +ve) | B (most –ve) | | 2 | 60° to 120° | **D1, D2** | R (most +ve) | Y (most –ve) | | 3 | 120° to 180° | **D3, D2** | Y (most +ve) | Y (most –ve) — wait, **D3, D4** | | 4 | 180° to 240° | **D3, D4** | Y (most +ve) | B (most –ve) | | 5 | 240° to 300° | **D5, D4** | B (most +ve) | B (most –ve) — wait, **D5, D6** | | 6 | 300° to 360° | **D5, D6** | B (most +ve) | R (most –ve) | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for the conducting pair sequence:** > **D1–D6 → D1–D2 → D3–D2 → D3–D4 → D5–D4 → D5–D6 → D1–D6 (repeats)** > The odd-numbered diodes (D1, D3, D5) feed the +DC rail in cyclic order. > The even-numbered diodes (D2, D4, D6) feed the –DC rail in cyclic order. ### Why Each Diode Conducts for 120° - D1 is forward-biased whenever $V_R > V_Y$ **AND** $V_R > V_B$. This condition is satisfied for a span of **120°** per cycle (centered around the peak of $V_R$). - Same for D3 around $V_Y$ peak, D5 around $V_B$ peak. - Each pair conduction lasts 60°, but since the "highest phase" stays highest for 120°, each diode conducts for 120°. --- ## 2.2 Output Voltage — Line-to-Line Behavior The output voltage $V_o$ at any instant is the difference between the most positive and most negative phase voltages — i.e., the **line-to-line voltage** at that instant. $$V_o(\omega t) = V_{line-to-line}(\omega t)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the heart of KTU 3-phase rectifier problems:** > The output waveform is **NOT** a phase voltage waveform — it is a **line-to-line voltage waveform** with 6 humps per cycle. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below contains **every formula** you need for KTU 2024 lab viva and university exam on this topic. | # | Parameter | Formula | Derivation Basis | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Average DC Output Voltage (No Load) | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_m = \dfrac{3V_{mL}}{\pi}$ | Average of $V_{mL}\cos(\theta)$ over 60° | | 2 | In terms of Line RMS | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_{ph,RMS} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{L,RMS}$ | Substituting $V_m = \sqrt{2}V_{ph}$ | | 3 | Numerical Constant | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} \approx 1.654$ | Pure math | | 4 | Numerical Constant | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \approx 1.350$ | Pure math | | 5 | RMS Output Voltage | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{3} V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi}}$ | RMS of $V_{mL}\cos(\theta)$ | | 6 | Numerical Value of $V_{rms}/V_m$ | $V_{rms} \approx 1.6554 \, V_m$ | Computed | | 7 | Ripple Factor (Unfiltered) | $\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1} \approx 0.0418$ | Definition | | 8 | Rectification Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2} \approx 99.83\%$ | For purely resistive load | | 9 | Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) per diode | $V_{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \, V_m$ | Line-to-line peak | | 10 | Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) | $TUF = \dfrac{V_{dc} I_{dc}}{V_{L,RMS} I_{L,RMS}} = 0.9552$ | For resistive load | | 11 | Output Frequency (ripple) | $f_o = 6f$ | 6 pulses per supply cycle | | 12 | Diode Conduction Angle | $\theta_c = 120° = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ rad | Topology | | 13 | Form Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} \approx 1.0009$ | Definition | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Industrial DC Motor Drives** — large DC drives in steel rolling mills, paper mills, and printing presses (historically). 2. **HVDC Converter Stations** — first stage of conversion before inversion in High-Voltage DC transmission lines. 3. **Battery Chargers** — for high-power EV charging stations and telecom backup systems. 4. **DC Welding Power Supplies** — provides near-constant DC current. 5. **Electrochemical & Electroplating Industries** — aluminum smelting, electroplating baths. 6. **Front-End of Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** — uncontrolled diode bridge + DC link capacitor + inverter. 7. **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** — initial AC-to-DC conversion stage. 8. **Traction Substations** — DC supply for electric trains and metros. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Industry Relevance:** > In modern designs, the uncontrolled 3-phase diode bridge is often used as the **input stage of an AC-DC-AC converter** (VFD), where the DC link capacitor then feeds an IGBT-based inverter. Understanding the diode bridge behavior is essential for analyzing input current harmonics, power factor, and DC link ripple design. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convention Used:** > - $V_m$ = Peak phase voltage (peak of $V_R$, $V_Y$, or $V_B$) > - $V_{mL}$ = Peak line-to-line voltage = $\sqrt{3} V_m$ > - All derivations are for a **highly inductive load** or **no-load (capacitor-fed)** depending on context, but the **average DC voltage formula is the same for all load types** because it is determined solely by source voltages. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average DC Output Voltage ($V_{dc}$) ### Step 1: Set Up the Output Waveform Equation Between $\omega t = 0°$ and $\omega t = 60°$, the most positive phase is R and the most negative phase is B. The output voltage is the line-to-line voltage $V_{RB} = V_R - V_B$. $$V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$V_B = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ Using the identity: $\sin A - \sin B = 2 \cos\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$ $$V_{RB} = 2 V_m \cos\left(\dfrac{2\omega t - 240°}{2}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{240°}{2}\right)$$ $$V_{RB} = 2 V_m \cos(\omega t - 120°) \sin(120°)$$ Since $\sin(120°) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$: $$V_{RB} = 2 V_m \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos(\omega t - 120°) = \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 120°)$$ This is the line-to-line peak voltage: $$V_{RB} = V_{mL} \cos(\omega t - 120°)$$ By symmetry, each of the 6 humps has the form $V_{mL} \cos(\theta)$ where $\theta$ is measured from the center of the hump. ### Step 2: Express in Standard Form Shifting the time origin to the center of the first hump (where $V_o$ is maximum), the output over one hump (from $-30°$ to $+30°$ relative to the peak) is: $$V_o(\theta) = V_{mL} \cos(\theta), \quad -\dfrac{\pi}{6} \le \theta \le +\dfrac{\pi}{6}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Average Over One Hump $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} V_{mL} \cos(\theta) \, d\theta$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator: $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_{mL}}{\pi/3} \left[ \sin(\theta) \right]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6}$$ $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\pi/6) - \sin(-\pi/6) \right]$$ $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[ \dfrac{1}{2} - \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \right]$$ $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cdot 1$$ $$\boxed{V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \approx 1.654 V_m}$$ ### Step 4: Express in Terms of Line RMS Since $V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{L,RMS}$: $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2} V_{L,RMS}}{\pi} \approx 1.350 \, V_{L,RMS}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Output Voltage ($V_{rms}$) ### Step 1: Square the Output Voltage $$V_o^2(\theta) = V_{mL}^2 \cos^2(\theta)$$ ### Step 2: Integrate Over One Hump $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} V_{mL}^2 \cos^2(\theta) \, d\theta$$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}^2}{\pi} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} \cos^2(\theta) \, d\theta$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Power-Reduction Identity $$\cos^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}^2}{\pi} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} \dfrac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2} \, d\theta$$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}^2}{2\pi} \left[ \theta + \dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{2} \right]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6}$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate the Bounds At $\theta = +\pi/6$: $$\theta = \pi/6, \quad \sin(2\theta) = \sin(\pi/3) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ At $\theta = -\pi/6$: $$\theta = -\pi/6, \quad \sin(2\theta) = \sin(-\pi/3) = -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ Subtracting: $$\left[ \theta + \dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{2} \right]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) - \left(-\dfrac{\pi}{6} - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{3} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ ### Step 5: Final Expression for $V_{rms}^2$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}^2}{2\pi} \left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{V_{mL}^2}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}^2}{4\pi}$$ ### Step 6: Take the Square Root $$V_{rms} = V_{mL} \sqrt{ \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi} }$$ $$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 1.6554 \, V_m = 0.9551 \, V_{mL}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ripple Factor ($\gamma$) ### Step 1: Define Ripple Factor $$\gamma = \dfrac{V_{ac,rms}}{V_{dc}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2}}{V_{dc}} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1}$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Ratio $$\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} = \dfrac{0.9551 \, V_{mL}}{0.9549 \, V_{mL}} \approx 1.0009$$ (using $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} = 0.9549 V_{mL}$ and $V_{rms} = 0.9551 V_{mL}$) ### Step 3: Final Numerical Value $$\gamma = \sqrt{(1.0009)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.0018 - 1} = \sqrt{0.0018}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma \approx 0.04218 \text{ or } 4.218\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Significance:** The ripple factor of 0.042 (4.2%) is extremely low compared to single-phase full-wave (0.482) and three-phase half-wave (0.183) rectifiers. This is why 3-phase diode bridges are preferred for high-power DC applications. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rectification Efficiency ($\eta$) ### Step 1: Definition $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2 / R}{V_{rms}^2 / R} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2}$$ ### Step 2: Compute $$\eta = \dfrac{(0.9549 \, V_{mL})^2}{(0.9551 \, V_{mL})^2} = \dfrac{0.9118}{0.9122} \approx 0.9995$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 99.83\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This near-100% efficiency assumes an **ideal lossless rectifier** (no diode drop, no source impedance). With practical silicon diodes (drop ≈ 0.7 V each, and 2 diodes in series with the load), the efficiency drops to about 95–97% for low-voltage outputs. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) Consider diode D1 connected to phase R. When D1 is OFF, the most negative potential at the –DC terminal is set by whichever even-numbered diode is conducting. The maximum reverse voltage across D1 occurs when D1 is OFF and the most positive phase is Y or B, but the load is fed through D3 or D5. At that moment: - The +DC rail is at the peak of, say, $V_Y$, i.e., $V_m$. - The cathode of D1 is connected to phase R. If $V_R = -V_m/2$ (worst case, when R is at the bottom of its sinusoid), then the reverse voltage across D1 is $V_m - (-V_m/2) = 1.5 V_m$. But this analysis neglects line-to-line contributions. The rigorous result is: $$\boxed{V_{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \, V_m = \sqrt{6} \, V_{ph,RMS}}$$ > [!NOTE] > KTU students must remember: **PIV per diode in 3-phase bridge = peak line-to-line voltage**, not the peak phase voltage. This is a frequent exam trap. --- ## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A 3-phase diode bridge rectifier is supplied from a 400 V, 50 Hz, balanced 3-phase source through a transformer. Compute: (a) Average DC output voltage, (b) RMS output voltage, (c) Ripple factor, (d) PIV per diode, (e) Output ripple frequency. ### Given: $$V_{L,RMS} = 400 \text{ V}, \quad f = 50 \text{ Hz}$$ ### Step (a): Average DC Output Voltage $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{L,RMS} = \dfrac{3 \times 1.4142}{3.1416} \times 400$$ $$V_{dc} = 1.350 \times 400 = \boxed{540 \text{ V}}$$ ### Step (b): RMS Output Voltage $$V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 400 = 565.69 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{rms} = V_{mL} \sqrt{ \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi} } = 565.69 \times 0.9551$$ $$V_{rms} = \boxed{540.31 \text{ V}}$$ ### Step (c): Ripple Factor $$\gamma = \sqrt{ \left( \dfrac{540.31}{540} \right)^2 - 1 } = \sqrt{1.00115 - 1} = \sqrt{0.00115}$$ $$\gamma \approx \boxed{0.0339 \text{ (or 3.39\%)}}$$ ### Step (d): PIV per Diode $$V_{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 400 = \boxed{565.69 \text{ V}}$$ ### Step (e): Output Ripple Frequency $$f_o = 6f = 6 \times 50 = \boxed{300 \text{ Hz}}$$ # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Block Diagram — Three-Phase Diode Bridge ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AC ["3-PHASE AC SUPPLY (50 Hz)"] R["PHASE R

Diodes D2, D4, D6 (bottom group) have their **anodes joined at –DC rail** (common anode connection). # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Set B]** **With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a three-phase diode bridge rectifier with a resistive load. Draw the output voltage waveform.** **Model Answer:** A three-phase diode bridge rectifier uses **6 diodes** connected in a bridge configuration across the three supply lines (R, Y, B). The upper group (D1, D3, D5) has its **cathodes tied together** to form the **+DC rail**, and the lower group (D2, D4, D6) has its **anodes tied together** to form the **–DC rail**. **Working:** The diode connected to the **most positive phase** in the upper group conducts and feeds the +DC rail. Simultaneously, the diode connected to the **most negative phase** in the lower group conducts and feeds the –DC rail. Each diode pair conducts for **60°**, and each diode conducts for **120°** per cycle. The output voltage follows the **line-to-line voltage envelope**, producing **6 pulses per cycle**. **Output Waveform:** (6 humps, each spanning 60°, peak = $V_{mL}$, average = $\dfrac{3V_{mL}}{\pi}$) --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **Define PIV. What is the PIV of each diode in a 3-phase diode bridge rectifier supplying a highly inductive load? Justify your answer.** **Model Answer:** **PIV (Peak Inverse Voltage)** is the maximum reverse voltage that a diode must withstand without breaking down when it is in the OFF state. For a 3-phase diode bridge, when a diode in the upper group (say D1) is OFF, the +DC rail is held at the line-to-line peak voltage by the conducting diode pair. The cathode of D1 is at the +DC rail potential, and its anode is at its respective phase voltage. The worst-case reverse voltage is the **peak line-to-line voltage**: $$\boxed{V_{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{6} \, V_{ph,RMS} = \sqrt{2} \, V_{L,RMS}}$$ **Justification:** When D1 is OFF, the most positive phase is either Y or B, and the –DC rail is held at the most negative phase. The maximum possible difference between D1's cathode and anode is the line-to-line peak. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper, Modified]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **average DC output voltage** of a three-phase diode bridge rectifier supplied from a balanced 3-phase source with peak phase voltage $V_m$. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A 3-phase diode bridge rectifier is fed from a 415 V, 50 Hz supply through a transformer with turns ratio 2:1 (step-down). Calculate: - (i) Average DC output voltage - (ii) RMS output voltage - (iii) Ripple factor - (iv) Output ripple frequency - (v) PIV per diode **\[7 Marks\]** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — Derivation **Setup:** In a 3-phase bridge, the output voltage at any instant equals the line-to-line voltage between the most positive and most negative phases. Considering the first 60° interval (0 to 60°), with phases R and B conducting: $$V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t), \quad V_B = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ $$V_o = V_R - V_B = 2V_m \sin(120°)\cos(\omega t - 120°)$$ Since $2\sin(120°) = \sqrt{3}$: $$V_o = V_{mL}\cos(\omega t - 120°) = \sqrt{3}V_m \cos(\omega t - 120°)$$ **Average value over one hump** ($\pi/3$ radian span): $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} \sqrt{3}V_m \cos(\theta) \, d\theta = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}V_m}{\pi}\Big[\sin(\theta)\Big]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6}$$ $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}V_m}{\pi}\left[\dfrac{1}{2} - \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\right] = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}V_m}{\pi}$$ $$\boxed{V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_m \approx 1.654 V_m}$$ #### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical **Given:** $V_{L,RMS} = 415 \text{ V}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$, Turns ratio = 2:1 (step-down). **Secondary (rectifier side) line voltage:** $$V_{L,RMS,sec} = \dfrac{415}{2} = 207.5 \text{ V}$$ **(i) Average DC Output Voltage:** $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{L,RMS,sec} = \dfrac{3 \times 1.4142}{3.1416} \times 207.5$$ $$V_{dc} = 1.350 \times 207.5 = \boxed{280.13 \text{ V}}$$ **(ii) RMS Output Voltage:** $$V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \times 207.5 = 293.45 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{rms} = V_{mL}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi}} = 293.45 \times 0.9551$$ $$V_{rms} = \boxed{280.26 \text{ V}}$$ **(iii) Ripple Factor:** $$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{280.26}{280.13}\right)^2 - 1}$$ $$\gamma = \sqrt{1.00093 - 1} = \boxed{0.0305 \text{ or } 3.05\%}$$ **(iv) Output Ripple Frequency:** $$f_o = 6f = 6 \times 50 = \boxed{300 \text{ Hz}}$$ **(v) PIV per Diode:** $$V_{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \times 207.5 = \boxed{293.45 \text{ V}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the **conduction sequence of diodes** in a 3-phase diode bridge rectifier. Show that each diode conducts for **120°** and that two diodes conduct at any instant. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Compare the performance of **single-phase full-wave, three-phase half-wave, and three-phase full-wave (bridge) rectifiers** in terms of: - (i) Number of diodes - (ii) Transformer utilization - (iii) Ripple factor - (iv) Rectification efficiency - (v) Output frequency **\[7 Marks\]** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — Conduction Sequence The 6 diodes are divided into two groups: - **Upper group (D1, D3, D5):** Cathodes tied to +DC rail. - **Lower group (D2, D4, D6):** Anodes tied to –DC rail. **Conduction Sequence (one full 360° cycle):** | Interval (degrees) | Most +ve Phase | Most -ve Phase | Conducting Pair | Output $V_o$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0° – 60° | R | B | **D1, D6** | $V_{RB}$ | | 60° – 120° | R | Y | **D1, D2** | $V_{RY}$ | | 120° – 180° | Y | B | **D3, D2** | $V_{YB}$ | | 180° – 240° | Y | R | **D3, D4** | $V_{YR}$ | | 240° – 300° | B | R | **D5, D4** | $V_{BR}$ | | 300° – 360° | B | Y | **D5, D6** | $V_{BY}$ | **Each diode conducts for 120°:** A diode in the upper group (e.g., D1) conducts whenever phase R is the most positive. This happens for a continuous 120° window centered on the peak of $V_R$. Same logic applies to all six diodes. **Two diodes conduct at any instant:** At any instant, the highest-voltage phase is connected to +DC through exactly one upper-group diode, and the lowest-voltage phase is connected to –DC through exactly one lower-group diode. Hence, the current path goes: source → one diode → load → another diode → source. #### Model Solution for (b) — Comparison Table | Parameter | 1-Phase Full-Wave | 3-Phase Half-Wave | 3-Phase Full-Wave (Bridge) | |---|---|---|---| | (i) Number of Diodes | 4 | 3 | **6** | | (ii) TUF (Resistive Load) | 0.6928 | 0.8271 | **0.9552** | | (iii) Ripple Factor | 0.482 | 0.183 | **0.042** | | (iv) Rectification Efficiency | 81.06% | 96.77% | **99.83%** | | (v) Output Ripple Frequency | $2f$ | $3f$ | **$6f$** | | (vi) PIV per Diode | $V_m$ | $V_m$ | $\sqrt{3}V_m$ | > **Conclusion:** The 3-phase full-wave bridge rectifier offers the best performance in all metrics except PIV (which is higher by a factor of $\sqrt{3}$), and it does not require a neutral connection. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Phase Voltage with Line-to-Line Voltage** — The output is a **line-to-line** waveform, not a phase waveform. Using $V_m$ (phase peak) instead of $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3}V_m$ leads to a $\sqrt{3}$ error in $V_{dc}$. > > 2. **Wrong PIV Formula** — Writing $V_{PIV} = V_m$ instead of $V_{PIV} = \sqrt{3}V_m$ is the most common mistake. PIV in 3-phase bridge = **peak line-to-line voltage**, NOT peak phase voltage. > > 3. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{2}$ in RMS Conversions** — The formula $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi}V_m$ uses **peak** phase voltage. If the problem gives RMS line voltage, you must convert: $V_m = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} V_{L,RMS}}{\sqrt{3}}$. > > 4. **Wrong Output Frequency** — Output frequency is $6f$, not $3f$ (which would be 3-phase half-wave) and not $f$ (which would be uncontrolled 3-phase with neutral issues). > > 5. **Wrong Diode Conduction Angle** — Each diode conducts for **120°** in a bridge (not 60°). Each pair conducts for 60° (this distinction is often confused). > > 6. **Numerical Calculation Errors** — Using $\pi = 3.14$ vs $\pi = 3.1416$ changes the answer in the 2nd decimal. Use $\pi = 3.1416$ always. > > 7. **Not Drawing the Output Waveform Properly** — Examiners expect **6 humps** of equal height ($V_{mL}$), each spanning **60°**, with the ripple variation clearly visible. > > 8. **Missing the "Highest & Lowest" Rule** — Always state this rule explicitly in your answer. It is the conceptual foundation expected by KTU examiners. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation):** > > - **Number of Diodes:** 6, arranged in two groups of 3 (upper: D1, D3, D5; lower: D2, D4, D6) > - **Conduction Rule:** Highest phase → +DC via upper group; Lowest phase → –DC via lower group > - **Conduction Sequence:** D1-D6 → D1-D2 → D3-D2 → D3-D4 → D5-D4 → D5-D6 → (repeat) > - **Pair Conduction Angle:** 60° (each pair) > - **Individual Diode Conduction Angle:** 120° (each diode) > - **Number of Diodes Conducting Simultaneously:** Always 2 > - **Output Pulses per Cycle:** 6 (hence "6-pulse rectifier") > - **Output Frequency:** $6f$ (300 Hz for 50 Hz supply, 360 Hz for 60 Hz supply) > - **Average DC Output Voltage:** $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_m = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{L,RMS} \approx 1.35 V_{L,RMS}$ > - **RMS Output Voltage:** $V_{rms} \approx 1.6554 V_m \approx 0.9551 V_{mL}$ > - **Ripple Factor:** 0.042 (4.2%) — extremely low, no filter needed for many applications > - **Rectification Efficiency:** 99.83% (ideal) > - **PIV per Diode:** $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} V_m = \sqrt{2} V_{L,RMS}$ > - **TUF:** 0.9552 (best among uncontrolled rectifiers) > - **Form Factor:** ~1.0009 (very close to unity) > - **Need for Transformer:** Optional — can be connected directly to 3-phase mains (unlike 3-phase half-wave which needs a neutral) > - **Output is "Line-to-Line" voltage waveform, NOT phase voltage waveform** — KEY INSIGHT > - **Applications:** HVDC, DC drives, VFD input stage, battery chargers, electroplating, UPS > - **Comparison Edge:** 3-phase bridge > 3-phase half-wave > 1-phase full-wave > 1-phase half-wave in nearly every metric

topics.title: Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers with R and RL Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Single Phase Controlled Rectifiers with R and RL Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

A **Single-Phase Controlled Rectifier** is a power electronic converter that converts a single-phase AC supply voltage into a **variable DC output voltage** by phase-controlling the conduction of **thyristors (SCRs)**. The average DC output voltage is regulated by varying the **firing angle (α)** — the delay angle measured from the natural commutation instant of the diode equivalent.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCEIL607), this experiment specifically investigates the behavior of:
- **Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier** (1-pulse)
- **Single-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier** — Semi-converter (2-pulse, half-controlled) and Full Converter (2-pulse, fully-controlled)
- Operating under **Resistive (R) load** and **Resistive-Inductive (R-L) load** conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The lab emphasizes *hardware observation* of firing pulse generation, load voltage, load current waveforms, and the **transition from discontinuous to continuous conduction** as load inductance increases.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap That You Can Rotate"

Imagine a **water tap (faucet)** connected to a pulsating water pipe (the AC mains).

- The **AC mains** = water flowing back-and-forth (sinusoidal supply $v_s = V_m \sin \omega t$).
- The **SCR (thyristor)** = a tap that **only opens** when you tell it to, and **stays open as long as water keeps flowing forward**. It cannot be closed by the gate; it only closes when the current naturally drops to zero.
- **Firing angle (α)** = the moment (after a zero-crossing) at which you decide to open the tap.
  - Open it at the very start of the positive half-cycle → α = 0° (maximum flow, just like an uncontrolled diode).
  - Open it later → α > 0° (less water makes it to the bucket — the average DC drops).
- The **R load** = a simple bucket (current is in phase with voltage, stops the moment the tap closes).
- The **R-L load** = a bucket with a **swirling flywheel attached to it**. Once the flywheel starts spinning, it has inertia (stored magnetic energy $\frac{1}{2}Li^2$) and **forces current to keep flowing even after the tap has closed and the supply has reversed**. This is why current may extend into the negative half-cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term Glossary for KTU Lab Viva:**
> - **Firing angle (α):** The delay from the natural commutation point (zero crossing of supply) to the instant the SCR is gated. Measured in **electrical degrees (°)**.
> - **Conduction angle (γ):** The total angle during which the SCR conducts.
> - **Extinction angle (β):** The instant the SCR naturally turns off.
> - **Overlap angle (μ):** Used in full converters with source inductance $L_s$ (not considered in this elementary experiment).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Supply RMS Voltage | $V_s$ | **230 V, 50 Hz** (India standard) |
| Supply Peak Voltage | $V_m$ | $\sqrt{2} \times V_s$ ≈ **325.27 V** |
| Supply Frequency | $f$ | **50 Hz** |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | $2\pi f$ = **314.159 rad/s** |
| Load Resistance | $R$ | typically 10–100 Ω in lab |
| Load Inductance | $L$ | 10 mH – 100 mH (selectable) |
| Firing Pulse Frequency | $f_g$ | **2 × f = 100 Hz** (for full converter) |
| Firing Pulse Width | $t_g$ | 50–100 µs (sufficient to latch SCR) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Topological Variants Studied in the Lab

The KTU experiment broadly covers **three topologies**, all driven from a single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz supply:

| Topology | Number of SCRs | Pulses/cycle | Quadrants of operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Half-Wave (1-pulse)** | 1 SCR | 1 | 1st quadrant only (R, RL loads) |
| **Full-Wave Semi-converter** | 2 SCRs + 2 Diodes | 2 | 1st quadrant only |
| **Full-Wave Full-Converter** | 4 SCRs (bridge) | 2 | 1st & 4th quadrants (regenerative) |

### 2.2 Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step conduction logic (R load):**
1. At $\omega t = 0$, the supply $v_s = V_m \sin \omega t$ becomes positive. The SCR is forward-biased but **not yet conducting** (no gate pulse).
2. At $\omega t = \alpha$ (firing angle), gate pulse is applied. The SCR turns **ON** (latches). 
   - Load voltage $v_o = v_s = V_m \sin \omega t$
   - Load current $i_o = v_o / R$
3. At $\omega t = \pi$ (180°), the supply reverses. Current attempts to become negative, but the SCR is a **unidirectional device** and **turns OFF** because $i_o$ tries to fall below zero.
4. The SCR remains OFF from $\pi$ to $(2\pi + \alpha)$. No voltage appears across the load.
5. At $(2\pi + \alpha)$, the next gate pulse arrives, and the cycle repeats.

**Step-by-step conduction logic (R-L load):**
1. At $\omega t = \alpha$, the SCR is fired. The circuit now has $v_s = i_o R + L \frac{di_o}{dt}$.
2. The load current **does not stop** at $\pi$ because the inductor maintains the current flow. The SCR continues to conduct even into the **negative half-cycle** of the supply.
3. During this negative half-cycle, $v_o$ becomes **negative**, but $i_o$ is still **positive** (the SCR is still forward-biased due to the inductive EMF maintaining current).
4. The SCR finally turns OFF at $\omega t = \beta$ (the **extinction angle**), where the load current naturally decays to zero.
5. If $\beta < (\pi + \alpha)$, conduction is **discontinuous**. If $\beta = (\pi + \alpha)$ exactly, it is the **boundary of continuous conduction**. For very large $L$, $\beta$ can approach infinity, leading to nearly continuous current (used to derive full-converter continuous-conduction equations).

### 2.3 Why Do We Need a Freewheeling Diode (FWD)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **full converter with R-L load**, the load voltage goes negative during a portion of every cycle, which causes **negative load voltage and reduced average output**. Adding a **Freewheeling Diode (FWD)** across the load:
> - Clamps the load voltage to **zero (not negative)** whenever the supply tries to drive it negative.
> - Provides a path for the inductive load current to circulate after the SCRs turn off.
> - Prevents the load voltage waveform from going negative — **improving the average output voltage and power factor**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Topology & Load Condition | Average Output Voltage $V_{o,avg}$ | RMS Output Voltage $V_{o,rms}$ | RMS / Avg SCR Current | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Half-Wave, R load** | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{2} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{4}\right)}$ | $I_{SCR} = I_{rms}$ | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ |
| 2 | **Half-Wave, R-L load** (continuous, $L \to \infty$) | $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$ — but $\beta \to \pi+\alpha$, so simplifies to $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | — | $I_{SCR} = I_{o,avg}$ (rectangular current) | $\gamma = \pi$ |
| 3 | **Full Semi-converter, R load** | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $V_s\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}\right)}$ | $I_{SCR} = I_o$ (same as load) | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ |
| 4 | **Full Semi-converter, R-L load with FWD** | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | $V_s\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | $I_{SCR} = I_o \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi}}$ | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ |
| 5 | **Full Converter, R load** | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $V_s\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | $I_{SCR} = I_o$ | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ |
| 6 | **Full Converter, R-L load (continuous)** | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\dfrac{2V_s}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2}\,V_s$ | $I_{SCR} = I_o$ | $\gamma = \pi$ |
| 7 | **Full Converter, R-L load with FWD** | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $V_s\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | $I_{SCR} = I_o \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi}}$ | $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The expression $\vert \cos\alpha \vert$ is replaced by the unambiguous $\vert\cos\alpha\vert$ (or written as $\lvert\cos\alpha\rvert$ for maximum LaTeX safety) to indicate that for full-converter operation, $\alpha$ is conventionally restricted to $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$ and the output becomes negative beyond $\alpha = \pi/2$ — this is the **inversion mode** used for DC motor regenerative braking.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Motor Speed Control (Armature Voltage Control):** The most common industrial application. A single-phase full converter feeds the armature of a separately-excited DC motor, and the speed is varied by changing $\alpha$.
- **Battery Chargers:** Controlled rectifiers regulate the charging voltage precisely.
- **DC Welding Power Supplies:** Semi-converters are used to vary the welding current.
- **HVDC (High Voltage DC) Transmission — Small Scale:** Although commercial HVDC uses 3-phase, the **single-phase full converter is a foundational building block** for understanding 3-phase 6-pulse and 12-pulse HVDC systems.
- **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS):** Controlled rectifiers form the front-end AC-DC converter stage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below assume an **ideal single-phase supply** $v_s(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, **ideal SCRs** (zero forward drop, zero leakage), and a **resistive or R-L load** connected at the converter output terminals.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Output Voltage of Single-Phase Half-Wave Rectifier with R Load

**Given:** Supply voltage $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, firing angle $\alpha$, purely resistive load $R$.

**Step 1 — Establish the load voltage expression over one complete cycle.**

The SCR conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$. Outside this interval, the SCR is OFF and $v_o = 0$.

$$
v_o(\omega t) = 
\begin{cases}
V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le \pi \\
0, & \pi \le \omega t \le (2\pi + \alpha)
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the formal definition of the average (DC) value of a periodic waveform.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the piecewise expression. Since $v_o = 0$ in the OFF interval, the integral reduces to a single limit.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4 — Pull out the constant $V_m / (2\pi)$.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral of $\sin(\omega t)$, which is $-\cos(\omega t)$.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the upper and lower limits.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ (-\cos\pi) - (-\cos\alpha) \Big]
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the trigonometric values. We know $\cos\pi = -1$ and $-\cos\pi = +1$.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ 1 + \cos\alpha \Big]
$$

**Step 8 — Final boxed result.**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\,(1 + \cos\alpha)\,}
$$

> **Sanity check:** When $\alpha = 0$, $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ — which is exactly the average output of an uncontrolled **half-wave diode rectifier** (the SCR behaves as a diode with zero firing delay). ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Output Voltage of Single-Phase Full Converter (Full-Bridge) with Highly Inductive R-L Load

**Given:** $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, four-SCR bridge, load current $I_o$ is **constant and ripple-free** (continuous conduction with $L \to \infty$), firing angle $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Recall the load voltage waveform.**

In a full converter, two SCRs conduct at a time. The positive pair ($T_1, T_2$) is fired at $\alpha$, the negative pair ($T_3, T_4$) is fired at $(\pi + \alpha)$. The load voltage is the **full supply** (not the half) during conduction intervals.

$$
v_o(\omega t) = 
\begin{cases}
+V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \le \omega t \le (\pi + \alpha) \\
-V_m \sin(\omega t), & (\pi + \alpha) \le \omega t \le (2\pi + \alpha)
\end{cases}
$$

Because the second interval is just the **mirror image** of the first, the average over a full period is **twice** the average over the first half.

**Step 2 — Write the formal average integral over the full period.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

(Note: the period for a full converter is $\pi$ because the waveform repeats every half-cycle. The denominator is therefore $\pi$, not $2\pi$.)

**Step 3 — Pull out the constant.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply limits.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\pi + \alpha) + \cos\alpha \Big]
$$

**Step 6 — Use the trigonometric identity $\cos(\pi + \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$.**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -(-\cos\alpha) + \cos\alpha \Big] = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ 2\cos\alpha \Big]
$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed result.**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha\,}
$$

> **Sanity check 1:** When $\alpha = 0$, $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ — which matches the average output of a **single-phase full-wave uncontrolled diode bridge** (two half-wave rectifiers in series). ✔
>
> **Sanity check 2:** When $\alpha = \pi/2$ (90°), $V_{o,avg} = 0$. The waveform has equal positive and negative areas — the converter is at the **inversion boundary**. ✔

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — RMS Output Voltage of Single-Phase Full Converter with R Load

**Step 1 — RMS is defined as the square root of the mean of the square.**

$$
V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \big[ V_m \sin(\omega t) \big]^2 \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 2 — Square the sine term.**

$$
V_{o,rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the half-angle identity $\sin^2 x = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2x}{2}$.**

$$
V_{o,rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \frac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2} \, d(\omega t)}
$$

**Step 4 — Split the integral.**

$$
V_{o,rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} 1 \, d(\omega t) - \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \cos 2\omega t \, d(\omega t) \right]}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the first integral (length of the interval).**

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} 1 \, d(\omega t) = \pi
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the second integral.**

$$
\int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \cos(2\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \left[ \frac{\sin 2\omega t}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} = \frac{1}{2}\big[ \sin 2(\pi + \alpha) - \sin 2\alpha \big]
$$

Using $\sin(2\pi + 2\alpha) = \sin 2\alpha$:

$$
= \frac{1}{2}\big[ \sin 2\alpha - \sin 2\alpha \big] = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back.**

$$
V_{o,rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \big[ \pi - 0 \big]} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = V_s
$$

**Step 8 — Final boxed result.**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right) }\,} \quad \text{(for R load)}
$$

> **Sanity check:** When $\alpha = 0$, $V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}(\pi + 0)} = V_s \sqrt{1} = V_s$, which is the RMS of a pure sine wave. ✔

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical Lab Question Style)

> **Problem (typical 14-mark KTU ESE):**
> A single-phase full converter operates from a 230 V, 50 Hz supply and feeds a highly inductive load such that the load current can be assumed constant at 15 A. Calculate the average output voltage, average and RMS SCR currents, and the active power delivered to the load for $\alpha = 45°$. Also, find the firing pulse delay time $t_\alpha$ in milliseconds.

**Given:**
- $V_s = 230$ V $\Rightarrow V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27$ V
- $I_o = 15$ A (constant)
- $\alpha = 45° = \pi/4$ rad
- $f = 50$ Hz $\Rightarrow T = 20$ ms

**Step 1 — Average output voltage (highly inductive load).**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \cos 45°
$$

$$
V_{o,avg} = 207.07 \times 0.7071 = 146.4 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Power delivered to the load.**

$$
P_o = V_{o,avg} \times I_o = 146.4 \times 15 = 2196 \text{ W} \approx 2.196 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Average SCR current.** Each SCR conducts for half the cycle ($\pi$ radians), so $I_{SCR,avg} = I_o / 2 = 7.5$ A.

**Step 4 — RMS SCR current.** Each SCR carries a constant $I_o$ for half the cycle, so $I_{SCR,rms} = I_o / \sqrt{2} = 15 / \sqrt{2} = 10.607$ A.

**Step 5 — Firing pulse delay time.**

$$
t_\alpha = \frac{\alpha}{360°} \times T = \frac{45°}{360°} \times 20 \text{ ms} = 2.5 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** $V_{o,avg} = 146.4$ V, $P_o = 2.196$ kW, $I_{SCR,avg} = 7.5$ A, $I_{SCR,rms} = 10.61$ A, $t_\alpha = 2.5$ ms.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are **functional/architectural block diagrams** rendered in Mermaid. They map the **signal flow, control structure, and conduction sequencing** of the lab setup — they are the schematic-of-intent that students must draw in their lab record and viva.

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Complete Lab Test Bench

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AC_MAINS[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> ISO[Isolator MCB and Fuse]
    ISO --> VREG[Variac 0 to 270V]
    VREG --> RECT[Converter Power Module SCR Bridge]
    CTRL[Controller UJT R or TCA785 or DSP] -->|Gate Pulses| RECT
    LOAD[Load Bank R or RL with FWD] -->|Sensed Voltage and Current| RECT
    RECT --> OUT[DC Output Terminals]
    OUT --> OSC[Digital Storage Oscilloscope 2 Channel]
    OUT --> METER[DC Voltmeter and Ammeter]
    OUT --> PROBE[Current Probe and Differential Voltage Probe]
    CTRL --> PULSE_MON[Pulse Monitor LED on Trainer]
```

**Reading this diagram:**
- The **Variac** (variable autotransformer) lets the student adjust the AC input to a safe level (e.g., start at 50 V) before increasing.
- The **Controller block** generates the **gating pulses** at the chosen firing angle. The student varies $\alpha$ via a potentiometer on the trainer and observes the resulting waveform change.
- **DSO** captures the load voltage and load current waveforms for the lab record.

---

### 4.2 Internal Topology — Single-Phase Full Converter (4-SCR Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Supply Terminal A] --> SCR1
    SCR1 --> D1[DC Positive Bus]
    A --> SCR3
    SCR3 --> D2[DC Negative Bus]
    B[AC Supply Terminal B] --> SCR2
    SCR2 --> D1
    B --> SCR4
    SCR4 --> D2
    D1 --> LOADPOS[Load Terminal Positive]
    D2 --> LOADNEG[Load Terminal Negative]
    SCR1 -.->|Gate Pulse alpha| GP1[Gating Circuit Pair 1]
    SCR2 -.->|Gate Pulse alpha| GP1
    SCR3 -.->|Gate Pulse pi plus alpha| GP2[Gating Circuit Pair 2]
    SCR4 -.->|Gate Pulse pi plus alpha| GP2
    LOADPOS --> FWD[Freewheeling Diode Optional]
    FWD --> LOADNEG
```

**Conduction sequencing** (for full converter with R-L continuous load):
- **Pair 1 (SCR1, SCR2):** Conducts during $\alpha$ to $(\pi + \alpha)$. Positive output.
- **Pair 2 (SCR3, SCR4):** Conducts during $(\pi + \alpha)$ to $(2\pi + \alpha)$. Negative output (but current is still positive due to inductance).

---

### 4.3 Waveform Sequencing — Conduction Map for Full Converter with R-L Load

```mermaid
gantt
    title Conduction Sequence (one full period of 20 ms)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section SCR Pair 1 alpha to pi plus alpha
    SCR1 and SCR2 conducting :a1, 0, 50
    section SCR Pair 2 pi plus alpha to 2pi plus alpha
    SCR3 and SCR4 conducting :a2, 50, 100
    section Freewheel if FWD present
    FWD conducts when load voltage tries to go negative :crit, a2, 100
```

---

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Converter to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Application Requirement] --> Q1{Is regeneration required?}
    Q1 -->|No - motoring only| SEMI[Use Semi-Converter]
    Q1 -->|Yes - need 4 quadrant operation| FULL[Use Full Converter]
    SEMI --> Q2{Load has inductance?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| SEMIFWD[Add Freewheeling Diode]
    Q2 -->|No| SEMIPURE[Pure R load - no FWD needed]
    FULL --> Q3{Inductance is high enough for continuous current?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| FULLCC[Continuous conduction - V avg is 2Vm cos alpha by pi]
    Q3 -->|No| FULLDC[Discontinuous conduction - use general V avg formula]
```

---

### 4.5 Lab Setup Photograph Map (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph B1[Power Section Left Rack]
        MCB1[MCB 16A] --> VAR1[Variac 0 to 270V 5A]
    end
    subgraph B2[Converter Section Center Rack]
        VAR1 --> BRIDGE1[SCR Bridge Module with Heat Sink]
        BRIDGE1 --> SENSE1[Voltage and Current Sensors]
        SENSE1 --> LOAD1[Load Bank with R and RL Switchable]
    end
    subgraph B3[Control Section Right Rack]
        OSC1[Controller UJT or Digital TCA785]
        OSC1 -->|Fiber optic or pulse transformer isolation| BRIDGE1
    end
    subgraph B4[Measurement Bench]
        DSO1[DSO 2 Channel 100 MHz]
        VM1[DC Voltmeter 0 to 300V]
        AM1[DC Ammeter 0 to 5A]
    end
    LOAD1 --> DSO1
    LOAD1 --> VM1
    LOAD1 --> AM1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B, Q1(a)]**
>
> **Q1. Define firing angle and conduction angle for a single-phase controlled rectifier. Why does the conduction angle of an SCR differ for R load vs R-L load?**

**Model Answer:**

The **firing angle (α)** is the delay angle (in electrical degrees) measured from the instant the supply voltage crosses zero (natural commutation point) to the instant the SCR is triggered by its gate pulse. For a 50 Hz supply, each electrical degree corresponds to $20\,\text{ms} / 360° \approx 55.56\,\mu s$.

The **conduction angle (γ)** is the total electrical angle during which the SCR actually carries current in one full cycle.

For an **R load**, the SCR can only conduct while the supply is positive. Once the supply reverses at $\pi$, the current becomes zero and the SCR turns off. Therefore $\gamma = \pi - \alpha$.

For an **R-L load**, the inductor stores energy $\frac{1}{2}Li^2$ and forces the current to continue even after the supply reverses. The SCR therefore conducts **beyond $\pi$** into the negative half-cycle, so $\gamma > \pi - \alpha$. In the limiting case of a very large $L$ (continuous conduction), $\gamma = \pi$ for a half-wave rectifier. *(3 marks — definition 1, R case 1, R-L case 1.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A, Q1(b)]**
>
> **Q2. What is the function of a freewheeling diode (FWD) in a controlled rectifier feeding an R-L load? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer:**

The **freewheeling diode (FWD)** is connected in **anti-parallel** with the R-L load (cathode at the positive DC bus, anode at the negative DC bus). Its function is to provide a **short-circuited path** for the inductive load current to circulate **after the SCRs turn off**, eliminating the negative excursion of the load voltage.

If the FWD is **omitted** in a full converter with R-L load, the load voltage waveform contains **negative portions** (during which the inductor's stored energy is returned to the AC source), which **reduces the average output voltage** and also causes **load voltage reversal**, which is undesirable for DC motor drives. The average output formula changes from $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ to a more complex form involving the extinction angle $\beta$. *(3 marks — FWD role 1, negative voltage consequence 1, formula impact 1.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module-2 Internal Choice, Q7]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat circuit diagram and waveform sketches, explain the operation of a **single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier with an R-L load**. Derive the expression for the **average output voltage** in the **continuous conduction** mode.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A single-phase half-wave controlled rectifier is supplied from $V_s = 230$ V, 50 Hz. It feeds a load of $R = 10$ Ω, $L = 50$ mH. The firing angle is $\alpha = 30°$. Calculate:
>   (i) Average output voltage
>   (ii) RMS output voltage
>   (iii) Average and RMS load current
>   (iv) True power delivered to the load

**Model Solution to (a) — Operation with R-L Load:**

1. The circuit consists of one SCR in series with the R-L load across the AC supply. 
2. At $\omega t = \alpha$, the gate pulse is applied. The SCR begins to conduct. The KVL gives:
$$
v_s = v_o \Rightarrow V_m \sin(\omega t) = i_o R + L \frac{di_o}{dt}
$$
3. The current $i_o$ rises from its initial value (which may be non-zero due to stored inductor energy from the previous cycle) and follows the forced sinusoidal response plus a decaying exponential transient.
4. In **continuous conduction** (large $L$), the inductor is so large that $i_o$ never falls to zero. The SCR continues to conduct beyond $\pi$, and the load voltage $v_o$ becomes **negative** during the interval $\pi < \omega t < (\pi + \alpha)$.
5. The extinction angle $\beta$ approaches $(\pi + \alpha)$ in the limit of infinite inductance.
6. Average output voltage derivation:

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{2\pi}\big[-\cos(\pi + \alpha) + \cos\alpha\big]
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha\,}
$$

*(Award 1 mark each for: circuit diagram 2, waveform sketch 2, KVL equation 1, integral setup 1, final derivation 1.)*

**Model Solution to (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V $\Rightarrow V_m = 325.27$ V, $\alpha = 30°$, $R = 10$ Ω, $L = 50$ mH, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step (i) — Average output voltage** (using half-wave continuous conduction formula):

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\cos 30° = \frac{325.27}{\pi} \times 0.8660 = 89.66 \text{ V}
$$

**Step (ii) — RMS output voltage** (assuming $\beta \approx \pi + \alpha$):

$$
V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{325.27}{\sqrt{2}} = 230 \text{ V}
$$

(RMS equals supply RMS in continuous conduction for half-wave rectifier — the entire half-wave appears across the load, with the other half-cycle also appearing in the negative half.)

**Step (iii) — Average and RMS load current:**

$$
I_{o,avg} = \frac{V_{o,avg}}{R} = \frac{89.66}{10} = 8.966 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{o,rms} = \frac{V_{o,rms}}{R} = \frac{230}{10} = 23.0 \text{ A}
$$

**Step (iv) — True power delivered:**

$$
P_o = I_{o,rms}^2 \times R = (23.0)^2 \times 10 = 5290 \text{ W} = 5.29 \text{ kW}
$$

*(1 mark for correct substitution, 1 for correct formula, 1 for correct final value × 4 sub-parts.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain with circuit and waveforms the operation of a **single-phase full converter (full-bridge) feeding a highly inductive R-L load**. Derive the **average and RMS output voltage** expressions.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] A single-phase full converter is fed from $V_s = 230$ V, 50 Hz. The firing angle is $\alpha = 60°$. The load is highly inductive such that load current is constant at $I_o = 10$ A. Calculate: (i) Average and RMS output voltage, (ii) Average and RMS SCR current, (iii) Active and reactive power input from AC source, (iv) Firing pulse delay in ms.

**Model Solution to (a):**

The full converter uses **four SCRs** ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$) in a bridge. Pair 1 ($T_1, T_2$) is fired at $\alpha$; Pair 2 ($T_3, T_4$) is fired at $(\pi + \alpha)$.

- During $\alpha \le \omega t \le (\pi + \alpha)$: $T_1, T_2$ conduct. Load voltage $v_o = +V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
- During $(\pi + \alpha) \le \omega t \le (2\pi + \alpha)$: $T_3, T_4$ conduct. Load voltage $v_o = -V_m \sin(\omega t)$ (i.e., the supply is reversed across the load).
- With highly inductive load, load current is **constant and positive** throughout.

**Average output voltage** (already derived in §3.2):

$$
\boxed{\,V_{o,avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha\,}
$$

**RMS output voltage:** Since each half-cycle contains a full sine wave of either polarity, the RMS equals the supply RMS:

$$
V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = V_s
$$

*(Award marks for circuit 2, waveforms 2, average derivation 2, RMS derivation 1.)*

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $V_s = 230$ V, $V_m = 325.27$ V, $\alpha = 60°$, $I_o = 10$ A, $f = 50$ Hz.

**(i) Average and RMS output voltage:**

$$
V_{o,avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos 60° = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \times 0.5 = 103.53 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{o,rms} = V_s = 230 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Average and RMS SCR current:** Each SCR conducts for half the period.

$$
I_{SCR,avg} = \frac{I_o}{2} = 5.0 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_{SCR,rms} = \frac{I_o}{\sqrt{2}} = 7.071 \text{ A}
$$

**(iii) Active and reactive power:**

Active power:

$$
P = V_{o,avg} \times I_o = 103.53 \times 10 = 1035.3 \text{ W} \approx 1.035 \text{ kW}
$$

Reactive power (using the standard full-converter relation):

$$
Q = \frac{2 V_m I_o}{\pi} \sin\alpha = \frac{2 \times 325.27 \times 10}{\pi} \times \sin 60° = 1792.3 \text{ VAR} \approx 1.792 \text{ kVAR}
$$

**(iv) Firing pulse delay:**

$$
t_\alpha = \frac{\alpha}{360°} \times T = \frac{60°}{360°} \times 20 \text{ ms} = 3.333 \text{ ms}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in PCEIL607 Lab Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_{o,avg}$ formula for R vs R-L load** — A student who writes $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ for a half-wave rectifier with R load will lose 3–4 marks. The half-wave R-load formula is $\dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to use $\sqrt{2}$ for $V_m$** — Supply is given as $V_s = 230$ V (RMS). Always convert to $V_m = 325.27$ V before substituting.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the limits of integration** — For the full converter, the period is $\pi$ (not $2\pi$) because the waveform repeats every half-cycle. Students using $2\pi$ in the denominator will get a wrong answer that is exactly half of the correct value.
>
> 4. **Saying "FWD increases voltage" without explaining why** — The FWD does **not generate** voltage. It simply **prevents the load voltage from going negative** by short-circuiting the load through itself, which means $v_o = 0$ during the freewheel interval (instead of being negative).
>
> 5. **Lab record mistakes:** Students often forget to mention the **snubber circuit** across each SCR in the circuit diagram. A snubber (RC network) is mandatory in the practical circuit to limit $\dfrac{dv}{dt}$ and prevent false triggering.
>
> 6. **Incorrect firing pulse train:** A common lab record error is drawing the gating pulse train for a full converter as having a single pulse per cycle. It must be **two pulses per cycle (one at $\alpha$, one at $\pi + \alpha$)**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU ESE & Lab Viva):**

- ⚡ **Half-Wave, R Load:** $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$. Conduction stops at $\pi$.
- ⚡ **Half-Wave, R-L Continuous Load:** $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$. Conduction continues to $\pi + \alpha$.
- ⚡ **Full Semi-Converter:** $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ — always non-negative, used for **1-quadrant** drives.
- ⚡ **Full Converter, R or R-L Continuous Load:** $V_{o,avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ — used for **2-quadrant** drives. Becomes **negative** when $\alpha > 90°$ (inversion mode).
- ⚡ **RMS Output Voltage (Full Converter, R Load):** $V_{o,rms} = V_s\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\big(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\big)}$
- ⚡ **RMS Output Voltage (Full Converter, Continuous R-L):** $V_{o,rms} = V_s$ (full sine wave across load in both halves).
- ⚡ **Firing Pulse Frequency** = $2f$ (100 Hz) for single-phase full-wave controlled rectifiers.
- ⚡ **Pulse Train Symmetry:** Pulses appear at $\alpha$ and $(\pi + \alpha)$ in every cycle.
- ⚡ **Freewheeling Diode** is **optional but recommended** for R-L loads; it **prevents negative load voltage** and **improves the input power factor** marginally.
- ⚡ **Conversion from degrees to time:** $t_\alpha = \dfrac{\alpha}{360°} \times \dfrac{1}{f}$ seconds.
- ⚡ **Lab Viva Favorites:** (a) Why is a **snubber** needed? (b) What is the **effect of increasing $L$** on current waveform? (c) Why is **isolation** between the firing circuit and power circuit required? (d) What happens if the gate pulse is **too narrow**? (Answer: SCR may fail to latch, no conduction.)
- ⚡ **Device used for firing:** UJT relaxation oscillator, or digital IC like **TCA785**, or **DSP/microcontroller-based** in modern setups.
- ⚡ **Key Safety Rule:** Always start with the variac at **zero output** and the firing angle near **180°** (no conduction) before energizing, then gradually reduce $\alpha$ to avoid surge currents.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Realization of Basic Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost Converters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Realization of Basic Buck, Boost, and Buck-Boost Converters # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What are DC-DC Converters? A **DC-DC Converter** (also known as a **Chopper**) is a static power electronic device that converts one level of DC voltage into another level of DC voltage. It operates by switching elements (typically MOSFETs, IGBTs, or BJTs) on and off at a high frequency and using energy storage elements (inductors and capacitors) to shape the output. In the context of this lab experiment, we focus on the three **non-isolated fundamental converter topologies**: 1. **Buck Converter** (Step-Down) 2. **Boost Converter** (Step-Up) 3. **Buck-Boost Converter** (Step-Down/Step-Up) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):** > *"A chopper is a high-speed ON/OFF semiconductor switch that connects a source to a load. When switched ON, it supplies power to the load; when OFF, it disconnects the source. By varying the duty cycle (D = T_on / T), the average output voltage is controlled."* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Water-Tap Analogy (Conceptual Intuition):** > Imagine a water tap connected to a high-pressure water tank. If you open the tap fully (D ≈ 1), the bucket fills fast — **this is like a Boost Converter pushing energy hard into the load**. If you open the tap just a trickle (D ≈ 0.1), the bucket fills slowly — **this is like a Buck Converter regulating down**. If you reverse the plumbing so the water flows upward into an elevated tank, you need a pump that pulses on and off — **this is the Buck-Boost topology**. - **Buck Converter** → Used in **Laptop chargers, LED drivers, USB power supplies** (e.g., stepping 12V down to 5V, 3.3V). - **Boost Converter** → Used in **Camera flash circuits, solar PV MPPT, hybrid car DC-DC stages** (e.g., stepping 1.5V battery up to 5V). - **Buck-Boost Converter** → Used in **Battery-powered devices where input may rise above or fall below the desired output** (e.g., 3V–5V input from a draining battery to a constant 3.3V output). ## 1.3 Key Terminology for the Experiment | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Duty Cycle (D)** | Ratio of switch ON-time to total switching period, $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | | **Switching Frequency (f_s)** | Number of switching cycles per second, typically **20 kHz – 200 kHz** | | **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** | Inductor current never falls to zero during the switching cycle | | **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)** | Inductor current becomes zero for a portion of the cycle | | **Critical Inductance (L_c)** | Minimum inductance required to maintain CCM | > [!IMPORTANT] > For all KTU 2024 lab exams, students must **realize the hardware circuit** (or simulate it) and **measure the output voltage and waveforms** for a fixed duty cycle. Always quote duty cycle as a **dimensionless ratio (0 < D < 1)** and switching frequency in **kHz**. # ⚡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principles (CCM Analysis) The analysis of all three converters in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) follows the same logical framework. The converter operation is divided into two intervals per switching period: - **Interval 1 (T_on):** The controlled switch (MOSFET) is ON. The diode is reverse-biased. Energy is stored in the inductor. - **Interval 2 (T_off):** The switch is OFF. The diode becomes forward-biased (freewheeling). The stored energy in the inductor is delivered to the load. ### 🔵 The Buck Converter (Step-Down) When the MOSFET is ON, the input voltage is applied across the inductor, and the inductor current ramps up linearly. $$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_s - V_o}{L}$$ When the MOSFET is OFF, the inductor current freewheels through the diode, and the current ramps down. $$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{-V_o}{L}$$ **Why and How:** Applying the **Volt-Second Balance Principle** (the average voltage across the inductor over one period must be zero for steady-state operation), we get the relationship shown in the formula sheet below. ### 🔴 The Boost Converter (Step-Up) When the MOSFET is ON, the inductor is connected directly across the source, storing energy. The diode is reverse-biased, and the load is supplied by the capacitor. $$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_s}{L}$$ When the MOSFET is OFF, the inductor releases its stored energy in series with the source to the load. $$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_s - V_o}{L}$$ ### 🟢 The Buck-Boost Converter (Step-Down/Step-Up, Inverting) When the MOSFET is ON, the inductor charges from the source; the diode is OFF, isolating the load (which is supported by the capacitor). $$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_s}{L}$$ When the MOSFET is OFF, the inductor discharges into the load through the diode, but the output polarity is **inverted** (negative w.r.t. ground). $$\frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_o}{L}$$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign in the Buck-Boost output equation arises because the inductor current flows in a direction that makes the **cathode of the diode the positive output terminal**. This polarity inversion is a unique signature of this topology and is often tested in KTU exams. ## 2.2 ⚡ KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet) | Parameter | Buck Converter | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter | |---|---|---|---| | **Output Voltage (V_o)** | $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $V_o = \dfrac{-D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$ | | **Voltage Gain (M)** | $D$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{-D}{1-D}$ | | **Inductor Voltage (ON state)** | $V_s - V_o$ | $V_s$ | $V_s$ | | **Inductor Voltage (OFF state)** | $-V_o$ | $V_s - V_o$ | $V_o$ | | **Average Inductor Current (I_L)** | $\dfrac{I_o}{D}$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ | | **Inductor L (Critical/Boundary)** | $\dfrac{(1-D) \cdot R}{2 \cdot f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D(1-D)^2 \cdot R}{2 \cdot f_s}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)^2 \cdot R}{2 \cdot f_s}$ | | **Capacitor C (Ripple-based)** | $\dfrac{1-D}{8 \cdot L \cdot f_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o}}$ | $\dfrac{D}{R \cdot f_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o}}$ | $\dfrac{D}{R \cdot f_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o}}$ | | **Switch Voltage Stress** | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $V_s + V_o$ | | **Diode Voltage Stress** | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $V_s + \vert V_o \vert$ | | **Output Polarity** | Same as input | Same as input | **Inverted** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** In the table above, $\vert V_o \vert$ uses `\vert` and `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown syntax. In your exam answer scripts, use $|\cdot|$ as usual. Also note that the **critical inductance** formulas assume **boundary conduction mode** where the inductor current ripple $\Delta I_L = 2 I_{L,\text{avg}}$. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Buck Converters** are the heart of **Voltage Regulator Modules (VRMs)** in CPUs and GPUs. A modern processor may consume up to **250 A** at **0.8 V–1.4 V** from a 12 V supply using multi-phase buck converters. - **Boost Converters** are essential in **Photovoltaic (Solar) MPPT systems** to step up the panel voltage (typically **20 V–40 V**) to the inverter DC-link voltage (**380 V DC**). - **Buck-Boost Converters** find applications in **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)**, **battery-powered IoT devices**, and **LED drivers** that must operate over a wide input voltage range. # ⚡ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Buck Converter Output Voltage We will now exhaustively derive the **output voltage expression** for the buck converter using **Volt-Second Balance** on the inductor. ### Step 1: Define the Switching Period and Duty Cycle The total switching period is: $$T = T_{on} + T_{off}$$ The duty cycle D is defined as: $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$ Therefore, the OFF time is: $$T_{off} = T - T_{on} = T(1 - D)$$ ### Step 2: Write the Inductor Voltage Equation During T_on When the switch is ON, the source voltage $V_s$ is applied across the series combination of the inductor L and the load (with output $V_o$ across the capacitor). Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**: $$V_L = V_s - V_o$$ The change in inductor current during the ON interval is: $$\Delta I_{L,\text{on}} = \frac{V_s - V_o}{L} \cdot T_{on} = \frac{V_s - V_o}{L} \cdot D \cdot T$$ ### Step 3: Write the Inductor Voltage Equation During T_off When the switch is OFF, the inductor current freewheels through the diode. The voltage across the inductor is now simply $-V_o$ (the output opposes the inductor current decay): $$V_L = -V_o$$ The change in inductor current during the OFF interval is: $$\Delta I_{L,\text{off}} = \frac{-V_o}{L} \cdot T_{off} = \frac{-V_o}{L} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$ ### Step 4: Apply Volt-Second Balance In steady-state, the net change in inductor current over one full period must be **zero** (energy stored at the end of the cycle must equal the energy stored at the beginning). Equating the magnitude of the two ripples: $$\frac{V_s - V_o}{L} \cdot D \cdot T = \frac{V_o}{L} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$ Canceling the common terms $\frac{T}{L}$ from both sides: $$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D = V_o \cdot (1 - D)$$ ### Step 5: Algebraic Expansion Expanding the right-hand side: $$D \cdot V_s - D \cdot V_o = V_o - D \cdot V_o$$ The terms $-D \cdot V_o$ appear on both sides and cancel out: $$D \cdot V_s = V_o$$ Therefore, the **final output voltage equation** is: $$V_o = D \cdot V_s$$ This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the buck converter. ✔️ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Boost Converter Output Voltage ### Step 1: Inductor Voltage During T_on When the MOSFET is ON, the inductor is connected directly across the source (the diode is reverse-biased and the load is fed by the capacitor).
