The inductor charges from the source: $$V_L = V_s$$ The current ripple during the ON interval is: $$\Delta I_{L,\text{on}} = \frac{V_s}{L} \cdot D \cdot T$$ ### Step 2: Inductor Voltage During T_off When the MOSFET is OFF, the inductor opposes the change in current by reversing its polarity, so the diode conducts and the source is now in **series** with the inductor across the load. Applying KVL around the loop containing the source, inductor, and load: $$V_s - V_L - V_o = 0 \implies V_L = V_s - V_o$$ The current ripple during the OFF interval is: $$\Delta I_{L,\text{off}} = \frac{V_s - V_o}{L} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$ ### Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance Setting the ON-state volt-seconds equal to the OFF-state volt-seconds in magnitude: $$\frac{V_s}{L} \cdot D \cdot T = \frac{V_o - V_s}{L} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$ Note: $(V_s - V_o)$ is negative in boost mode, so we write its magnitude as $(V_o - V_s)$ for the ripple equation. ### Step 4: Algebraic Simplification Canceling $\frac{T}{L}$: $$V_s \cdot D = (V_o - V_s) \cdot (1 - D)$$ Expanding the right side: $$V_s \cdot D = V_o \cdot (1 - D) - V_s \cdot (1 - D)$$ Bringing $V_s$ terms to the left: $$V_s \cdot D + V_s \cdot (1 - D) = V_o \cdot (1 - D)$$ The left side simplifies to $V_s$: $$V_s \cdot [D + (1 - D)] = V_s = V_o \cdot (1 - D)$$ Therefore: $$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}$$ This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the boost converter. ✔️ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Buck-Boost Converter Output Voltage ### Step 1: Inductor Voltage During T_on When the MOSFET is ON, the diode is reverse-biased (the output capacitor is isolated from the inductor and supplies the load). The inductor charges from the source: $$V_L = V_s$$ Ripple equation: $$\Delta I_{L,\text{on}} = \frac{V_s}{L} \cdot D \cdot T$$ ### Step 2: Inductor Voltage During T_off When the MOSFET is OFF, the inductor current must continue to flow. The diode becomes forward-biased, providing a path for the inductor to discharge into the load. The voltage across the inductor is now $-V_o$ (the negative output): $$V_L = V_o$$ The ripple equation magnitude: $$\Delta I_{L,\text{off}} = \frac{V_o}{L} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$ ### Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance $$\frac{V_s}{L} \cdot D \cdot T = \frac{V_o}{L} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$ ### Step 4: Algebraic Simplification Canceling $\frac{T}{L}$: $$V_s \cdot D = V_o \cdot (1 - D)$$ Solving for $V_o$: $$V_o = \frac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}$$ Because of the **inverted output polarity** (the diode is oriented such that the inductor's discharge path reverses the output node voltage with respect to ground), we include a negative sign: $$V_o = \frac{-D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}$$ This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the buck-boost converter. ✔️ --- ## 3.4 Numerical Example for Exam Practice (KTU-Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example:** A buck converter is supplied from a **$V_s = 24 \text{ V}$** DC source and operates at a switching frequency of **$f_s = 50 \text{ kHz}$**. The load resistance is **$R = 10 \, \Omega$** and the desired output voltage is **$V_o = 12 \text{ V}$**. **Step (a):** Calculate the duty cycle D. $$D = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{12}{24} = 0.5$$ **Step (b):** Calculate the ON and OFF times. $$T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^3} = 20 \, \mu s$$ $$T_{on} = D \cdot T = 0.5 \times 20 \, \mu s = 10 \, \mu s$$ $$T_{off} = (1 - D) \cdot T = 0.5 \times 20 \, \mu s = 10 \, \mu s$$ **Step (c):** Calculate the average output current. $$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{12}{10} = 1.2 \, \text{A}$$ **Step (d):** Calculate the average inductor current (note: $I_L = I_o / D$ in CCM buck). $$I_L = \frac{I_o}{D} = \frac{1.2}{0.5} = 2.4 \, \text{A}$$ **Step (e):** Calculate the critical inductance assuming $30\%$ ripple. $$\Delta I_L = 0.3 \times I_L = 0.3 \times 2.4 = 0.72 \, \text{A}$$ $$L_c = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T}{\Delta I_L} = \frac{(24 - 12) \times 0.5 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{0.72}$$ $$L_c = \frac{12 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{0.72} = \frac{120 \times 10^{-6}}{0.72} \approx 166.67 \, \mu H$$ Therefore, choose a standard value: **$L = 200 \, \mu H$** to remain safely in CCM. # ⚡ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > The following **Mermaid block diagrams** illustrate the functional architecture of each converter topology, including the source, switching element (MOSFET), freewheeling diode, energy storage elements (L, C), and the load. These are adapted as block-level functional flow maps per the KTU rendering safeguards. ## 4.1 Buck Converter — Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD srcA[DC Source Vs] -->|Positive terminal| swA[MOSFET Switch S] swA -->|Conducts during T_on| lA[Inductor L] lA -->|Smooths current| capA[Capacitor C] capA -->|Filters voltage| ldA[Load Resistor R] ldA -->|Return path| gndA[Ground] swA -.->|OFF state path| dA[Freewheeling Diode D] dA -->|Cathode at switch node| swA pwmA[PWM Gate Driver Signal] -->|Controls ON time| swA capA --- gndA ldA --- gndA ``` **Operational Reading of the Diagram:** - When **PWM** is HIGH → MOSFET turns ON → current flows from **Source → L → C → Load**. - When **PWM** is LOW → MOSFET turns OFF → inductor current freewheels through the **Diode D**. --- ## 4.2 Boost Converter — Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD srcB[DC Source Vs] -->|Positive terminal| lB[Inductor L] lB -->|Charged during T_on| swB[MOSFET Switch S] swB -->|Returns to ground during ON| gndB[Ground] srcB -->|Source in series during OFF| dB[Diode D] lB -->|Inductor discharge path| dB dB -->|Forward biased during T_off| capB[Output Capacitor C] capB -->|Holds output voltage| ldB[Load Resistor R] ldB -->|Return to source negative| srcB pwmB[PWM Gate Driver Signal] -->|Controls switch| swB capB --- gndB ``` **Operational Reading of the Diagram:** - When **PWM** is HIGH → MOSFET shorts the inductor across the source → energy stored in **L**; diode is reverse-biased; load is supplied by **C**. - When **PWM** is LOW → MOSFET opens; inductor voltage reverses; **D** conducts; energy is delivered to load in series with source, **boosting the output voltage**. --- ## 4.3 Buck-Boost Converter — Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph TD srcC[DC Source Vs] -->|Positive terminal| lC[Inductor L] lC -->|Charged during T_on| swC[MOSFET Switch S] swC -->|Returns to ground during ON| gndC[Ground] srcC --- gndC lC -->|Discharge path during T_off| dC[Diode D] dC -->|Cathode to load| capC[Output Capacitor C] capC -->|Holds inverted output| ldC[Load Resistor R] ldC -->|Returns to ground| gndC pwmC[PWM Gate Driver Signal] -->|Controls switch| swC capC --- gndC noteC[Output Polarity: INVERTED w.r.t. source] -.-> ldC ``` **Operational Reading of the Diagram:** - When **PWM** is HIGH → MOSFET ON → inductor charges from source; diode is reverse-biased; load is supplied by capacitor. - When **PWM** is LOW → MOSFET OFF → inductor current freewheels through **D** into the load with **inverted polarity** (cathode of D is the positive output terminal). --- ## 4.4 Signal Waveform Comparison (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR pwmSig[PWM Gate Signal
Square wave 0 to Vg] -->|Time axis| indVolt1[Inductor Voltage
VL Buck: + to -] pwmSig -->|Time axis| indVolt2[Inductor Voltage
VL Boost: + to -] pwmSig -->|Time axis| indVolt3[Inductor Voltage
VL BuckBoost: + to -] outWave[Output Voltage
Constant average Vo] -->|Filtered by C| loadResp[Load Response
Low ripple DC] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Identification Tip for Lab Viva:** In an oscilloscope view, the **inductor current** waveform is a **triangular wave** (rising during $T_{on}$, falling during $T_{off}$), while the **switch voltage** waveform is a **square wave** (zero when ON, equal to $V_s$ or $V_o$ depending on topology when OFF). The **output voltage** is a near-constant DC with a small triangular ripple. # ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a chopper and list the three basic non-isolated DC-DC converter topologies.** **Model Answer:** A chopper is a power electronic switching device that converts a fixed DC input voltage into a variable DC output voltage by varying the **duty cycle (D)** of the controlled switch. The three basic non-isolated topologies are: 1. **Buck Converter** — Step-Down ($V_o < V_s$, $V_o = D \cdot V_s$) 2. **Boost Converter** — Step-Up ($V_o > V_s$, $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$) 3. **Buck-Boost Converter** — Step-Up/Down with inverted output ($V_o = \dfrac{-D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$) --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and how is it maintained in a buck converter?** **Model Answer:** CCM refers to the operating condition where the inductor current **never falls to zero** during the entire switching cycle. To maintain CCM in a buck converter, the inductance value must exceed the **critical inductance (L_c)**, given by: $$L_c = \frac{(1 - D) \cdot R}{2 \cdot f_s}$$ If the actual inductance $L < L_c$, the converter enters **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)**, where the output voltage becomes a function of load current, complicating control. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a **boost converter** operating in CCM. Clearly state the **Volt-Second Balance Principle** and show the inductor voltage waveforms for both switching states. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A boost converter is supplied from a **$V_s = 12 \text{ V}$** source. The load resistance is $R = 24 \, \Omega$, the switching frequency is $f_s = 25 \text{ kHz}$, and the duty cycle is $D = 0.4$. Calculate: (i) Output voltage $V_o$ (ii) Average output current $I_o$ (iii) Average inductor current $I_L$ (iv) Critical inductance $L_c$ **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Question A ##### Part (a): Derivation of Boost Output Voltage **Statement of Volt-Second Balance:** In steady-state, the average voltage across an inductor over one complete switching cycle must be zero. Equivalently, the **volt-seconds applied** during $T_{on}$ must equal the **volt-seconds removed** during $T_{off}$. **Switch ON State (T_on = D·T):** The MOSFET conducts, shorting the inductor across the source. The diode is reverse-biased. Applying KVL: $$V_{L,\text{on}} = V_s$$ **Switch OFF State (T_off = (1-D)·T):** The MOSFET opens. The diode is forward-biased, connecting the inductor in series with the source across the load. Applying KVL: $$V_{L,\text{off}} = V_s - V_o$$ **Applying Volt-Second Balance:** $$V_s \cdot D \cdot T + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T = 0$$ Dividing by $T$ and rearranging: $$V_s \cdot D + V_s \cdot (1 - D) = V_o \cdot (1 - D)$$ $$V_s = V_o \cdot (1 - D)$$ $$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}}$$ ##### Part (b): Numerical Calculations **(i) Output Voltage:** $$V_o = \frac{V_s}{1 - D} = \frac{12}{1 - 0.4} = \frac{12}{0.6} = 20 \, \text{V}$$ **(ii) Average Output Current:** $$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{20}{24} = 0.833 \, \text{A}$$ **(iii) Average Inductor Current:** For a boost converter, $I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{1 - D}$: $$I_L = \frac{0.833}{1 - 0.4} = \frac{0.833}{0.6} = 1.389 \, \text{A}$$ **(iv) Critical Inductance:** $$L_c = \frac{D \cdot (1 - D)^2 \cdot R}{2 \cdot f_s}$$ $$L_c = \frac{0.4 \times (0.6)^2 \times 24}{2 \times 25 \times 10^3}$$ $$L_c = \frac{0.4 \times 0.36 \times 24}{50{,}000} = \frac{3.456}{50{,}000} \approx 69.12 \, \mu H$$ --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **buck-boost converter** in CCM. Prove that the output voltage is $V_o = \dfrac{-D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A buck converter has $V_s = 20 \text{ V}$, $D = 0.6$, $R = 5 \, \Omega$, $f_s = 40 \text{ kHz}$. The allowed inductor current ripple is $\Delta I_L = 0.5 \, \text{A}$. Calculate: (i) Output voltage $V_o$ and load current $I_o$ (ii) Average inductor current $I_L$ (iii) Required minimum inductance $L_{min}$ (iv) Required capacitance $C$ if the output voltage ripple must be limited to $1\%$ of $V_o$ **(7 Marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution to Question B ##### Part (a): Buck-Boost Operation and Derivation **Circuit Operation:** The buck-boost converter has the inductor in series with the source-switch junction, and the load is connected across the output capacitor with the diode in between. The switching is as follows: - **During T_on:** The MOSFET conducts. The source $V_s$ charges the inductor. The diode is reverse-biased, isolating the load. The output capacitor supplies the load current. - **During T_off:** The MOSFET opens. The inductor current must continue to flow, so it forward-biases the diode and discharges into the load capacitor and load resistor. Critically, the **output polarity is inverted** with respect to the input. **Derivation:** During T_on, inductor voltage is $V_s$; during T_off, inductor voltage is $V_o$ (positive value of inverted output). Applying volt-second balance: $$V_s \cdot D \cdot T = V_o \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$ $$V_o = \frac{D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}$$ Including the **negative sign** for inverted polarity: $$\boxed{V_o = \frac{-D \cdot V_s}{1 - D}}$$ ##### Part (b): Buck Converter Numerical Calculations **(i) Output Voltage and Load Current:** $$V_o = D \cdot V_s = 0.6 \times 20 = 12 \, \text{V}$$ $$I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4 \, \text{A}$$ **(ii) Average Inductor Current:** $$I_L = \frac{I_o}{D} = \frac{2.4}{0.6} = 4.0 \, \text{A}$$ **(iii) Minimum Inductance:** Using the ripple equation during T_on: $$L_{min} = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T}{\Delta I_L}$$ $$T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{40 \times 10^3} = 25 \, \mu s$$ $$L_{min} = \frac{(20 - 12) \times 0.6 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5} = \frac{8 \times 15 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5} = \frac{120 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5}$$ $$L_{min} = 240 \, \mu H$$ **(iv) Required Capacitance:** Allowed ripple: $\Delta V_o = 0.01 \times 12 = 0.12 \, \text{V}$ For a buck converter, the capacitor supplies the load current during T_on, and the ripple is given by: $$\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta I_L}{8 \cdot C \cdot f_s}$$ $$C = \frac{\Delta I_L}{8 \cdot f_s \cdot \Delta V_o} = \frac{0.5}{8 \times 40 \times 10^3 \times 0.12} = \frac{0.5}{38{,}400}$$ $$C \approx 13.02 \, \mu F$$ Therefore, select a standard value: **$C = 15 \, \mu F$** (with appropriate voltage rating, e.g., 25 V). --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Buck-Boost Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the Negative Sign:** The most common error is writing $V_o = \dfrac{D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$ instead of $V_o = \dfrac{-D \cdot V_s}{1-D}$. The negative sign is **mandatory** in KTU answer scripts to indicate the inverted polarity. Loss: **2 marks**. > 2. **Wrong Diode Orientation in the Circuit Diagram:** If the diode is drawn pointing the wrong way, the entire operation is incorrect. The diode must be oriented such that its **cathode is at the output node** (so the inductor's discharge path produces a negative output w.r.t. ground). > 3. **Confusing Inductor and Output Current Relationships:** In CCM, $I_L = I_o / D$ (Buck) and $I_L = I_o / (1-D)$ (Boost and Buck-Boost). Mixing these up leads to wrong current ratings, which in a real design would destroy the inductor. > 4. **Not Stating CCM Assumption:** Always write "Assuming Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)..." at the start of your derivation. The formula $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ is **only valid in CCM**. In DCM, a more complex expression applies. --- ## 5.4 🔖 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam:** - ✅ The three basic DC-DC converters are **Buck (Step-Down)**, **Boost (Step-Up)**, and **Buck-Boost (Inverting Step-Down/Up)**. - ✅ All converters operate using a **controlled switch (MOSFET/IGBT) + a freewheeling diode + an inductor + a capacitor** as the four essential building blocks. - ✅ The **duty cycle D = T_on / T** is the primary control variable. - ✅ The **Volt-Second Balance** (or Inductor-Ripple Equality) principle is the foundation of all CCM derivations. - ✅ **Output Voltage Equations (CCM):** - Buck: $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ - Boost: $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$ - Buck-Boost: $V_o = -D \cdot V_s / (1-D)$ - ✅ **Output Polarity:** Buck and Boost are **non-inverting**; Buck-Boost is **inverting**. - ✅ **Inductor Current (CCM):** $I_L = I_o / D$ for Buck; $I_L = I_o / (1-D)$ for Boost and Buck-Boost. - ✅ **Critical Inductance** ensures the converter remains in CCM; choose $L \geq L_c$ in design. - ✅ **Capacitor Sizing** is governed by the allowable output voltage ripple (typically 1%–5% of $V_o$). - ✅ **Switch and Diode Voltage Stresses:** Buck & Boost switch stress is $V_s$ and $V_o$ respectively; Buck-Boost switch stress is $V_s + \vert V_o \vert$ (highest). - ✅ **Lab Realization Steps:** (1) Wire the circuit on a breadboard or PCB. (2) Generate a PWM signal using a function generator or microcontroller. (3) Measure $V_o$ with a multimeter. (4) Observe inductor current and switch voltage waveforms on a DSO. (5) Verify $V_o$ matches the formula within ±5% tolerance. - ✅ **Typical KTU Lab Viva Question:** "Why is the Buck-Boost output inverted?" — Answer: *"Because during T_off, the inductor current freewheels through the diode into the load with a polarity opposite to the input source, making the output node negative with respect to ground."* - ✅ **Practical Tip:** When using a real MOSFET in the lab, always include a **gate driver** (e.g., TLP250, IR2110) because microcontroller PWM signals cannot drive the MOSFET gate capacitance directly.

topics.title: Realization of Half Bridge Converter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Realization of Half Bridge Converter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Realization of Half Bridge Converter

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Realization of a Half-Bridge Converter* refers to the practical hardware implementation and testing of a single-phase half-bridge inverter/converter topology using power semiconductor switches (typically MOSFETs or IGBTs), driven by gate pulses, to convert a DC input voltage into a controlled AC or stepped DC output across a load.

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Half-Bridge Converter (HBC)** is a fundamental power electronics topology consisting of **two controlled switches** (S1 and S2) and **two split DC source capacitors** (C1 and C2) that form the "bridge" half-leg. The switches operate in a complementary (bi-polar) switching pattern with a small dead-time to prevent shoot-through. The load is connected between the **midpoint of the two switches** and the **center-tap of the DC source**.

The standard KTU lab realization involves:
- DC bus voltage ($V_s$ or $V_{dc}$)
- Two equal-valued DC link capacitors ($C_1 = C_2$) splitting the bus
- Two power switches (IGBTs/MOSFETs) forming a phase leg
- Gate driver circuit (opto-isolated or bootstrap)
- PWM/square-wave control signal from a microcontroller or function generator
- Resistive/RL load for observation

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The See-Saw of Power:**
> Imagine a **see-saw** in a playground. Two children sit on either end (Switch 1 and Switch 2). They take turns pushing themselves UP and DOWN. When the left child goes up, the right comes down — *never both up at the same time* (that would be a short circuit / shoot-through). The center of the see-saw is where the **load** is attached. As the see-saw moves, the load experiences alternating motion (UP-DOWN), just like the output voltage alternates between $+V_s/2$ and $-V_s/2$.

A simpler way: think of it as a **two-way water tap**. Only one pipe can be open at a time. When Switch 1 is ON, current flows from the *top half* of the tank to the load. When Switch 2 is ON, current flows from the load into the *bottom half* of the tank. The two large capacitors act as **two water tanks** stacked vertically, and the load sees the difference.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|
| Switching Frequency ($f_s$) | **1 kHz – 20 kHz** (audio range typical: 1–2 kHz for KTU lab) |
| DC Bus Voltage ($V_s$) | **12 V – 48 V** (typical KTU kit) |
| Load Resistance ($R_L$) | **10 Ω – 100 Ω** |
| Capacitor Value ($C_1, C_2$) | **470 µF – 2200 µF** (electrolytic) |
| Dead-Time ($t_d$) | **1 µs – 5 µs** |
| Dead-band voltage level | **0 V** (midpoint) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always explicitly state the **mid-point voltage = $V_s/2$** in your lab record. This is the most commonly tested value in viva.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Two Switching Modes

The half-bridge converter operates in **two complementary modes** based on which switch is conducting:

### Mode 1: Switch S1 ON, Switch S2 OFF
- Current path: $V_s \rightarrow S_1 \rightarrow \text{Load} \rightarrow C_2 \rightarrow \text{Ground}$
- Output voltage: $v_o = +V_s/2$
- $C_1$ discharges slightly into the load
- $C_2$ charges from the supply via the load

### Mode 2: Switch S1 OFF, Switch S2 ON
- Current path: $C_1 \rightarrow \text{Load} \rightarrow S_2 \rightarrow \text{Ground}$
- Output voltage: $v_o = -V_s/2$
- $C_1$ charges from the supply
- $C_2$ discharges into the load

### Mode 3 (Dead-Time): Both Switches OFF
- Both switches are OFF for a brief interval $t_d$
- Current freewheels through the **anti-parallel diodes** (D1 or D2)
- This prevents destructive **shoot-through** current

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| RMS Output Voltage (Square Wave) | $V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{V_s}{2}$ | Volts | Bipolar switching, symmetric |
| Peak Output Voltage | $V_{o,peak} = \dfrac{V_s}{2}$ | Volts | Bipolar switching |
| Fundamental RMS (Square Wave) | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{0.45 \cdot V_s}{2} = 0.225 \, V_s$ | Volts | First harmonic, $n=1$ |
| n-th Harmonic RMS | $V_{on} = \dfrac{4 V_s}{n \pi \sqrt{2}} \cdot \cos\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{2}\right) \sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\alpha}{2}\right) \cdot \ldots$ | Volts | n is odd (1, 3, 5, …) |
| THD (Square Wave) | $THD = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \approx 48.3\%$ | % | Theoretical maximum |
| Capacitor Voltage | $V_{C1} = V_{C2} = \dfrac{V_s}{2}$ | Volts | Balanced capacitors |
| Switching Period | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | seconds | — |
| Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_s}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le D \le 0.5$ for HBC |
| Dead-Time Constraint | $t_d \le \dfrac{T_s}{20}$ | seconds | Practical safe limit |
| Output Power (R-load) | $P_o = \dfrac{V_{o,rms}^2}{R_L} = \dfrac{V_s^2}{4 R_L}$ | Watts | Purely resistive |
| Power Switch Voltage Stress | $V_{sw} = V_s$ | Volts | Peak inverse rating |
| Power Switch Current Stress | $I_{sw,peak} = \dfrac{V_s}{2 R_L}$ | Amps | Resistive load peak |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions (KTU Viva Favorite):**
> 1. For **square wave** operation: $D = 0.5$, fundamental frequency = $f_s$
> 2. For **SPWM** operation: $D$ varies sinusoidally around 0.5
> 3. The **mid-point voltage** must remain at $V_s/2$ — imbalance causes DC offset and even harmonics

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Half-Bridge so important?**
> 1. **Building block**: Every full-bridge is made of TWO half-bridges. Mastering this topology means you can understand H-bridges, 3-phase inverters, and motor drives.
> 2. **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)**: Used in laptop chargers, phone adapters, and computer power supplies.
> 3. **Solar Microinverters**: Common topology in residential PV systems.
> 4. **Class-D Audio Amplifiers**: Your home theater amplifier likely uses a half-bridge or full-bridge output stage.
> 5. **Motor Drives**: Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for induction motors use this exact half-bridge cell as the basic unit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of RMS Output Voltage (Square Wave Operation)

**Given:** A half-bridge converter with DC bus voltage $V_s$ and equal capacitors $C_1 = C_2$ such that $V_{C1} = V_{C2} = V_s/2$.

**Step 1: Define the output waveform.**

The output voltage $v_o(t)$ across the load alternates between two levels:
- $v_o = +V_s/2$ for $0 \le t \lt T_s/2$ (when S1 conducts)
- $v_o = -V_s/2$ for $T_s/2 \le t \lt T_s$ (when S2 conducts)

This is a **symmetrical square wave** with period $T_s$.

**Step 2: Apply the RMS definition.**

The RMS value of a periodic waveform is given by:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_s} \int_{0}^{T_s} v_o^2(t)\, dt}
$$

**Step 3: Split the integral into the two half-cycles.**

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_s}\left[ \int_{0}^{T_s/2} \left(\frac{V_s}{2}\right)^2 dt + \int_{T_s/2}^{T_s} \left(-\frac{V_s}{2}\right)^2 dt \right]}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrand.**

Since $\left(-\dfrac{V_s}{2}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{V_s}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{V_s^2}{4}$, both integrals are identical:

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_s}\left[ \frac{V_s^2}{4}\cdot\frac{T_s}{2} + \frac{V_s^2}{4}\cdot\frac{T_s}{2} \right]}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify the bracket.**

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T_s} \cdot \frac{V_s^2 \cdot T_s}{4}}
$$

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_s^2}{4}} = \frac{V_s}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_s}{2}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Fundamental (1st Harmonic) Component

**Step 1: Use Fourier Series for square wave.**

A square wave of amplitude $A = V_s/2$ has the Fourier expansion:

$$
v_o(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $A = V_s/2$.**

$$
v_o(t) = \frac{4 \cdot (V_s/2)}{\pi}\sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}\sin(n\omega t)
$$

$$
v_o(t) = \frac{2V_s}{\pi}\left[\sin(\omega t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega t) + \cdots\right]
$$

**Step 3: Extract the fundamental amplitude ($n=1$).**

The peak value of the fundamental component is:

$$
V_{o1,peak} = \frac{2V_s}{\pi}
$$

**Step 4: Convert peak to RMS.**

$$
V_{o1,rms} = \frac{V_{o1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2V_s}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, V_s}{\pi}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{o1,rms} \approx 0.45\, V_s}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

**Step 1: Use the definition of THD.**

$$
THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{o1,rms}^2}}{V_{o1,rms}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total RMS.**

For a square wave, $V_{rms} = V_s/2$.

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$
THD = \frac{\sqrt{(V_s/2)^2 - (\sqrt{2}\,V_s/\pi)^2}}{\sqrt{2}\,V_s/\pi}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify numerator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = \sqrt{\frac{V_s^2}{4} - \frac{2V_s^2}{\pi^2}} = V_s\sqrt{\frac{1}{4} - \frac{2}{\pi^2}}
$$

**Step 5: Divide and evaluate.**

$$
THD = \frac{V_s\sqrt{\frac{1}{4} - \frac{2}{\pi^2}}}{\sqrt{2}\,V_s/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{4} - \frac{2}{\pi^2}}
$$

$$
THD = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{THD \approx 0.4832 \text{ or } 48.32\%}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Half-Bridge | Full-Bridge | Push-Pull |
|---|---|---|---|
| No. of Switches | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| Output Voltage | $V_s/2$ | $V_s$ | $V_s$ |
| Switch Voltage Stress | $V_s$ | $V_s$ | $2V_s$ |
| Component Cost | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Transformer Utilization | Poor | Best | Good |
| KTU Lab Popularity | **★★★★★** | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Common Application | Low-power SMPS | Motor drives | Audio amplifiers |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is a half-bridge converter? Draw its circuit diagram and explain its working in brief.**

**Model Answer:**

A **half-bridge converter** is a power electronic circuit that converts a DC input into a controlled AC or DC output using two switches (S1, S2) and two equal-valued DC link capacitors (C1, C2). The load is connected between the midpoint of the switches and the midpoint of the capacitors.

**Working:**
- When **S1 is ON** and S2 is OFF, current flows from the upper capacitor through S1, through the load, and back to ground. The output voltage is $v_o = +V_s/2$.
- When **S2 is ON** and S1 is OFF, current flows from ground through S2, through the load (in reverse), back to the upper capacitor. The output voltage is $v_o = -V_s/2$.

A square-wave AC output is obtained by switching S1 and S2 alternately. The peak output voltage equals $V_s/2$ and the RMS value is also $V_s/2$ (for square wave).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is shoot-through in a half-bridge converter? How is it prevented?**

**Model Answer:**

**Shoot-through** is a fault condition in a half-bridge (or any bridge) converter when **both switches S1 and S2 are ON simultaneously**, creating a low-impedance path directly across the DC bus through the two switches. This causes a very large short-circuit current limited only by the parasitic resistances, which can destroy the switches within microseconds.

**Prevention methods:**
1. **Dead-time insertion:** A small time gap (1–5 µs) is inserted between the turn-OFF of one switch and the turn-ON of the complementary switch.
2. **Complementary gate signals:** Using an inverter (NOT gate) ensures S2 is OFF when S1 is ON.
3. **Hardware interlock:** Logic gates in the gate driver circuit prevent simultaneous ON signals.
4. **Overcurrent protection:** Fast-acting fuses or electronic current limiters trip during fault.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a single-phase half-bridge inverter. Derive the expression for its RMS output voltage and the fundamental RMS voltage.

**(b)** A single-phase half-bridge inverter has a DC source of $V_s = 100$ V. It supplies a purely resistive load of $R_L = 10$ Ω. Determine:
1. The RMS output voltage
2. The RMS fundamental output voltage
3. The RMS load current
4. The power delivered to the load
5. The total harmonic distortion (THD) of the output voltage

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a)

The **single-phase half-bridge inverter** consists of:
- A DC source $V_s$ split into two equal halves by capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$
- Two controlled switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ (with anti-parallel diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$)
- A load $R_L$ connected between the switch midpoint and the capacitor midpoint

**Operation (refer to timing diagram in Section 4.2):**

- **Mode 1 (0 to $T_s/2$):** $S_1$ is ON, $S_2$ is OFF. Load current flows from $+V_s/2$ through $S_1$, through $R_L$, to ground. $v_o = +V_s/2$.
- **Mode 2 ($T_s/2$ to $T_s$):** $S_1$ is OFF, $S_2$ is ON. Load current flows from ground through $R_L$, through $S_2$, to $-V_s/2$. $v_o = -V_s/2$.

**Derivation of RMS output voltage (see Section 3.1):**

$$
V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_s}{2}
$$

**Derivation of fundamental RMS (see Section 3.2):**

$$
V_{o1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.45\, V_s
$$

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Given:** $V_s = 100$ V, $R_L = 10$ Ω, square-wave operation

**1. RMS Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{o,rms} = \frac{V_s}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ V}
$$

**2. RMS Fundamental Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{o1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi} = \frac{1.414 \times 100}{3.1416} = 45.02 \text{ V}
$$

**3. RMS Load Current:**

$$
I_{o,rms} = \frac{V_{o,rms}}{R_L} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ A}
$$

**4. Power Delivered to the Load:**

$$
P_o = \frac{V_{o,rms}^2}{R_L} = \frac{50^2}{10} = \frac{2500}{10} = 250 \text{ W}
$$

**5. Total Harmonic Distortion:**

$$
THD = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} = \sqrt{\frac{9.8696}{8} - 1} = \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2337}
$$

$$
THD \approx 0.4834 = 48.34\%
$$

**Result Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $V_{o,rms}$ | **50 V** |
| $V_{o1,rms}$ | **45.02 V** |
| $I_{o,rms}$ | **5 A** |
| $P_o$ | **250 W** |
| $THD$ | **48.34 %** |

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** List and explain the components required for the practical realization of a half-bridge converter in the laboratory. Discuss the role of dead-time in the gate signal.

**(b)** A half-bridge DC-DC converter operates with a switching frequency of $f_s = 2$ kHz, duty cycle $D = 0.4$, and DC input $V_s = 48$ V. Compute the average output voltage, the switching period, and sketch the gate pulse and output voltage waveforms with proper labeling.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Components required for realization of a half-bridge converter:**

1. **DC Source** ($V_s$): Provides the input DC voltage. Usually a regulated bench power supply (0–30 V or 0–60 V) or a rectified-and-filtered mains supply.

2. **DC Link Capacitors** ($C_1, C_2$): Two equal-valued, large electrolytic capacitors that split the DC bus into two symmetric halves. Typical value: 470 µF to 2200 µF, voltage rating ≥ $1.5 \times V_s$. Their function is to:
   - Provide a stiff midpoint voltage ($V_s/2$)
   - Absorb high-frequency switching ripple current
   - Act as instantaneous energy reservoirs during switching

3. **Power Switches** ($S_1, S_2$): IGBTs or power MOSFETs rated for at least the full DC bus voltage with a safety margin (typically $V_{rating} \ge 1.5 V_s$). They handle the main load current.

4. **Freewheel / Anti-parallel Diodes** ($D_1, D_2$): Provide a path for **inductive load currents** when both switches are OFF, preventing destructive voltage spikes. They are often integrated within the IGBT/MOSFET module.

5. **Gate Driver Circuit**: Isolates the low-voltage control circuit from the high-power switch terminals and provides the high-current gate charge/discharge. Common ICs: **TLP250, IR2110, HCPL-3120**.

6. **Gate Resistors** ($R_g$): Limit the gate charging current (typically 10–100 Ω) to control $dv/dt$ and ringing.

7. **Control Signal Source**: Function generator, PWM controller IC (SG3525, TL494), or a microcontroller (Arduino/STM32/DSP).

8. **Snubber Circuits** (RC or RCD): Across each switch to limit voltage spikes during turn-off (dv/dt protection).

9. **Load**: Resistive ($R_L$) for basic testing, or R-L for observing freewheeling action.

10. **Measurement Equipment**: Oscilloscope (2-channel, ≥ 20 MHz), multimeters, current probes.

**Role of Dead-Time:**

**Dead-time** ($t_d$) is a small intentional delay inserted between the turn-OFF of one switch and the turn-ON of the complementary switch. Its primary role is to **prevent shoot-through** (cross-conduction) — a destructive short-circuit across the DC bus. The minimum dead-time is determined by the switch's turn-off time:

$$
t_d \ge t_{off,max} + t_{margin}
$$

A typical $t_d$ is **1–5 µs**. The downside of dead-time is a small distortion in the output waveform (slight voltage loss and added harmonics), but this is a necessary trade-off for switch safety.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Given:** $f_s = 2$ kHz, $D = 0.4$, $V_s = 48$ V

**Step 1: Switching period.**

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{2000} = 0.5 \text{ ms} = 500 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 2: ON and OFF times.**

$$
T_{on} = D \cdot T_s = 0.4 \times 500 = 200 \,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{off} = (1 - D) \cdot T_s = 0.6 \times 500 = 300 \,\mu s
$$

**Step 3: Average output voltage (for a half-bridge DC-DC, output is between 0 and $V_s/2$).**

For a half-bridge with unipolar output, the average output is:

$$
V_{o,avg} = D \cdot \frac{V_s}{2} = 0.4 \times \frac{48}{2} = 0.4 \times 24 = 9.6 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4: Sketch the waveforms.**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Switching period $T_s$ | **500 µs** |
| ON time $T_{on}$ | **200 µs** |
| OFF time $T_{off}$ | **300 µs** |
| Output high level | **+24 V** (= $V_s/2$) |
| Output low level | **−24 V** (= $-V_s/2$) |
| Average output | **9.6 V** |

**Gate and Output Waveform Description:**

```
Gate S1:  ┌────┐         ┌────┐         (200 µs ON, 300 µs OFF)
          │    │         │    │
──────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────

Gate S2:  ──────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─── (complementary to S1)
                │    │         │    │

Vo:       ┌──┐    ┌──┐              +24 V
          │  │    │  │              0 V
──────────┘  └────┘  └──────────── -24 V
          ↑200µs↑300µs
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the RMS output voltage formula: Many students write $V_{o,rms} = V_s$ instead of $V_s/2$. The half-bridge output is HALF the bus voltage — this is the #1 KTU marking trap.
> 2. **Shoot-through question:** Students often describe the *symptom* (short circuit) but forget the *prevention* (dead-time, interlock). Always mention the dead-time explicitly for full marks.
> 3. **Fourier coefficients:** For square wave, only **odd harmonics** exist (1, 3, 5, …). Even harmonics (2, 4, 6, …) are ZERO due to half-wave symmetry. Forgetting this loses easy marks.
> 4. **Capacitor midpoint:** If you connect the load to one capacitor terminal instead of the midpoint, the circuit is no longer a half-bridge — it becomes a simple chopper. The KTU examiner WILL deduct marks for this.
> 5. **Unit mistakes:** $THD$ is a **dimensionless ratio** (or %). Writing "Volts" for THD is a common error.
> 6. **Practical vs. ideal:** Always state that the practical $V_{o,rms}$ is slightly LESS than $V_s/2$ due to switch voltage drops ($V_{CE,sat} \approx 1.5$–2 V for IGBTs).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **One-Page Revision Summary:**
> - [x] A **half-bridge converter** uses **2 switches + 2 capacitors** to convert DC to AC.
> - [x] Output is a **bipolar square wave** with levels $+V_s/2$ and $-V_s/2$.
> - [x] **RMS output voltage** $V_{o,rms} = V_s/2$ (for ideal square wave).
> - [x] **Fundamental RMS** $V_{o1,rms} = \sqrt{2}\,V_s/\pi \approx 0.45 V_s$.
> - [x] **THD of square wave** is fixed at ≈ 48.3% (cannot be reduced without filtering or PWM).
> - [x] **Shoot-through** is prevented using **dead-time** (1–5 µs) and complementary gating.
> - [x] **Switch voltage stress** = $V_s$ (full DC bus).
> - [x] **Switch current stress** = $V_s/(2R_L)$ peak (resistive load).
> - [x] **Capacitor value** must be large enough to maintain midpoint at $V_s/2$ under load.
> - [x] **Freewheel diodes** are essential for R-L loads — they carry current during switch transitions.
> - [x] **Gate driver** must be **isolated** (use TLP250 or IR2110) for safety and noise immunity.
> - [x] **Half-bridge = one leg of a full-bridge** — mastering it is a prerequisite for 3-phase inverter study.
> - [x] **Lab record must include:** circuit diagram, component list, gate pulse waveform, output waveform, frequency/period measurements, RMS calculation, and one-line conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of PWM IC TL 494
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Application of PWM IC TL 494

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **TL494 — Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)**
> The **TL494** is a **fixed-frequency, voltage-mode Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) controller IC** fabricated on a monolithic bipolar substrate. It integrates a complete PWM control loop — comprising a **5 V internal reference**, a **linear sawtooth oscillator**, **two high-gain error amplifiers**, a **dead-time control comparator**, a **PWM comparator**, and a **totem-pole output stage with ±500 mA drive capability** — on a single 16-pin DIP/SOIC chip. It is the de-facto industry-standard controller for **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)**, **DC–DC converters**, **inverters**, and **DC motor drives**.

The IC was originally pioneered by **Texas Instruments** and is now second-sourced by multiple vendors. It operates over a supply range of **VCC = 7 V to 40 V** and supports switching frequencies from **1 kHz to 300 kHz**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Conductor"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture — TL494 as a Traffic Conductor**
> Imagine a busy intersection where two streams of traffic (current) must NEVER collide (short-circuit). The TL494 acts like a **traffic conductor**: it decides precisely *how long* the green light stays ON for the "upper switch" and the "lower switch" of a power converter, while always inserting a mandatory **"all-red" buffer (dead time)** between them. The **error amplifier** is the conductor's *ear*, listening to the output voltage; the **oscillator** is the *metronome* setting the rhythm; and the **output transistors** are the *traffic lights* themselves.

## 1.3 Salient Features of TL494 (Board-Examiner Favourites)

The features below are the **most-repeated 3-mark short-answer content** in KTU examinations:

- **Built-in 5 V reference** with ±5 % tolerance (Pin 14).
- **Two independent error amplifiers** (EA1 and EA2) for voltage and current feedback.
- **Adjustable dead-time control** via Pin 4 (range 0 % to ~100 %).
- **Selectable output mode** — *Push-Pull* (each output at ≤ 50 % duty) or *Single-Ended* (paralleled outputs, ≤ 100 % duty).
- **Totem-pole output transistors** rated **±500 mA peak** sink/source.
- **Wide oscillator range**: **1 kHz to 300 kHz**, set by external R_T and C_T.
- **Under-voltage lockout (UVLO)** below **6 V** to prevent misfiring at low VCC.
- **PWM comparator** with internal feedback-path latch for clean duty-cycle transitions.

## 1.4 Why TL494? — Engineering Motivation

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Use Cases**
> - **Offline SMPS** (TV, PC, Laptop adapters, Battery chargers).
> - **DC–DC Buck / Boost / Push-Pull converters** in industrial drives.
> - **Solar Micro-Inverters** and **UPS** front-end converters.
> - **DC Motor Speed Control** through armature-voltage modulation.
> - **Battery-Equalisation Circuits** in EV battery management systems.

The IC eliminated the need for designers to hand-build discrete PWM circuits using op-amps, 555 timers, and discrete flip-flops — making **reliable, frequency-stable PWM control affordable** in cost-sensitive mass-produced power electronics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture — Functional Block Walk-Through

The TL494 is internally partitioned into **six functional blocks**. Understanding each is mandatory for the KTU lab-viva and the 14-mark design questions.

### Block 1: 5 V Internal Reference (Pin 14)
A band-gap-derived reference providing a stable **V_REF = 5 V ± 5 %** used to bias the oscillator, the error amplifiers, and the dead-time comparator. It can source up to **10 mA** to external circuitry (e.g., as pull-up reference for feedback divider).

### Block 2: Linear Sawtooth Oscillator (Pins 5, 6)
Comprises an **internal charging transistor** and a **comparator-driven discharge path**. External components:
- **C_T** (Pin 5 → GND) — timing capacitor.
- **R_T** (Pin 6 → V_REF) — timing resistor.

The capacitor charges linearly from approximately **0.3 V to 3.0 V** producing a sawtooth of amplitude $\approx$ **2.7 V peak-to-peak**. The charging current is set by $R_T$ from the 5 V reference, and the discharge occurs synchronously to ground when the upper threshold is reached.

### Block 3: Error Amplifier 1 (Pins 1, 2, 3) and Error Amplifier 2 (Pins 15, 16)
Both are **high-gain, single-supply op-amps** with open-loop gain ≈ 60–70 dB. Typical connection:
- **EA1** → senses output voltage (voltage-mode control loop).
- **EA2** → senses switch current (current-limit / over-current protection).
- The **output of EA1 (Pin 3)** is *OR-wired* with the **dead-time control voltage (Pin 4)** — whichever is *lower* wins and is fed to the PWM comparator. This is the **KTU-favourite concept** for the 7-mark "explain feedback" question.

### Block 4: Dead-Time Control Comparator (Pin 4)
- Pin 4 is fed a **DC control voltage (0 to 3.3 V)**.
- The comparator compares Pin 4 with the sawtooth on C_T.
- A *higher* Pin 4 voltage ⇒ *earlier* turn-off of the output ⇒ **larger dead time**.
- Maximum dead time (≈ 100 % OFF) occurs at **V_pin4 ≥ 3.0 V**.
- Zero dead time (max duty) occurs at **V_pin4 = 0 V**.

### Block 5: PWM Comparator
A single comparator that compares:
- The **lower of (V_pin3, V_pin4)** → from the feedback path.
- The **sawtooth ramp on C_T** → from the oscillator.

The comparator **resets the output latch** when the ramp exceeds the lower of the two control signals, thereby **defining the duty cycle**.

### Block 6: Output Stage (Pins 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13)
Contains **two NPN transistors** (Q1: pins 8/9, Q2: pins 10/11) with collectors and emitters brought out. Pin 13 selects the mode:
- **Pin 13 = GND** → **Push-Pull** mode (Q1 and Q2 alternate 180° out of phase, max duty = 50 %).
- **Pin 13 = V_REF** → **Single-Ended / Parallel** mode (Q1 and Q2 switch simultaneously, max duty = 100 %).

The output transistors are **open-collector / open-emitter** and the emitter pins are tied to GND through Pin 12 (or externally to a common emitter node).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise the table below — these are the only 5 equations asked across 90 % of KTU numerical questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Oscillator Frequency** | $f_{osc} = \dfrac{1.1}{R_T \cdot C_T}$ | **1 kHz – 300 kHz** |
| 2 | **Charging Current** | $I_{chg} = \dfrac{V_{REF} - 0.3}{R_T} = \dfrac{4.7}{R_T}$ | µA to mA |
| 3 | **Sawtooth Peak-to-Peak Swing** | $\Delta V_{ramp} = V_{H} - V_{L} \approx 2.7 \text{ V}$ | Volts |
| 4 | **Maximum Duty Cycle (Push-Pull)** | $D_{max} = 48 \, \%$ (fixed, with internal blanking) | – |
| 5 | **Maximum Duty Cycle (Single-Ended)** | $D_{max} = \dfrac{V_{REF} - V_{pin4}}{V_{ramp}} \times 100$ | 0 – 100 % |
| 6 | **Dead-Time Control Voltage** | $V_{DT} = \dfrac{D_{max} \cdot \Delta V_{ramp}}{100}$ | Volts |
| 7 | **Soft-Start RC Time Constant** | $\tau_{ss} = R_{ss} \cdot C_{ss}$ | Seconds |
| 8 | **Output Drive Current (per transistor)** | $I_{sink} = I_{source} = 500$ **mA peak** | mA |
| 9 | **Reference Output** | $V_{REF} = 5 \text{ V} \pm 5\,\%$ | Volts |
| 10 | **UVLO Threshold** | $V_{CC(uvlo)} \approx 6$ **V** | Volts |

> **Note on Pin 4 Dead-Time Control**: The output duty cycle is *inversely* related to V_pin4. When V_pin4 is at 0 V, the dead-time control contributes nothing, and the duty cycle is governed solely by the error-amplifier output (Pin 3).

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of TL494 in Engineering

The TL494's enduring popularity in the **four-decade-old power-electronics industry** stems from three reasons:
1. **Deterministic switching frequency** — independent of load, making EMI filter design straightforward.
2. **Two error amplifiers** — allow simultaneous **voltage regulation + cycle-by-cycle current limiting**, the cornerstone of modern SMPS protection.
3. **Flexible output stage** — directly drives **bipolar power transistors**, **power MOSFETs**, or **gate-drive transformers** for high-side isolation.

This is why the **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Module Experiment 2** mandates the TL494 — it is the *lowest-cost platform* on which a student can validate the **theory of closed-loop PWM control** against actual hardware.

# SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are written **exhaustively** — every algebraic transition is shown, exactly as expected in a 14-mark KTU valuation key. *No step is skipped.*

## 3.1 Derivation — Oscillator Frequency $f_{osc}$

### Statement
The frequency of oscillation of the TL494 is set by an external resistor $R_T$ connected between Pin 6 and $V_{REF} = 5$ V, and an external capacitor $C_T$ connected between Pin 5 and ground.

### Step 1 — Identify the Charging Mechanism
Inside the IC, the timing capacitor $C_T$ is charged by a current $I_{chg}$ flowing through $R_T$ from the 5 V reference. The charging current is:

$$
I_{chg} = \frac{V_{REF} - V_{CT(L)}}{R_T}
$$

where $V_{CT(L)} \approx 0.3$ V is the lower threshold of the internal comparator.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Standard Value

$$
I_{chg} = \frac{5 - 0.3}{R_T} = \frac{4.7}{R_T}
$$

### Step 3 — Express the Voltage Swing
The capacitor is charged from $V_L \approx 0.3$ V to $V_H \approx 3.0$ V, giving:

$$
\Delta V = V_H - V_L = 3.0 - 0.3 = 2.7 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 4 — Time to Charge $\Delta V$
For a constant-current charge of a capacitor:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{C_T \cdot \Delta V}{I_{chg}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{C_T \cdot 2.7}{\dfrac{4.7}{R_T}} = \frac{2.7 \cdot R_T \cdot C_T}{4.7}
$$

### Step 5 — Recognise That the Period is Roughly $2 \cdot \Delta t$ (Charge + Discharge)
The discharge phase of the capacitor (through the internal discharge transistor) is short but comparable. Empirically, the **oscillator period** is:

$$
T_{osc} = 2 \cdot \Delta t = \frac{2 \cdot 2.7 \cdot R_T \cdot C_T}{4.7}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Frequency Equation

$$
T_{osc} = \frac{5.4 \cdot R_T \cdot C_T}{4.7} \approx 1.15 \cdot R_T \cdot C_T
$$

Inverting:

$$
f_{osc} = \frac{1}{T_{osc}} = \frac{4.7}{5.4 \cdot R_T \cdot C_T}
$$

Rounded and using the manufacturer’s empirical constant (1.1 instead of 4.7/5.4 = 0.87, but datasheet uses 1.1 for the *typical* discharge behaviour):

$$
\boxed{\,f_{osc} = \frac{1.1}{R_T \cdot C_T}\,}
$$

This is the canonical TL494 oscillator formula found on every KTU reference sheet.

## 3.2 Derivation — Duty Cycle as a Function of V_pin4

### Statement
The maximum duty cycle in the **single-ended (paralleled)** output mode is governed by the dead-time control voltage on Pin 4.

### Step 1 — Comparator Transfer Function
The PWM comparator compares the sawtooth $V_{CT}(t)$ with the control voltage $V_{ctrl} = \min(V_{pin3}, V_{pin4})$. The output transistor is ON while $V_{CT}(t) < V_{ctrl}$.

### Step 2 — Time Above Threshold in One Cycle
The sawtooth rises linearly from $V_L$ to $V_H$ over $T_{osc}$. The instant at which $V_{CT} = V_{ctrl}$ is:

$$
t_{on} = T_{osc} \cdot \frac{V_{ctrl} - V_L}{V_H - V_L} = T_{osc} \cdot \frac{V_{ctrl} - 0.3}{2.7}
$$

### Step 3 — Define Duty Cycle
$D$ is the fraction of the period that the output is ON:

$$
D = \frac{t_{on}}{T_{osc}} = \frac{V_{ctrl} - 0.3}{2.7}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $V_{ctrl} = V_{pin4}$ (Dead-Time-Dominated Case)
In the worst-case (Pin 3 saturated high) the control voltage equals $V_{pin4}$:

$$
D = \frac{V_{pin4} - 0.3}{2.7} \times 100 \; \%
$$

### Step 5 — Boundary Conditions
- If $V_{pin4} \le 0.3$ V, $D = 0$ (output is permanently OFF — full dead time).
- If $V_{pin4} \ge 3.0$ V, $D \approx 100$ % (output is permanently ON — no dead time).

### Final Generalised Expression

$$
\boxed{\,D = \left(\dfrac{V_{pin4} - 0.3}{2.7}\right) \times 100 \; \% \quad \text{(Single-Ended Mode)}\,}
$$

In **push-pull mode** the same equation holds for *each* output, but the active period is automatically shifted by 180°, restricting the maximum to 50 % per channel.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style Design Problem

> **Question (KTU Pattern):** Design a TL494-based PWM oscillator to generate $f_{osc} = 40$ kHz. Choose $C_T = 0.001 \, \mu F$ and determine $R_T$. Also find the required Pin-4 voltage for 35 % duty cycle in single-ended mode.

### Step 1 — Use the Oscillator Formula

$$
R_T = \frac{1.1}{f_{osc} \cdot C_T}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
R_T = \frac{1.1}{40 \times 10^3 \cdot 0.001 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.1}{40 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

### Step 3 — Final Numerical Evaluation

$$
R_T = \frac{1.1}{4 \times 10^{-8}} = 27.5 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

> Choose the nearest standard E12 value: **$R_T = 27 \text{ k}\Omega$**.

### Step 4 — Pin-4 Voltage for 35 % Duty Cycle
Using the duty-cycle equation derived in §3.2:

$$
V_{pin4} = 0.3 + 0.35 \cdot 2.7 = 0.3 + 0.945 = 1.245 \text{ V}
$$

### Step 5 — Design Summary Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Calculated Value | Standard Value |
|-----------|--------|------------------|----------------|
| Oscillator Frequency | $f_{osc}$ | 40 kHz | 40 kHz |
| Timing Capacitor | $C_T$ | 0.001 µF | 1 nF (E12) |
| Timing Resistor | $R_T$ | 27.5 kΩ | **27 kΩ** |
| Pin-4 Voltage | $V_{pin4}$ | 1.245 V | 1.25 V (ref. divider) |
| Duty Cycle (single-ended) | $D$ | 35 % | 35 % |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operating-Mode Selection

> [!TIP]
> The matrix below is a **favourite viva question** — "When do you choose push-pull versus single-ended mode?"

| Criterion | Push-Pull Mode (Pin 13 = GND) | Single-Ended Mode (Pin 13 = V_REF) |
|-----------|--------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Output transistor Q1, Q2 | Alternate 180° | Switch in parallel (simultaneously) |
| Max duty per channel | **48 %** | **96 %** |
| Use case | **DC–AC inverters, full-bridge, half-bridge** | **Buck/Boost/Flyback SMPS** |
| Driver requirement | **Gate-drive transformer** or **level-shifter** | **Direct gate drive** to low-side switch |
| Dead time | **Always present** (built-in 4 % blanking) | User-controlled via Pin 4 |
| Transformer utilisation | Bi-directional flux — **full B-H swing** | Unidirectional flux — **half B-H swing** |
| Power level | Medium-High (100 W – 1 kW) | Low-Medium (5 W – 200 W) |

**Block-Level Narrative for the KTU Lab Record:**
1. The **DC bus** supplies both the TL494 and the half-bridge through the bulk capacitor.
2. The **RT/CT network** sets the switching frequency (typically 20–50 kHz to keep the transformer compact).
3. The **feedback divider** samples the output and feeds EA1 → closes the voltage loop.
4. The **current-sense resistor** feeds EA2 → provides cycle-by-cycle over-current protection.
5. **Pin 4** sets the maximum duty cycle / dead time.
6. The two output pins drive **gate-drive transformers** that level-shift the PWM to the high-side MOSFET.
7. The **transformer** delivers isolated AC to the load.
8. The **soft-start capacitor** on Pin 4 prevents inrush current at power-on.

# SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. The internal-choice structure is *strictly* 14-mark questions split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any four salient features of the TL494 PWM controller IC and state its operating supply-voltage range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Built-in **5 V internal reference** with ±5 % tolerance (Pin 14). *(0.5 mark)*
2. Two **independent high-gain error amplifiers** for voltage and current feedback. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Adjustable dead-time control** via Pin 4 (0 % to 100 %). *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Selectable output mode** — Push-Pull (max 48 % duty) or Single-Ended (max 96 % duty) via Pin 13. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Wide oscillator range**: 1 kHz to 300 kHz set by R_T and C_T. *(0.5 mark)*
6. **Operating supply range**: VCC = **7 V to 40 V**. *(0.5 mark)*

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the role of the dead-time control pin (Pin 4) in the TL494.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **dead-time control (Pin 4)** sets the *minimum OFF-time* (or *maximum duty cycle*) of the PWM output. It is internally compared with the sawtooth waveform on Pin 5; the comparator resets the PWM latch whenever the ramp exceeds the *lower* of V_pin3 and V_pin4. A **higher** V_pin4 voltage forces the output transistors to switch OFF *earlier* in the cycle, thereby **increasing the dead time** and protecting the power switches from *shoot-through* (cross-conduction). At **V_pin4 = 0 V**, the dead time is essentially zero and the duty cycle is governed entirely by the error-amplifier output on Pin 3. At **V_pin4 ≥ 3.0 V**, the output is permanently OFF (full dead time, useful for soft-start). *(Full 3 marks for complete explanation with boundary voltages.)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal-Choice Structure** — Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of the TL494 PWM controller IC.**

**Model Solution:**

The TL494 comprises six functional blocks:

1. **5 V Reference (Pin 14)** — band-gap-derived, sources 10 mA, used to bias the oscillator and amplifiers. *(1 mark)*
2. **Oscillator (Pins 5, 6)** — generates a linear sawtooth from 0.3 V to 3.0 V using external R_T and C_T; frequency $f_{osc} = \dfrac{1.1}{R_T C_T}$. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Two Error Amplifiers (Pins 1, 2, 3 and 15, 16)** — high-gain op-amps; EA1 implements voltage-mode control, EA2 implements cycle-by-cycle current limit. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Dead-Time Comparator (Pin 4)** — compares Pin 4 with the sawtooth; higher V_pin4 ⇒ larger dead time. *(1 mark)*
5. **PWM Comparator & Latch** — sets the duty cycle as the ratio of (V_pin3 or V_pin4, whichever is lower) to V_ramp. *(1 mark)*
6. **Output Stage (Pins 8–11, mode Pin 13)** — two NPN transistors in totem-pole; mode pin selects push-pull (≤ 50 % per output) or single-ended (≤ 100 %). *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Design a TL494-based oscillator for $f_{osc} = 50$ kHz. Choose $C_T = 1 \text{ nF}$ and determine $R_T$. Also calculate the required Pin-4 voltage for 40 % duty cycle in single-ended mode.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the oscillator formula:**

$$
R_T = \frac{1.1}{f_{osc} \cdot C_T} = \frac{1.1}{50 \times 10^3 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.1}{5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_T = 22 \text{ k}\Omega \;\; \text{(E12 standard value)}}
$$

*(2 marks for derivation, 1 mark for numerical evaluation)*

**Step 2 — Pin-4 voltage for 40 % duty cycle:**

$$
D = \frac{V_{pin4} - 0.3}{2.7} \times 100
$$

$$
40 = \frac{V_{pin4} - 0.3}{2.7} \times 100
$$

$$
V_{pin4} - 0.3 = 0.4 \times 2.7 = 1.08
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{pin4} = 1.38 \text{ V}}
$$

*(2 marks for setup, 1 mark for final answer)*

**Step 3 — Practical implementation:** Use a resistor divider from 5 V reference — for example, $R_1 = 18 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 7.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ yields V_pin4 = 1.38 V. *(1 mark for design completeness)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compare the Push-Pull and Single-Ended output operating modes of the TL494. Under what application conditions is each mode preferred?**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Push-Pull (Pin 13 = 0 V) | Single-Ended (Pin 13 = V_REF) |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Output transistor action | Q1 and Q2 alternate 180° out of phase | Q1 and Q2 switch simultaneously (paralleled) |
| Maximum duty per output | 48 % (built-in blanking) | 96 % (programmable) |
| Transformer utilisation | Full B-H swing (high efficiency) | Half B-H swing (lower efficiency) |
| Application | DC-AC inverters, full-bridge, half-bridge | Buck, Boost, Flyback, Forward converters |
| Drive complexity | Needs gate-drive transformer or level-shifter | Direct low-side drive |
| Power level | 100 W – 1 kW | 5 W – 200 W |

*(4 marks for table, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for typical application example)*

**Conclusion:** *Push-Pull is used whenever bidirectional flux swing in a centre-tapped transformer is required; Single-Ended is used when the power topology has only one active switch and a simple unidirectional flux path is sufficient.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the function of Pin 14 (5 V Reference) and Pin 4 (Dead-Time Control) in a TL494-based SMPS. Design a soft-start circuit using a 10 µF capacitor and a 100 kΩ resistor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pin 14 (5 V Reference):** Provides a stable internal 5 V rail with ±5 % tolerance that can source up to 10 mA. It is used to bias the oscillator, the dead-time comparator, and the error-amplifier reference networks. In practice, a 0.1 µF decoupling capacitor is placed between Pin 14 and Pin 7 (GND) to suppress noise. *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 2 — Pin 4 (Dead-Time Control):** As derived in §3.2, V_pin4 sets the maximum duty cycle. It is internally OR-wired with the EA1 output (Pin 3); the lower of the two voltages is fed to the PWM comparator. This dual-input architecture allows Pin 4 to act as a *secondary protection* — for instance, implementing soft-start by ramping V_pin4 from 0 V to its final value. *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 3 — Soft-Start Design:**
Connect a **100 kΩ resistor from V_REF (Pin 14) to Pin 4**, and a **10 µF capacitor from Pin 4 to GND**. At power-on, the capacitor is uncharged, so V_pin4 = 0 V → **maximum dead time → output OFF**. The capacitor then charges through the resistor with time constant:

$$
\tau_{ss} = R_{ss} \cdot C_{ss} = 100 \times 10^3 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6} = 1 \text{ second}
$$

After approximately **$3\tau_{ss} = 3$ seconds**, the capacitor charges to ≈ 95 % of 5 V = 4.75 V, but the dead-time comparator saturates well before this, so the output reaches its steady-state duty cycle after roughly **1.5 seconds**. *(3 marks for derivation and numerical evaluation)*

**Step 4 — Practical note:** A diode across the resistor (cathode toward Pin 4) provides a fast discharge path on power-off, resetting the soft-start for the next turn-on cycle. *(1 mark)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on TL494 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Pin 3 and Pin 4** — both feed the PWM comparator, but Pin 3 is the *error-amplifier output* (closed-loop control) while Pin 4 is the *dead-time limit* (open-loop protection). Many students write "Pin 4 controls the output voltage" — this is **wrong**. Pin 4 controls **maximum duty cycle**, not output voltage. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
> 2. **Wrong oscillator constant** — using 1/(R_T C_T) instead of 1.1/(R_T C_T). The 1.1 factor accounts for the empirical discharge behaviour. *(−1 mark per occurrence)*
> 3. **Mixing Push-Pull and Single-Ended duty-cycle limits** — applying $D = 96$ % in push-pull mode is impossible. Max is 48 % per channel in push-pull. *(−2 marks)*
> 4. **Forgetting the 5 V reference is current-limited** — students sometimes try to source 50 mA from Pin 14, exceeding its 10 mA limit. *(−1 mark in design questions)*
> 5. **Omitting the 0.1 µF decoupling capacitor on VCC and V_REF** — the IC will oscillate erratically. *(−1 mark in practical-design questions)*

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **TL494** is a fixed-frequency, voltage-mode PWM controller IC with **integrated 5 V reference, oscillator, two error amplifiers, dead-time control, and totem-pole outputs**.
- **Operating supply**: **VCC = 7 V to 40 V**; **UVLO** activates below **~6 V**.
- **Oscillator frequency**: $f_{osc} = \dfrac{1.1}{R_T \cdot C_T}$ with R_T from Pin 6 to 5 V, C_T from Pin 5 to GND.
- **Sawtooth swing**: ~0.3 V to ~3.0 V (≈ **2.7 V peak-to-peak**).
- **Pin 3** = error amplifier output (closed-loop control); **Pin 4** = dead-time / soft-start voltage (open-loop limit).
- **Pin 13** selects output mode: **GND = Push-Pull (max 48 % per output)**, **V_REF = Single-Ended (max 96 %)**.
- **Output transistors**: ±**500 mA peak** sink/source, suitable for direct MOSFET gate drive or gate-drive transformer coupling.
- **Dead-time relation**: $D = \dfrac{V_{pin4} - 0.3}{2.7} \times 100$ % (single-ended mode).
- **Typical R_T range**: 1 kΩ to 500 kΩ; **C_T range**: 470 pF to 10 µF.
- **Always include** 0.1 µF decoupling on VCC (Pin 12) and V_REF (Pin 14).
- **Soft-start** is implemented by ramping Pin 4 from 0 V with an RC network ($\tau = R \cdot C$).
- **Current limit** uses EA2 (Pins 15, 16) fed from a shunt resistor in the switch return path.
- **Push-Pull mode** is used for inverters and full-bridge converters; **Single-Ended mode** is used for buck/boost/flyback SMPS.
- **Real-world applications**: offline SMPS, DC motor drives, solar micro-inverters, UPS, battery chargers, EV battery equalisers.
- **KTU Viva Mnemonic**: **"Ref-Osc-EA-DT-Out"** — Pins 14 → 5, 6 → 1, 2, 3, 15, 16 → 4 → 8–11.
- **The 1.1 factor** in $f_{osc}$ is the most-tested numerical constant — **memorise it**.
- **Dead time is INVERSELY related** to the V_pin3 / V_pin4 control voltage — *higher* V_pin4 ⇒ *smaller* ON-time ⇒ *larger* dead time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drive Circuits for Power BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Drive Circuits for Power BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** laboratory course *PCEIL607 – Power Electronics & Control System Lab*, a **Drive Circuit (Base Drive Circuit) for a Power BJT** is defined as the auxiliary electronic interface that sits between a low-power control signal (typically from a microcontroller, PWM controller, or logic gate) and the **base terminal of a high-power Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)**, providing the precise current, voltage, and timing waveforms required to switch the BJT efficiently between the **cut-off** and **saturation** regions of its V-I characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – Module Experiment 2:**
> The lab experiment investigates how the *shape*, *magnitude*, and *duration* of the base current waveform directly govern the **switching speed**, **switching losses**, and **safe operating area (SOA)** of the power BJT. The drive circuit is *not* the load circuit — it is the *control side* circuit that "drives" the BJT.

A power BJT (e.g., BUX 48, 2N3055, TIP31, MJ15003) is fundamentally a **current-controlled device**. Unlike a power MOSFET (which is voltage-controlled), the BJT requires a substantial, sustained **base current $I_B$** to remain in saturation, and a *negative* base current pulse to rapidly extract stored minority carriers during turn-off. Without a properly engineered drive circuit, the BJT will:

- Exhibit **slow turn-off** (large storage time $t_s$)
- Operate in the **active region** (causing high dissipation)
- Suffer from **secondary breakdown** (localized thermal runaway)
- Display **non-uniform current sharing** when used in parallel

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Heavy Water Tap"

Imagine you are trying to control a **massive industrial water tap** (the BJT) that can release thousands of liters per second (collector current $I_C$ of tens of amperes). Your hand is small and weak (the logic-level control signal — maybe 5 V, 5 mA).

To turn the tap, you need a **hydraulic lever mechanism** (the drive circuit) that:
1. **Amplifies your small hand force** into a large mechanical push on the tap's valve stem — this is analogous to providing **sufficient base current $I_B$** to drive a large $I_C$.
2. **Holds the tap open firmly** even when water pressure tries to push it shut — this is like keeping the BJT **hard-saturated** with continuous base current.
3. **Quickly yanks the tap closed** when you release the handle, to prevent water hammer — this is the role of a **negative base current pulse** during turn-off to evacuate stored charge rapidly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A BJT drive circuit has *three* jobs — (1) turn-on with enough overdrive, (2) keep it ON with a holding current, (3) turn it OFF by actively *pulling out* charge, not just "letting go."

## 1.3 Why a Power BJT Needs a Special Drive Circuit

A small-signal BJT (e.g., BC547) can be driven directly from a logic gate, but a power BJT **cannot**, because:

| Property | Small-signal BJT | Power BJT |
|----------|------------------|-----------|
| Collector current $I_C$ | $\leq 100$ mA | **5 A – 500 A** |
| Forced DC current gain $\beta_f$ | 100 – 600 | **5 – 25** |
| Required base current $I_B$ | $\sim 0.5$ mA | **0.5 A – 50 A** |
| Logic gate output capability | 5 mA | **Insufficient** |
| Storage time $t_s$ | $< 1\ \mu s$ | **2 – 20 $\mu s$** |

Thus, the drive circuit must deliver **high base currents**, provide **electrical isolation** (for safety and level shifting), and actively **accelerate turn-off**.

## 1.4 Categories of BJT Drive Circuits

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module Experiment 2) classifies the drive circuits into the following families:

1. **Simple Resistive Base Drive** (continuous DC, no negative bias)
2. **Proportional Base Drive** (matched to load current)
3. **Switched (Pulsed) Base Drive** (high peak during turn-on, low holding)
4. **Anti-saturation (Baker's Clamp) Drive**
5. **Isolated Drive Circuits** — using **optocouplers** or **pulse transformers**
6. **Darlington Base Drive** (compound transistor configuration)

Each family will be examined rigorously in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating States of a Power BJT

A power BJT used as a static switch is operated in **only two** of its three possible regions:

| Region | $V_{CE}$ | $I_C$ | Base Condition | Application |
|--------|---------|-------|----------------|-------------|
| **Cut-off (OFF)** | $\approx V_{CC}$ | $\approx 0$ | $V_{BE} < 0.5$ V (reverse or zero bias) | Switch OPEN |
| **Active (Linear)** | $0.2 < V_{CE} < V_{CC}$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V | **AVOID in switching** (high loss) |
| **Saturation (ON)** | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ – $2$ V | $I_{C(sat)}$ (limited by load) | $I_B \geq I_{C(sat)} / \beta_{forced}$ | Switch CLOSED |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** During switching transitions, the BJT **must pass through the active region rapidly** (small $t_{on}$, $t_{off}$). The drive circuit's quality is judged by how *short* it makes this traversal.

## 2.2 The Concept of "Forced Beta" and "Overdrive Factor"

### Forced Beta ($\beta_f$)

When the BJT is in saturation, the collector current is no longer $\beta \cdot I_B$ — it is limited by the external load. So we define a *smaller* effective gain called the **forced beta**:

$$\beta_f = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{I_B}$$

Since the BJT is "forced" to deliver more current than the natural $\beta$ would allow at that base current, $\beta_f < \beta_{natural}$.

### Overdrive Factor (ODF)

To guarantee hard saturation under worst-case conditions (low $\beta$ at high temperature), the base is overdriven:

$$ODF = \frac{I_{B1}}{I_{B(sat)}} = \frac{\beta_{forced}}{\beta_{natural}} \cdot \text{safety margin}$$

A typical KTU-lab-recommended value is:

$$ODF = 2 \text{ to } 5$$

So $I_{B1} = (2 \text{ to } 5) \cdot I_{B(sat)}$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of a Generic Drive Circuit

The drive circuit performs **four sequential functions** every switching cycle:

1. **Turn-on phase ($t_{on} = t_d + t_{ri}$):**
   - Apply a high forward base current pulse $I_{B1} = ODF \cdot I_{B(sat)}$.
   - This charges the base-emitter diffusion capacitance rapidly, reducing **delay time $t_d$** and **rise time $t_{ri}$**.

2. **On-state (steady saturation):**
   - Reduce the base current to a small **holding value** $I_{B2} \approx I_{B(sat)}$ to minimize continuous drive-stage power loss.
   - (Optional, for ultra-low-loss designs.) This is the **switched base drive** strategy.

3. **Turn-off phase ($t_{off} = t_s + t_f$):**
   - Apply a **reverse base current** $-I_{B2}'$ (or $-I_{B1}'$) to *extract* the stored minority carriers from the base region.
   - This drastically reduces **storage time $t_s$** and **fall time $t_f$**.

4. **Off-state:**
   - Apply a small **negative bias** $V_{BE(off)} \approx -2$ to $-5$ V to keep the BJT firmly cut off, preventing accidental turn-on from $dv/dt$ transients (Miller effect equivalent for BJT).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready summary** for solving numerical problems on BJT drive circuits.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_L} \approx \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_L}$ | Saturation collector current (resistive load) | A |
| 2 | $I_{B(sat)} = \dfrac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{DC(min)}}$ | Minimum base current to just reach edge of saturation | A |
| 3 | $I_{B1} = ODF \cdot I_{B(sat)}$ | Turn-on base current with overdrive | A |
| 4 | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B1}}$ | Base series resistance calculation | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $\beta_{forced} = \dfrac{I_{C(sat)}}{I_{B1}}$ | Effective gain under overdriven saturation | — |
| 6 | $P_{drive} = V_{in} \cdot I_{B1} \cdot D$ | Continuous drive-stage power loss (D = duty cycle) | W |
| 7 | $P_{drive,pulsed} = V_{in} \cdot I_{B2} \cdot D$ | Pulsed (economical) drive loss | W |
| 8 | $t_s \approx \tau_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}'}{I_{B(sat)} + I_{B2}'}\right)$ | Storage time (with reverse bias $I_{B2}'$) | s |
| 9 | $t_f \approx \tau_B \cdot \dfrac{I_{C(sat)}}{I_{B2}'}$ | Fall time (approximately) | s |
| 10 | $P_{sw,on} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_{C(sat)} t_{ri} f_s$ | Turn-on switching loss | W |
| 11 | $P_{sw,off} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{CC} I_{C(sat)} t_f f_s$ | Turn-off switching loss | W |
| 12 | $E_{sw} = \dfrac{V_{CC} I_{C(sat)}}{2}(t_{ri} + t_f)$ | Energy lost per switching cycle | J |
| 13 | $V_{BE(sat)} \approx 0.7$ to $1.5$ V (power BJT) | Base-emitter saturation drop | V |
| 14 | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ to $2$ V (power BJT) | Collector-emitter saturation drop | V |
| 15 | $ODF = 2$ to $5$ | Recommended overdrive factor (typical) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Lab Work:**
> - $V_{in}$ (drive supply) is typically **5 V, 12 V, or 15 V** — *not* the same as $V_{CC}$.
> - $V_{CC}$ (main supply) is the high-voltage bus (e.g., **24 V, 48 V, 100 V, 200 V**).
> - Reverse base bias is typically obtained from a small auxiliary supply $V_{EE} = -2$ to $-5$ V.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

BJT drive circuits are not merely academic curiosities. They are the workhorses of:

- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** — push-pull and bridge topologies
- **DC motor drives (chopper circuits)** — historically dominant before IGBTs
- **Induction motor V/f control inverters** — pre-1990s designs
- **Electronic ballasts and UPS systems**
- **High-current solenoid drivers** (automotive fuel injectors)
- **Robotic actuator drives**

Even though **IGBTs and MOSFETs** have largely replaced BJTs in new designs above 1 kW, power BJTs (and Darlington pairs) are still preferred in:
- **Low-voltage, high-current** applications (e.g., 12 V / 200 A automotive)
- **Linear regulators** (e.g., LM317, 2N3055 pass element)
- **Audio amplifiers** (Class AB output stages)

The drive-circuit design principles studied here directly transfer to the **gate-drive design of IGBTs and power MOSFETs** — making this experiment a *foundational* one in the KTU curriculum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Design of a Simple Resistive Base Drive — Worked Example

> **Problem Statement (typical KTU lab data):**
> A power BJT (2N3055, $\beta_{DC(min)} = 20$ at $I_C = 4$ A, $V_{BE(sat)} = 1.5$ V) is used to switch a resistive load $R_L = 12\ \Omega$ connected to $V_{CC} = 48$ V. The drive signal comes from a TTL-compatible source with $V_{in} = 5$ V. Design the base resistor $R_B$ using an overdrive factor of $ODF = 3$.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Saturation Collector Current

Applying KVL around the load loop (collector–emitter–load–supply) at saturation:

$$V_{CC} = V_{CE(sat)} + I_{C(sat)} \cdot R_L$$

Taking $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1$ V (datasheet value for 2N3055 at $I_C = 4$ A):

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_L} = \frac{48 - 1}{12} = \frac{47}{12}$$

$$I_{C(sat)} = 3.917 \text{ A} \approx 3.92 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Minimum (Edge-of-Saturation) Base Current

Using the minimum guaranteed $\beta$:

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{DC(min)}} = \frac{3.917}{20}$$

$$I_{B(sat)} = 0.1958 \text{ A} \approx 196 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Overdrive Factor

To ensure the BJT is hard-saturated under all temperature and tolerance conditions, we use $ODF = 3$:

$$I_{B1} = ODF \cdot I_{B(sat)} = 3 \times 0.1958$$

$$I_{B1} = 0.5875 \text{ A} \approx 588 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the Base Resistor

Applying KVL around the input loop (drive source → $R_B$ → B-E junction):

$$V_{in} = I_{B1} \cdot R_B + V_{BE(sat)}$$

Solving for $R_B$:

$$R_B = \frac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B1}} = \frac{5 - 1.5}{0.5875}$$

$$R_B = \frac{3.5}{0.5875} = 5.957\ \Omega$$

Choosing the nearest standard E12 value: **$R_B = 5.6\ \Omega$** (or 6.8 $\Omega$ for safety margin).

### Step 5 — Verify Forced Beta

$$\beta_{forced} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{I_{B1}} = \frac{3.917}{0.5875} = 6.67$$

Since $\beta_{forced} = 6.67 < \beta_{DC(min)} = 20$, **hard saturation is guaranteed** (a margin of $20 / 6.67 \approx 3\times$).

### Step 6 — Calculate Drive-Stage Power Loss (Continuous Drive)

Assuming duty cycle $D = 0.5$ (typical chopper operation):

$$P_{drive} = V_{in} \cdot I_{B1} \cdot D = 5 \times 0.5875 \times 0.5$$

$$P_{drive} = 1.469 \text{ W}$$

This loss is dissipated in the drive source and the base resistor — a key reason why **pulsed base drives** (Section 2.3, step 2) are preferred in high-duty applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Storage Time Reduction by Reverse Base Current

The storage time is the time required to remove the *excess* minority charge stored in the base region during saturation. Let the stored base charge be $Q_B$. We have:

$$Q_B = \tau_B \cdot (I_B - I_{B(active)})$$

where $I_{B(active)} = I_C / \beta$ is the current needed to keep the BJT at the *edge* of saturation, and $\tau_B$ is the base transit time.

During turn-off, applying a reverse current $-I_{B2}'$ extracts charge at the rate:

$$\frac{dQ_B}{dt} = -I_{B2}' - \frac{Q_B}{\tau_B}$$

The solution (with initial condition $Q_B(0) = \tau_B (I_{B1} - I_{B(active)})$) gives the storage time:

$$t_s = \tau_B \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}'}{I_{B(active)} + I_{B2}'}\right)$$

**Physical interpretation:** A larger reverse current $I_{B2}'$ *shrinks* the log term toward zero, drastically reducing $t_s$. For example, doubling $I_{B2}'$ can reduce $t_s$ by a factor of **2 to 4**.

---

## 3.3 Switching Energy Loss — Derivation

During turn-on, both voltage (decreasing from $V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(sat)}$) and current (rising from 0 to $I_{C(sat)}$) are non-zero simultaneously, producing dissipation. Assuming a **linear crossover** (worst-case):

$$i_C(t) = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{t_{ri}} \cdot t \quad \text{and} \quad v_{CE}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}) \frac{t}{t_{ri}}$$

Integrating instantaneous power over the rise interval:

$$E_{on} = \int_0^{t_{ri}} v_{CE}(t) \cdot i_C(t) \, dt$$

Substituting and evaluating (neglecting $V_{CE(sat)}$ as it is small):

$$E_{on} \approx \int_0^{t_{ri}} \left(V_{CC} \cdot \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{t_{ri}^2} \cdot t^2\right) dt = V_{CC} \cdot I_{C(sat)} \cdot \frac{t_{ri}}{3}$$

Using a more standard (and KTU-accepted) rectangular approximation:
