$$\boxed{E_{sw} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{C(sat)}}{2} \left(t_{ri} + t_f\right)}$$

The switching power loss at switching frequency $f_s$ is:

$$P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot f_s$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab takeaway:** To minimize $P_{sw}$, the drive circuit must *shorten* $t_{ri}$ and $t_f$ — this is achieved by **larger $I_{B1}$** (faster turn-on) and **larger $I_{B2}'$** (faster turn-off).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Drive Circuit Topologies

This is the **single most important table** for the KTU 14-mark question on drive circuits.

| Drive Topology | Turn-on Speed | Turn-off Speed | Drive Loss | Complexity | Cost | Typical Use |
|----------------|---------------|----------------|------------|------------|------|-------------|
| **Resistive (simple DC)** | Slow | **Very slow** (no reverse bias) | High | Very low | Very low | Toy circuits, lab demo |
| **Switched (pulsed) base drive** | Fast (high $I_{B1}$) | Medium (low $I_{B2}'$) | **Low** | Medium | Low | SMPS, choppers |
| **Proportional base drive** | Medium | Medium | **Lowest** | High | Medium | High-current paralleled BJTs |
| **Baker's clamp (anti-saturation)** | Fast | Medium | Low | Medium | Low | Inductive loads (motors) |
| **Opto-isolated drive** | Fast | Fast (with -ve bias) | Low | High | Medium | Galvanic isolation needed |
| **Pulse-transformer drive** | Fast | Very fast (sharp -ve pulse) | **Very low** | High | Medium | High-side switches, half-bridge |
| **Darlington driver** | Slow (compound $\beta$) | Very slow | Medium | Low | Low | Low-$\beta$ applications |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often assume "more base current is always better." This is **false**. Excess $I_B$ increases storage time $t_s$, *slowing* turn-off, and raises drive-stage loss. The art is in choosing the *right* $I_{B1}$ and $I_{B2}$ — not just maximizing them.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Why a Baker's Clamp Reduces Storage Time

In a Baker's clamp, two diodes ($D_1$, $D_2$) are placed between the collector and the base-drive network. The collector voltage is *clamped* to:

$$V_{CE,sat,Baker} = V_{BE(sat)} + V_{D1} + V_{D2} - V_{D1(on)} \approx V_{BE(sat)} + V_{D2}$$

Typically $\approx 1.5$ to $2$ V (instead of $0.2$ V in hard saturation). Because the BJT is held *just* out of deep saturation:

- The base region has **less stored minority charge** ($Q_B \propto I_C \tau_B$ — but $I_C$ is unchanged, the *voltage margin* is what matters)
- The excess base current is **diverted through the diode** to the collector instead of building up charge

Quantitative result: storage time is reduced by **30% to 50%** compared to a deep-saturated drive, with only a modest increase in $V_{CE}$ loss (a worthwhile trade-off at high switching frequencies).

---

## 3.6 KTU Numerical — Verify Drive Design for Given Specifications

> **Worked problem:**
> Given: $V_{CC} = 100$ V, $R_L = 25\ \Omega$, $\beta_{min} = 15$, $V_{in} = 12$ V, $V_{BE(sat)} = 1.2$ V, target $ODF = 4$. Find $I_{C(sat)}$, $I_{B1}$, $R_B$, $\beta_{forced}$, $P_{drive}$ at $D = 0.6$.

**Step 1:** $I_{C(sat)} = 100 / 25 = 4$ A

**Step 2:** $I_{B(sat)} = 4 / 15 = 0.267$ A

**Step 3:** $I_{B1} = 4 \times 0.267 = 1.067$ A

**Step 4:** $R_B = (12 - 1.2) / 1.067 = 10.8 / 1.067 = 10.12\ \Omega \approx 10\ \Omega$ standard.

**Step 5:** $\beta_{forced} = 4 / 1.067 = 3.75$

**Step 6:** $P_{drive} = 12 \times 1.067 \times 0.6 = 7.68$ W

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering observation:** A 7.68 W drive loss is non-trivial — this is precisely why *switched* or *proportional* drives are used in real power converters.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete BJT Drive System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the signal flow, isolation boundary, and power flow in a typical KTU-lab BJT drive experiment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrlSrc["CONTROL SIGNAL SOURCE\nLOGIC GATE OR MCU PWM"]
    isoStage["ISOLATION STAGE\nOPTOCOUPLER OR PULSE TRANSFORMER"]
    drvAmp["DRIVE AMPLIFIER STAGE\nPUSH PULL BJT PAIR"]
    baseNet["BASE DRIVE NETWORK\nRB AND ANTI SAT DIODES"]
    powerBJT["POWER BJT\nMAIN SWITCH"]
    load["INDUCTIVE OR RESISTIVE LOAD\nRL WITH FREEWHEEL DIODE"]
    senseFB["CURRENT SENSE\nSHUNT OR CT"]
    fbPath["FEEDBACK PATH\nTO CONTROLLER"]

    ctrlSrc --> isoStage
    isoStage --> drvAmp
    drvAmp --> baseNet
    baseNet --> powerBJT
    powerBJT --> load
    senseFB -.-> load
    senseFB -.-> fbPath
    fbPath -.-> ctrlSrc
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Switched Base Drive with Negative Bias

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwmIn["PWM INPUT\n0 TO 5 VOLT"]
    bufAmp["BUFFER AND INVERTER\nLEVEL SHIFTER TO 15 V"]
    turnOnPath["TURN ON PATH\nQ1 NPN SWITCH"]
    turnOffPath["TURN OFF PATH\nQ2 PNP SWITCH"]
    vPos["POSITIVE 15 V SUPPLY\nFOR FORWARD BASE CURRENT"]
    vNeg["NEGATIVE 5 V SUPPLY\nFOR REVERSE BASE CURRENT"]
    rb1["RB1\nTURN ON RESISTOR"]
    rb2["RB2\nTURN OFF RESISTOR"]
    powerBJT2["POWER BJT Q3\nMAIN SWITCH"]
    load2["LOAD RL"]
    fwDiode["FREEWHEEL DIODE DFW"]

    pwmIn --> bufAmp
    bufAmp --> turnOnPath
    bufAmp --> turnOffPath
    vPos --> rb1 --> turnOnPath
    turnOnPath --> powerBJT2
    vNeg --> rb2 --> turnOffPath
    turnOffPath --> powerBJT2
    powerBJT2 --> load2
    load2 --> fwDiode
```

## 4.3 Anti-Saturation (Baker's Clamp) Drive Circuit — Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vd["DRIVE SUPPLY\nVDRIVE 12 V"]
    rbBaker["BASE RESISTOR RB"]
    d1["DIODE D1\nFORWARD FROM BASE TO COLLECTOR NODE"]
    d2["DIODE D2\nFORWARD FROM DRIVE SUPPLY TO COLLECTOR NODE"]
    qBJT["POWER BJT Q"]
    loadBaker["LOAD RL TO VCC"]
    clmpNode["CLAMP NODE\nVC SAT FIXED AT VBE PLUS VD2"]

    vd --> rbBaker --> qBJT
    rbBaker --> d1
    vd --> d2
    d1 --> clmpNode
    d2 --> clmpNode
    clmpNode --> qBJT
    qBJT --> loadBaker
```

## 4.4 Opto-Isolated BJT Drive — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    logicIn["LOGIC INPUT\n5 V TTL PWM"]
    ledDrv["CURRENT LIMITING RESISTOR\nFOR LED"]
    optoLed["OPTO LED\nINSIDE ISOLATOR"]
    optoTr["OPTO TRANSISTOR\nPHOTOTR RECEIVER SIDE"]
    pullup["PULL UP RESISTOR\nON RECEIVER SIDE"]
    bufStage["BUFFER AMPLIFIER\nQ4 Q5 TOTEM POLE"]
    mainBJT["MAIN POWER BJT QMAIN"]
    mainLoad["MAIN LOAD"]

    logicIn --> ledDrv --> optoLed
    optoLed -.光电耦合.-> optoTr
    optoTr --> pullup --> bufStage --> mainBJT --> mainLoad
```

## 4.5 Sequence of Operations in a Switching Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CTRL as Controller PWM
    participant DRV as Drive Circuit
    participant BJT as Power BJT
    participant LD as Load RL

    CTRL->>DRV: Send HIGH pulse at t = 0
    DRV->>BJT: Inject IB1 = 588 mA (overdrive)
    BJT->>LD: IC rises from 0 to 3.92 A in tri = 1 us
    Note over BJT,LD: Steady ON state IB reduced to IB2 = 200 mA
    CTRL->>DRV: Send LOW at t = D x T
    DRV->>BJT: Apply reverse IB2 prime = 100 mA
    BJT->>LD: IC falls to 0 in tf = 0.5 us
    Note over BJT,LD: BJT OFF, freewheel diode conducts load current
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation for lab record:**
> In Section 4.1, the *control domain* (left) is galvanically isolated from the *power domain* (right) by the **isolation stage**. The **current sense** closes a feedback loop — the foundation of any closed-loop chopper or inverter. In Section 4.5, the *sequence diagram* clearly shows the three distinct base-current magnitudes: $I_{B1}$ (overdrive), $I_{B2}$ (holding), and $I_{B2}'$ (reverse, for fast turn-off).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define "overdrive factor" for a power BJT base drive. Why is it typically chosen between 2 and 5?**

**Model Answer:**

The **overdrive factor (ODF)** of a power BJT base drive is defined as the ratio of the *actual* base current applied during turn-on to the *minimum* base current required to just reach the edge of saturation:

$$ODF = \frac{I_{B1}}{I_{B(sat)}}$$

It is typically chosen between **2 and 5** for the following engineering reasons:

1. **Temperature derating:** The current gain $\beta$ of a BJT *decreases* with rising junction temperature (negative temperature coefficient). An ODF of 2 to 5 ensures the BJT stays in saturation even at the *worst-case* high temperature (e.g., $T_j = 125°$ C).
2. **Component tolerance:** The actual $\beta$ of a manufactured BJT can be **40% to 60% below** the typical datasheet value.
3. **Storage time trade-off:** Too high an ODF (e.g., > 10) saturates the base region *excessively*, increasing the storage time $t_s$ and *slowing* turn-off — a harmful side-effect.
4. **Drive-stage loss:** Excess ODF wastes power in the base resistor and drive transistor (a 3× ODF roughly triples $P_{drive}$).

Hence, an ODF of 2 to 5 is the **engineering compromise** between *guaranteed saturation* and *acceptable drive loss / switching speed*.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any three methods to reduce the storage time $t_s$ of a power BJT. State the principle behind each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **storage time $t_s$** is the interval during which the BJT remains ON after the base signal is removed, because excess minority carriers in the base must first recombine or be extracted. Three standard methods to reduce it are:

| # | Method | Principle |
|---|--------|-----------|
| 1 | **Apply a reverse base bias ($V_{EE} = -2$ to $-5$ V)** during turn-off | Establishes a *reverse* base current $-I_{B2}'$ that **actively pulls out** the stored minority charge, accelerating the OFF transition. |
| 2 | **Reduce the overdrive factor (ODF)** during ON state | Less excess stored charge $Q_B$ accumulates in the base — there is *less* charge to remove during turn-off, so $t_s$ shrinks. |
| 3 | **Use an anti-saturation (Baker's clamp) circuit** | Keeps the BJT *just out of deep saturation* by diverting excess base current into the collector via diodes — drastically reduces stored charge. |
| 4 | **Add a speed-up capacitor** across the base resistor | Provides a brief, high-amplitude *reverse* current spike at the instant of turn-off, sweeping out stored charge. |
| 5 | **Use gold doping** in the base region (manufacturing technique) | Introduces recombination centers that shorten minority carrier lifetime $\tau_B$. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse *storage time* with *fall time*. Storage time $t_s$ is governed by **stored charge removal**; fall time $t_f$ is governed by the *discharge* of the collector depletion capacitance and recombination during active-region operation. Different mechanisms — different solutions.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **KTU Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: A power BJT (BUX 48, $\beta_{min} = 8$, $V_{BE(sat)} = 1.5$ V, $V_{CE(sat)} = 1$ V) is used as a chopper switch for a resistive load $R_L = 5\ \Omega$ connected to $V_{CC} = 100$ V. The drive signal is a 0–5 V TTL PWM at 25 kHz with duty cycle $D = 0.4$.**

**(a)** Design the base-drive circuit (calculate $I_{C(sat)}$, $I_{B(sat)}$, $I_{B1}$ for ODF = 3, base resistor $R_B$, and drive-stage loss) for a **simple resistive base drive**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Redesign the drive circuit as a **switched (pulsed) base drive** with $I_{B1} = 3 I_{B(sat)}$ for the first 10% of the ON period and $I_{B2} = 1.2 I_{B(sat)}$ for the remaining 90%. Calculate the *new* drive-stage loss and comment on the **percentage reduction** compared to part (a). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Saturation collector current** *(1 mark)*

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_L} = \frac{100 - 1}{5} = \frac{99}{5} = 19.8 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Minimum base current (edge of saturation)** *(1 mark)*

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{min}} = \frac{19.8}{8} = 2.475 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Overdriven base current** *(1 mark)*

$$I_{B1} = ODF \times I_{B(sat)} = 3 \times 2.475 = 7.425 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Base resistor** *(2 marks)*

$$R_B = \frac{V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}}{I_{B1}} = \frac{5 - 1.5}{7.425} = \frac{3.5}{7.425} = 0.4714\ \Omega$$

Choose the nearest standard: **$R_B = 0.47\ \Omega$**, **5 W rating**.

**Step 5: Drive-stage power loss** *(2 marks)*

$$P_{drive,a} = V_{in} \times I_{B1} \times D = 5 \times 7.425 \times 0.4 = 14.85 \text{ W}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Define the two-phase base currents** *(2 marks)*

- Phase 1 (first 10% of ON time, $D_1 = 0.1 \times 0.4 = 0.04$): $I_{B,on} = 3 I_{B(sat)} = 7.425$ A
- Phase 2 (remaining 90% of ON time, $D_2 = 0.9 \times 0.4 = 0.36$): $I_{B,hold} = 1.2 I_{B(sat)} = 1.2 \times 2.475 = 2.97$ A

**Step 2: Compute the average drive power over the switching period** *(3 marks)*

$$P_{drive,b} = V_{in} \times \left[ I_{B,on} \cdot D_1 + I_{B,hold} \cdot D_2 \right]$$

$$P_{drive,b} = 5 \times \left[ 7.425 \times 0.04 + 2.97 \times 0.36 \right]$$

$$P_{drive,b} = 5 \times \left[ 0.297 + 1.0692 \right] = 5 \times 1.3662 = 6.831 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Percentage reduction** *(2 marks)*

$$\Delta P\,\% = \frac{P_{drive,a} - P_{drive,b}}{P_{drive,a}} \times 100 = \frac{14.85 - 6.831}{14.85} \times 100$$

$$\Delta P\,\% = \frac{8.019}{14.85} \times 100 \approx 54\%$$

**Conclusion:** The switched base drive reduces drive-stage loss by approximately **54%** while maintaining hard saturation throughout the ON period — a substantial efficiency improvement, validating its widespread use in commercial SMPS and chopper designs.

---

### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: With the aid of neat functional block diagrams, explain the following BJT base-drive techniques. For each, state one advantage and one disadvantage:**

**(a)** **Proportional base drive circuit** — derive the relationship showing how $I_B$ tracks $I_C$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** **Opto-isolated base drive with negative bias** — describe its operation and compute the storage time improvement factor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition** *(1 mark)*: A **proportional base drive** is one in which the base current is continuously adjusted (in real time) to be *proportional* to the load current, so the BJT is always at the *edge of saturation* — never over-saturated, never under-saturated.

**Block diagram (described)** *(2 marks)*: The output (collector) current is sensed by a **current transformer (CT)** or a **sense resistor**. The sensed current is rectified and fed back to the base-drive circuit. A current-mirror or transconductance amplifier adjusts $I_B$ such that $I_B = I_C / \beta_{forced}$ with a small fixed overdrive.

**Derivation** *(3 marks)*:

Let the current-sense ratio be $k = I_{sense} / I_C$ (a constant, e.g., $k = 0.01$ for a 100:1 CT). The base drive generates:

$$I_B = \frac{I_{sense}}{k \cdot \beta_{forced}} = \frac{I_C \cdot k}{k \cdot \beta_{forced}} = \frac{I_C}{\beta_{forced}}$$

Hence $I_B$ *tracks* $I_C$ linearly with slope $1 / \beta_{forced}$. When $I_C$ falls (light load), $I_B$ falls too — preventing *excess* storage. When $I_C$ rises (heavy load), $I_B$ rises — preventing *under-saturation*.

**Advantage** *(0.5 mark)*: Minimal storage time and minimal drive loss across a wide load range.

**Disadvantage** *(0.5 mark)*: Requires a current sensor (CT or sense resistor) — adds cost, size, and complexity; the current transformer also has bandwidth limitations and may not work for DC.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Block diagram (described)** *(2 marks)*: A logic-level PWM signal drives the **LED of an optocoupler** (e.g., 4N35, PC817). The opto's phototransistor on the *secondary* side is pulled up to a local supply (e.g., +15 V) and its output drives a **totem-pole buffer amplifier** (Q1 NPN + Q2 PNP). The buffer output feeds the power BJT base through a base resistor $R_B$ during turn-on, and through a *reverse-bias network* to a negative rail ($-V_{EE}$) during turn-off.

**Operation** *(3 marks)*:

- **ON state:** Opto LED ON → phototransistor ON → buffer pulls base high → $I_{B1}$ flows from $+V_{CC,base}$ through $R_B$ into the BJT base.
- **OFF state:** Opto LED OFF → phototransistor OFF → buffer pulls base *low* to $-V_{EE}$ → reverse base current $I_{B2}'$ flows *out* of the BJT base → stored charge evacuated rapidly.
- **Isolation:** The optocoupler provides galvanic isolation of typically **5 kV** between the low-voltage control side and the high-voltage power side — essential for safety and noise immunity in chopper/inverter circuits.

**Storage time improvement** *(2 marks)*:

For the same $I_{B1}$, with no reverse bias, the storage time is:

$$t_{s,0} = \tau_B \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1}}{I_{B(active)}}\right)$$

With reverse bias current $I_{B2}' = 0.5 \times I_{B1}$:

$$t_{s,reverse} = \tau_B \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1} + 0.5 I_{B1}}{I_{B(active)} + 0.5 I_{B1}}\right) = \tau_B \ln\!\left(\frac{1.5 I_{B1}}{I_{B(active)} + 0.5 I_{B1}}\right)$$

For typical $I_{B1} = 3 I_{B(active)}$: $t_{s,reverse} \approx \tau_B \ln(1.5 \times 3 / (1 + 1.5)) = \tau_B \ln(1.8) = 0.588 \tau_B$, whereas $t_{s,0} = \tau_B \ln(3) = 1.099 \tau_B$. **Improvement factor ≈ 1.87×** — i.e., storage time reduced by nearly half.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $V_{in}$ and $V_{CC}$:** Many students plug the *main* DC bus voltage (e.g., 100 V) into the base-resistor formula. The base resistor uses the *drive* supply (5 V or 12 V), **NOT** $V_{CC}$. This single error costs up to **3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $V_{BE(sat)}$ term:** Writing $R_B = V_{in} / I_{B1}$ instead of $R_B = (V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}) / I_{B1}$ is a common slip that examiners *always* deduct for (about **1 mark**).
> 3. **Not stating the ODF used:** A bare calculation of $I_{B1}$ without justifying *why* ODF = 2 to 5 is chosen loses **conceptual marks**.
> 4. **Mixing up switching times:** $t_d$, $t_{ri}$, $t_s$, $t_f$ are *four different* intervals. Confusing them is a recurring 14-mark question killer.
> 5. **Skipping units:** Writing "$R_B = 5.6$" without "$\Omega$" or "$I_B = 588$" without "mA" loses **presentation marks**.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU lab exam and viva voce:**

- ☐ A power BJT is a **current-controlled** device — it is the only major power device that still requires a *continuous* base current to stay ON.
- ☐ **Three base currents** exist: $I_{B1}$ (overdrive, for turn-on), $I_{B2}$ (holding, during ON state), and $I_{B2}'$ (reverse, for fast turn-off).
- ☐ **Overdrive factor (ODF)** = $I_{B1} / I_{B(sat)}$ is chosen as **2 to 5** — never 1 (insufficient margin) and never > 10 (excess storage).
- ☐ **Forced beta** $\beta_f = I_{C(sat)} / I_{B1}$ must be **less than** the minimum $\beta$ for hard saturation.
- ☐ The base resistor is $R_B = (V_{in} - V_{BE(sat)}) / I_{B1}$ — using $V_{in}$ alone is **wrong**.
- ☐ **Storage time $t_s$** is the slowest of the four switching intervals and is reduced by: (1) negative base bias, (2) lower ODF, (3) Baker's clamp, (4) speed-up capacitor.
- ☐ **Anti-saturation (Baker's clamp)** trades a small rise in $V_{CE}$ loss for a large drop in storage time — preferred in high-frequency SMPS.
- ☐ **Opto-isolated drives** provide **galvanic isolation** (safety) and enable high-side driving; pulse transformers are an alternative without the LED's aging problem.
- ☐ **Darlington BJT** has compound $\beta$ (product of two $\beta$'s) but *suffers* from very high $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$ V — a critical drawback in low-voltage applications.
- ☐ Drive-stage power loss is $P_{drive} = V_{in} \cdot I_B \cdot D$ — this loss is *in addition to* the main switching/conduction losses and must be included in thermal design.
- ☐ **Switching energy loss** $E_{sw} = (V_{CC} \cdot I_{C(sat)} / 2)(t_{ri} + t_f)$ scales *linearly* with switching frequency $f_s$ — the fundamental reason why high-frequency operation demands *faster* drive circuits.
- ☐ The freewheeling diode (FWD) across an *inductive* load is **mandatory** — without it, the BJT will see a destructive $V_{CC} + I_C (di/dt)$ voltage spike at turn-off.
- ☐ Lab measurement: $V_{CE(sat)}$ is measured with the BJT fully ON, and the **base current must be measured** to verify ODF — these two measurements together validate the entire drive design.
- ☐ For the viva, expect at least one question on **"Why is a Baker's clamp called anti-saturation?"** — the answer is that the BJT is held *just at the edge* of saturation, not deeply inside it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Snubber Circuits - Shunt and Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Snubber Circuits - Shunt and Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **snubber circuit** is an auxiliary electrical network deliberately connected across (shunt) or in series with a power semiconductor switching device to **suppress transient overvoltages, limit the rate of change of voltage (dv/dt) and current (di/dt)**, and to reshape the switching trajectory of the device. Snubbers are indispensable sub-circuits in any practical power electronic converter (choppers, inverters, SMPS, motor drives) because they protect the switch from simultaneous high voltage and high current overlap that would otherwise produce destructive switching losses.

In the context of **Module Experiment 2 (PCEIL607)**, two canonical topologies are studied:
- **Shunt Snubber (RCD Turn-off / Overvoltage Snubber)** – connected across the switch.
- **Series Snubber (RCD Turn-on / di–dt Limiting Snubber)** – connected in series with the switch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A snubber is defined as a *protective circuit used to limit the transient voltage across a thyristor (or any power switch) and to limit the dv/dt and di/dt during turn-off and turn-on respectively.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of a **snubber circuit as a "shock absorber" for a power switch.**

Imagine a tap that is opened and closed rapidly (the switch) on a high-pressure water pipe (the DC bus).
- The instant you close the tap, the water hammer effect creates a violent pressure spike — this is equivalent to a **di/dt spike** that can destroy a thyristor at turn-on.
- The instant you open the tap, the collapsing magnetic field of the load inductor (the water column's momentum) generates a high reverse voltage — this is equivalent to a **dv/dt spike** at turn-off.

A **shunt snubber (capacitor + diode)** acts like a "pressure relief tank" that absorbs the reverse voltage spike by charging itself, while a **series snubber (inductor + diode)** acts like a "flow-restricting nozzle" that slows down the rate at which current rushes into the switch.

## 1.3 Why Snubbers Are Mandatory in Power Electronics

> [!NOTE]
> - Power semiconductor devices (SCR, BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, GTO) have finite **turn-off time $t_q$** and limited **dv/dt, di/dt ratings**.
> - During switching transitions, the device simultaneously carries full load current **and** supports the full supply voltage — creating a rectangular switching locus in the V-I plane with large area (i.e., huge instantaneous loss $P = v \cdot i$).
> - The snubber shapes the locus into a "Z" or "S" curve, drastically reducing the area (loss) and keeping the device inside its **SOA (Safe Operating Area)**.

Standard metrics used in KTU exam answers:

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Switching Frequency | $f_s$ | **1 kHz – 20 kHz** |
| Snubber Capacitor | $C_s$ | **0.1 µF – 2 µF** |
| Snubber Inductor | $L_s$ | **5 µH – 50 µH** |
| dv/dt Rating of SCR | — | **100 V/µs – 1000 V/µs** |
| di/dt Rating of SCR | — | **50 A/µs – 500 A/µs** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Snubbers

| Type | Connection | Function | Components |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shunt (Turn-off) Snubber** | Parallel to switch | Limits **dv/dt**, absorbs reverse overvoltage | R, C, D |
| **Series (Turn-on) Snubber** | Series with switch | Limits **di/dt**, protects against di/dt failure | R, L, D |
| **Polarity Snubber** | Across the load | Provides path for inductive load current | R, C, D |

## 2.2 Shunt (RCD Turn-off) Snubber — Working Logic

1. **Before turn-off:** Switch (SCR) is conducting. The snubber capacitor $C_s$ is discharged (or charged to a small forward drop via resistor $R_s$).
2. **At turn-off:** The load current $I_L$ cannot change instantly. It is **commutated into the snubber branch** through the diode $D_s$, charging $C_s$ linearly.
3. **The capacitor** $C_s$ **forces the voltage across the switch to rise slowly**, controlling **dv/dt**.
4. **After commutation:** $C_s$ is charged to a voltage higher than the supply (if any overshoot exists). $R_s$ discharges $C_s$ through the supply, readying it for the next cycle.
5. The diode $D_s$ **prevents $C_s$ from discharging back into the switch** during conduction (otherwise it would bypass the switch).

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy balance per cycle:** Energy stored in $C_s$ during turn-off is $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_c^2$. This energy is dissipated as heat in $R_s$, so the snubber resistor must be **wire-wound / high-pulse-rated**.

## 2.3 Series (RCD Turn-on) Snubber — Working Logic

1. **Before turn-on:** The series inductor $L_s$ is un-energized. The freewheeling diode $D_s$ (anti-parallel to the series combination, but with cathode toward the switch) carries the inductor current.
2. **At turn-on:** The supply voltage $V_s$ appears across the series combination. The inductor $L_s$ **limits the rate of rise of current** through the switch (di/dt = $V_s / L_s$).
3. **After turn-on:** Once current is established, the diode $D_s$ turns off naturally (freewheeling current has decayed through $R_s$).
4. **At turn-off:** The inductor $L_s$ tries to force current to continue — the path is provided by the diode $D_s$ and resistor $R_s$, dissipating $\tfrac{1}{2} L_s I_L^2$ as heat.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **mandatory** for any KTU 2024 numerical on snubber design. Memorize the units, the meaning of every variable, and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{I_L}{C_s}$ | dv/dt at turn-off (shunt snubber) | V/s |
| 2 | $\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{V_s}{L_s}$ | di/dt at turn-on (series snubber) | A/s |
| 3 | $C_s = \dfrac{I_L}{(dv/dt)_{max}}$ | Required shunt capacitance | F |
| 4 | $L_s = \dfrac{V_s}{(di/dt)_{max}}$ | Required series inductance | H |
| 5 | $E_c = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_{c,\max}^2$ | Energy stored in snubber capacitor per cycle | J |
| 6 | $E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L_s I_{L,\max}^2$ | Energy stored in snubber inductor per cycle | J |
| 7 | $P_c = E_c \cdot f_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_c^2 f_s$ | Power dissipation in $R_s$ | W |
| 8 | $P_L = E_L \cdot f_s = \tfrac{1}{2} L_s I_L^2 f_s$ | Power dissipation in $R_s$ (series) | W |
| 9 | $R_s = \dfrac{T}{3 C_s} = \dfrac{1}{3 R_s C_s}$ (design rule) | Discharging time constant rule (Snubber reset) | Ω |
| 10 | $R_s \cdot C_s \le \dfrac{T_{off}}{3}$ | Reset condition to ensure full discharge | s |

> **Engineering Utility:** In industrial drives (e.g., 3-phase IGBT inverter feeding a 5 HP motor), the snubber design directly affects **converter efficiency, EMI levels, and mean time between failures (MTBF)**. Incorrect snubber sizing causes **dv/dt-triggered false triggering of thyristors** in HVDC systems and **catastrophic avalanche breakdown** in IGBTs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Shunt Capacitor Value $C_s$

**Problem Setup:** A thyristor operating at load current $I_L$ must not see a voltage rise faster than the manufacturer-specified $(dv/dt)_{rated}$ during turn-off. Derive $C_s$.

**Step 1 — KCL at the switching node during commutation.**

When the SCR turns off, the load current $i_L(t)$ previously flowing through the switch must now flow through the snubber capacitor (since the switch is open). Apply KCL:

$$
i_L(t) = i_{C_s}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Capacitor current definition.**

The current through a capacitor is proportional to the rate of change of voltage across it:

$$
i_{C_s}(t) = C_s \, \dfrac{dv_{C_s}}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$
i_L(t) = C_s \, \dfrac{dv_{C_s}}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Assume worst case constant current.**

At the instant of turn-off, the load current cannot change abruptly (it is being maintained by the inductive load). For a worst-case conservative design, treat $i_L(t)$ as approximately constant at $I_L$ during the commutation interval:

$$
I_L = C_s \, \dfrac{dv_{C_s}}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $C_s$ using the rate constraint.**

Set the actual $\dfrac{dv_{C_s}}{dt}$ to be less than or equal to the device rating $(dv/dt)_{rated}$:

$$
\dfrac{dv_{C_s}}{dt} \le \left(\dfrac{dv}{dt}\right)_{rated}
$$

Substitute:

$$
C_s \ge \dfrac{I_L}{(dv/dt)_{rated}}
$$

This is the **KTU textbook formula** for snubber capacitor sizing.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Series Inductor Value $L_s$

**Problem Setup:** A thyristor is turned on across a DC bus $V_s$. Limit the di/dt to $(di/dt)_{rated}$.

**Step 1 — KVL around the loop during turn-on.**

The supply voltage $V_s$ is applied across the series combination of switch and $L_s$. KVL gives:

$$
V_s = v_{switch}(t) + L_s \, \dfrac{di_L}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume ideal switch in active region.**

During the linear current-rise interval, the switch voltage drop is small (≈ 0 for an ideal model), so:

$$
V_s \approx L_s \, \dfrac{di_L}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rate constraint.**

$$
\dfrac{di_L}{dt} \le \left(\dfrac{di}{dt}\right)_{rated}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for $L_s$.**

$$
L_s \ge \dfrac{V_s}{(di/dt)_{rated}}
$$

This is the **KTU textbook formula** for series snubber inductor sizing.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Dissipation in Shunt Resistor

**Step 1 — Energy stored in capacitor at peak voltage.**

When the SCR is OFF, the capacitor charges to a peak voltage $V_{c,\max}$. The stored energy is:

$$
E_c = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_{c,\max}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Resistor discharges the capacitor during the OFF time.**

This stored energy is fully dissipated in $R_s$ as heat (since the snubber R is the only discharge path once the diode is reverse-biased).

**Step 3 — Per cycle energy = stored energy.**

$$
E_{cycle} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_{c,\max}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by switching frequency $f_s$ to get average power.**

$$
P_{R_s} = E_{cycle} \cdot f_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_{c,\max}^2 f_s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the snubber resistor **must be a high-pulse-power rated wire-wound resistor**. A standard ¼ W carbon resistor would explode under high-frequency, high-voltage snubber service.

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **[Problem]** A thyristor feeds a DC load current of $I_L = 100 \text{ A}$. The supply is $V_s = 400 \text{ V DC}$. The device ratings are $(dv/dt)_{rated} = 200 \text{ V/µs}$ and $(di/dt)_{rated} = 100 \text{ A/µs}$. The switching frequency is $f_s = 2 \text{ kHz}$. Design the snubber.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) Shunt (turn-off) snubber:**

$$
C_s = \dfrac{I_L}{(dv/dt)_{rated}} = \dfrac{100 \text{ A}}{200 \text{ V/µs}} = \dfrac{100}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 0.5 \text{ µF}
$$

Energy stored per cycle:

$$
E_c = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_{c,\max}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times (400)^2 = 0.04 \text{ J}
$$

Power dissipated in $R_s$:

$$
P_{R_s} = E_c \cdot f_s = 0.04 \times 2000 = 80 \text{ W}
$$

Choose $R_s$ such that $R_s C_s \le T_{off} / 3$:

$$
T_{off} \approx \tfrac{1}{2 f_s} = 250 \text{ µs} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_s C_s \le 83.3 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
R_s \le \dfrac{83.3 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 166.6 \text{ Ω} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{use } R_s = 150 \text{ Ω, 100 W}
$$

**Part (b) Series (turn-on) snubber:**

$$
L_s = \dfrac{V_s}{(di/dt)_{rated}} = \dfrac{400 \text{ V}}{100 \text{ A/µs}} = \dfrac{400}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 4 \text{ µH}
$$

Energy stored per cycle:

$$
E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L_s I_L^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times (100)^2 = 0.02 \text{ J}
$$

Power dissipated in series $R_s$:

$$
P_{R_s} = E_L \cdot f_s = 0.02 \times 2000 = 40 \text{ W}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always round the chosen $C_s$ **upwards** to the next standard E12 value, and round $L_s$ **downwards** so the device is never stressed beyond its rating.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Shunt (RCD Turn-off) Snubber Placement

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a chopper circuit with a shunt snubber across the thyristor/SCR. The waveform below shows the typical capacitor voltage and switch current during turn-off.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DC Source Vs"] -->|Positive Terminal| B["Anode of Main SCR T1"]
    A -->|Negative Terminal| F["Return Path to Source"]
    B --> C["Cathode of T1"]
    C --> G["Load RL (Inductive)"]
    G --> F
    C --> D["Snubber Branch: Diode Ds (cathode at Anode of T1)"]
    D --> E["Snubber Capacitor Cs"]
    E --> R1["Snubber Resistor Rs"]
    R1 --> F
    style A fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000
    style B fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    style C fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    style G fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009933,color:#000000
    style D fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style E fill:#f2e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000
    style R1 fill:#f2e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000
```

**Node-to-Component Mapping:**

| Node | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | DC Source $V_s$ | Supplies energy to the load |
| B / C | Main SCR $T_1$ | Primary controllable switch |
| G | Load $R_L$ (inductive) | Stores energy; creates commutation problem |
| D | Diode $D_s$ | Steers load current into $C_s$ at turn-off |
| E | Capacitor $C_s$ | Limits dv/dt; absorbs inductive energy |
| R1 | Resistor $R_s$ | Dissipates stored energy; resets $C_s$ |

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Series (RCD Turn-on) Snubber Placement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DC Source Vs"] -->|Positive Terminal| L1["Series Inductor Ls"]
    L1 --> S1["Anode of Main SCR T1"]
    S1 --> S2["Cathode of T1"]
    S2 --> LOAD["Load RL Inductive"]
    LOAD --> RTRN["Return to Source Negative"]
    S2 --> D1["Freewheeling Diode Ds Anode to Cathode of T1"]
    D1 --> R1["Series Resistor Rs"]
    R1 --> L1
    style A fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000
    style L1 fill:#fff0e6,stroke:#ff6600,color:#000000
    style S1 fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    style S2 fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    style LOAD fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009933,color:#000000
    style D1 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style R1 fill:#f2e6ff,stroke:#6600cc,color:#000000
```

**Node-to-Component Mapping:**

| Node | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | DC Source $V_s$ | Provides supply |
| L1 | Inductor $L_s$ | Limits di/dt at turn-on |
| S1 / S2 | Main SCR $T_1$ | Primary switch |
| LOAD | Inductive Load $R_L$ | Current sink/source |
| D1 | Freewheeling Diode $D_s$ | Provides path for $L_s$ at turn-off |
| R1 | Resistor $R_s$ | Dissipates energy stored in $L_s$ |

## 4.3 Switching Trajectory Comparison (Conceptual)

| Without Snubber | With Shunt + Series Snubber |
|---|---|
| Rectangular locus (vertical + horizontal lines crossing at the corner) — huge overlap area, large $P = v \cdot i$ | "Z"-shaped locus — voltage and current rise/fall sequentially; minimal overlap area, low switching loss |
| Device operates near the **FBSOA / RBSOA boundary** | Device operates well inside the SOA |
| EMI emissions are high (sharp $dV/dt$, $dI/dt$) | EMI is suppressed (controlled edges) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU lab, you will **observe the SCR anode-cathode voltage and anode current waveforms on a DSO** with and without the snubber connected. The dramatic reduction in voltage spike and current rise time is the experimental evidence that validates the theory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define a snubber circuit. List the two main classifications of snubbers used in power electronic circuits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **snubber circuit** is an auxiliary protective network connected to a power semiconductor switch to limit the rate of change of voltage ($dv/dt$) and current ($di/dt$), suppress transient overvoltages, and reduce switching losses. The two main classifications are:
1. **Shunt snubber (turn-off / overvoltage snubber)** — connected in **parallel** with the switch, composed of $R$, $C$, $D$. Limits **dv/dt** at turn-off.
2. **Series snubber (turn-on / di/dt snubber)** — connected in **series** with the switch, composed of $R$, $L$, $D$. Limits **di/dt** at turn-on.

> *Mark split: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each classification (0.5+0.5 for components/function).*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Why is a freewheeling diode used in conjunction with the snubber capacitor? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The freewheeling (steering) diode $D_s$ ensures **unidirectional charging** of the snubber capacitor. During turn-off, the load current is commutated into $C_s$ through $D_s$, raising the voltage across the switch gradually. **If $D_s$ is omitted**, the capacitor would discharge back through the switch during its conduction period, effectively short-circuiting the load, dissipating huge energy in the switch, and destroying the device. The diode also prevents reverse current flow that would otherwise bypass the SCR.

> *Mark split: 1.5 marks for unidirectional charging, 1.5 marks for failure-mode explanation.*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Question Paper Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **shunt (RCD) snubber circuit** connected across an SCR. Derive the expression for the snubber capacitor value.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An SCR in a chopper circuit operates from a 230 V DC supply and carries a load current of 80 A. The device ratings are $(dv/dt)_{rated} = 500 \text{ V/µs}$ and the switching frequency is $f_s = 1 \text{ kHz}$. **Design the shunt snubber** (find $C_s$, energy stored per cycle, power dissipated in $R_s$, and choose $R_s$).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation of Shunt RCD Snubber [7 marks]:**
- **Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the circuit showing SCR $T_1$, snubber branch ($D_s$, $C_s$, $R_s$) in parallel with the SCR, and inductive load $R_L$.
- **Three operating phases (3 marks):**
  - *Phase I (SCR ON):* $C_s$ is fully discharged through $R_s$. $D_s$ is reverse-biased. Load current flows through SCR.
  - *Phase II (Turn-off):* SCR is triggered OFF. Load current commutates to $C_s$ via $D_s$. Capacitor charges linearly: $v_c(t) = (I_L / C_s) t$. The voltage across the SCR rises with controlled $dv/dt = I_L / C_s$.
  - *Phase III (Reset):* Once $v_c$ reaches supply $V_s$, the diode $D_s$ becomes reverse-biased. $C_s$ discharges through $R_s$ with time constant $\tau = R_s C_s$. For proper reset, $\tau \le T_{off} / 3$.
- **Derivation (2 marks):** As shown in Section 3.1 above,
  $$C_s = \dfrac{I_L}{(dv/dt)_{rated}}$$

**(b) Numerical Design [7 marks]:**

- $C_s = \dfrac{80}{500 \times 10^{6}} = 0.16 \text{ µF}$ → **choose $C_s = 0.22 \text{ µF}$ (next E12 value) (1 mark)**
- $E_c = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 0.22 \times 10^{-6} \times (230)^2 = 5.82 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J} = 5.82 \text{ mJ}$ **(1 mark)**
- $P_{R_s} = E_c \cdot f_s = 5.82 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 5.82 \text{ W}$ → **choose 10 W rated resistor (1 mark)**
- Reset condition: $T_{off} = 1/(2 \times 1000) = 500 \text{ µs}$. $R_s C_s \le 500/3 = 166.7 \text{ µs}$. $R_s \le \dfrac{166.7 \times 10^{-6}}{0.22 \times 10^{-6}} = 757 \text{ Ω}$. **Choose $R_s = 470 \text{ Ω}$ (1 mark)**
- (Bonus: Component selection summary table 1 mark; final 2 marks for full presentation and units.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the operation of a **series (RCD) snubber circuit**. Derive the expression for the snubber inductor $L_s$. Why is this snubber essential during the turn-on of an SCR?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thyristor feeds a 50 A load from a 415 V DC source. The device has a $(di/dt)_{rated} = 200 \text{ A/µs}$ and operates at $f_s = 500 \text{ Hz}$. **Design the series snubber** (compute $L_s$, energy stored per cycle, power loss, and choose $R_s$).

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Series Snubber Operation [7 marks]:**
- **Circuit (2 marks):** Show $V_s$ → $L_s$ → SCR → Load → back to $V_s$, with $D_s$ + $R_s$ forming a freewheeling path across the $L_s$ + SCR combination.
- **Three phases (3 marks):**
  - *Phase I (SCR OFF):* Load freewheels through $D_s$ and $R_s$.
  - *Phase II (Turn-on):* SCR is gated. The current through the SCR rises with slope $di/dt = V_s / L_s$ (linear). The inductor opposes any sudden current change.
  - *Phase III (Turn-off):* Inductor tries to maintain current. Freewheeling path through $D_s$ and $R_s$ dissipates $\tfrac{1}{2} L_s I_L^2$ as heat.
- **Derivation (2 marks):** As shown in Section 3.2,
  $$L_s = \dfrac{V_s}{(di/dt)_{rated}}$$
  At SCR turn-on, the anode current rises from zero to the load value almost instantaneously. Without $L_s$, localized hot spots form in the silicon die and the device fails within microseconds (di/dt failure).

**(b) Numerical Design [7 marks]:**
- $L_s = \dfrac{415}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 2.075 \text{ µH}$ → **choose $L_s = 2.2 \text{ µH}$ (1 mark)**
- $E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2.2 \times 10^{-6} \times (50)^2 = 2.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}$ **(1 mark)**
- $P_{R_s} = 2.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 = 1.375 \text{ W}$ → **choose 2 W resistor (1 mark)**
- $R_s$ selection: For freewheeling reset, $R_s / L_s \ge 3 f_s$ minimum decay rate. $R_s \ge 3 \times 500 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-6} = 3.3 \text{ mΩ}$. **Practically choose $R_s = 1 \text{ Ω}$ for freewheeling, rated 2 W (2 marks)**
- Final summary + units (2 marks).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the diode $D_s$:** The freewheeling / steering diode is **NOT optional**. Drawing only $R$ and $C$ (or $R$ and $L$) without $D_s$ is the single most common error — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit confusion in $C_s$ formula:** Many students write $C_s = I_L / (dv/dt)$ but forget to convert $\text{V/µs}$ to $\text{V/s}$. The numerical answer is wrong by a factor of $10^6$. Always show: "$500 \text{ V/µs} = 500 \times 10^6 \text{ V/s}$" in your solution.
> 3. **Power dissipation not calculated:** Marks are awarded for the power dissipated in $R_s$. Many students stop at $C_s$ and lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing snubber types:** Series and shunt snubbers are often interchanged in diagrams. Memorize: **Shunt = Capacitor = dv/dt = Turn-off** and **Series = Inductor = di/dt = Turn-on**.
> 5. **Not mentioning the Safe Operating Area (SOA):** KTU examiners frequently award bonus marks for stating that "the snubber keeps the device within its SOA."

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Memorize it before every lab viva and exam.**

- ☐ **Snubber = protective auxiliary circuit** for a power switch.
- ☐ Two types: **Shunt (RCD) turn-off snubber** and **Series (RCD) turn-on snubber**.
- ☐ Shunt snubber components: **Resistor $R_s$, Capacitor $C_s$, Diode $D_s$** in parallel with the switch.
- ☐ Series snubber components: **Resistor $R_s$, Inductor $L_s$, Diode $D_s$** in series with the switch.
- ☐ **Shunt** controls **dv/dt**; **Series** controls **di/dt**.
- ☐ Key formulas:
    - $C_s = I_L / (dv/dt)_{rated}$
    - $L_s = V_s / (di/dt)_{rated}$
    - $P_{R_s} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V^2 f_s$ (shunt) or $\tfrac{1}{2} L_s I^2 f_s$ (series)
- ☐ Reset rule: **$R_s C_s \le T_{off} / 3$** ensures capacitor is fully discharged before next turn-off.
- ☐ Freewheeling diode $D_s$ is **mandatory** — it prevents reverse discharge through the switch.
- ☐ Snubber resistor must be **high-pulse-power rated** (e.g., wire-wound, not carbon film).
- ☐ Always **round $C_s$ up** and $L_s$ **down** for safety margin.
- ☐ In lab, observe the SCR anode-cathode waveform on the DSO — the **voltage spike is dramatically reduced** when the snubber is connected.
- ☐ Real-world applications: choppers, inverters, SMPS, HVDC converters, induction heating, motor drives.
- ☐ Snubbers keep the device inside the **SOA (Safe Operating Area)**.
- ☐ Common exam keywords: *turn-off snubber, dv/dt protection, RC time constant, freewheeling, RCD configuration, energy dissipation, switching loss reduction.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drive Circuits for Power MOSFET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Drive Circuits for Power MOSFET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **MOSFET Driver Circuit** (also called a **Gate Drive Circuit** or **Gate Driver**) is a specialized power-electronic interface circuit whose primary function is to deliver controlled voltage and current pulses to the gate terminal of a **Power MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)** to switch it rapidly and reliably between its **cut-off (OFF)** and **ohmic/triode (ON)** regions of operation. The driver must source and sink sufficient transient gate current to charge and discharge the **input capacitance ($C_{iss}$)** of the MOSFET, while providing the necessary **galvanic isolation** between the low-power logic domain (microcontroller, PWM IC) and the high-power switching domain (DC bus, load).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Power MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled, majority-carrier device**. Unlike BJTs (which require continuous base current), the MOSFET gate is essentially **insulated** by a thin SiO₂ layer (typically 1000 Å), so it behaves like a **capacitor** that must be charged/discharged to control conduction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tap with a Spring"

Imagine a giant industrial **water valve** controlling a high-pressure pipe (the power circuit). The valve itself is heavy and sluggish.

- The **MOSFET's gate** is like the **handle** of this valve.
- The **handle is spring-loaded and oily** — you must push hard to open it (charge the gate capacitance), and pull hard to close it (discharge).
- Your hand is the **control signal** (logic 3.3 V or 5 V from a microcontroller), which is far too weak to move the heavy handle directly.
- The **drive circuit** is the **hydraulic piston** that amplifies your small hand-push into a strong, decisive movement of the valve.
- Without the piston, the valve either doesn't open fully (causing overheating) or opens too slowly (wasting energy as heat).

This is precisely why a **dedicated gate driver IC** (e.g., TLP250, IR2110, UCC27211) is mandatory in any practical power-electronic converter.

### 1.3 Why Power MOSFETs Demand Special Drive Circuits

| Property | Practical Implication |
|---|---|
| **Gate is capacitive** ($Q_g$ in nC) | Requires high transient current ($i_g = C \cdot dV/dt$) |
| **Gate threshold voltage** $V_{GS(th)}$ ≈ 2–4 V | Logic 3.3 V from MCUs is **insufficient**; needs 10–15 V |
| **Miller plateau** at $V_{GS,plat}$ | Switching speed limited by charge removal at drain transition |
| **High-side drive** in half-bridge | Source of low-side MOSFET swings; bootstrap needed |
| **$dv/dt$ induced turn-on** | False triggering if gate resistance is too high |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Condition (Datasheets):** Gate drive voltage is typically specified as **$V_{GS} = +10\,V$ (or +12 V) for full enhancement** and **$V_{GS} = 0\,V$ (or -5 V for negative bias) for turn-off**, with $R_g$ (external gate resistor) = **10 Ω** unless otherwise stated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Internal Structure of a Power MOSFET (The Three Capacitances)

Every Power MOSFET, viewed from its three terminals (G, D, S), exhibits three parasitic capacitances that fundamentally dictate the driver's job:

$$
C_{iss} = C_{GS} + C_{GD} \quad \text{(Input Capacitance, Gate-to-Source + Gate-to-Drain)}
$$

$$
C_{oss} = C_{DS} + C_{GD} \quad \text{(Output Capacitance, Drain-to-Source + Gate-to-Drain)}
$$

$$
C_{rss} = C_{GD} \quad \text{(Reverse Transfer / Miller Capacitance)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are **non-linear**; they vary strongly with $V_{DS}$. Manufacturers therefore specify **Gate Charge $Q_g$ (in nC)** instead, which integrates this non-linearity.

### 2.2 The Four Phases of MOSFET Turn-ON (Qualitative Walkthrough)

**Phase 1 — $V_{GS}$ rises from 0 to $V_{GS(th)}$ (≈ 3 V):** Gate current $i_g$ flows entirely into $C_{GS}$. The drain current $I_D$ remains ≈ 0. The MOSFET is in **cut-off**.

**Phase 2 — $V_{GS}$ rises from $V_{GS(th)}$ to Miller Plateau $V_{GS,pl}$:** The channel begins conducting. $I_D$ rises linearly to the load current $I_L$. $V_{DS}$ is still ≈ $V_{DD}$.

**Phase 3 — Miller Plateau (flat $V_{GS} = V_{GS,pl}$):** All gate current now flows into $C_{GD}$ (the Miller capacitor). $V_{DS}$ collapses rapidly from $V_{DD}$ to $R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_L$. This is the **slowest and most power-consuming phase**.

**Phase 4 — $V_{GS}$ rises from $V_{GS,pl}$ to final $+V_{drive}$ (e.g., +12 V):** $C_{GS}$ and $C_{GD}$ are fully charged. The MOSFET is fully enhanced with $R_{DS(on)}$ at its minimum.

> The same four phases occur in **reverse** during turn-OFF. The driver must deliver **peak current** $I_{g,peak}$ during Phase 3.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak Gate Current | $I_{g,peak} = \dfrac{V_{drive} - V_{GS,pl}}{R_g}$ | A | Determines switching speed |
| 2 | Turn-ON Time (approx) | $t_{on} \approx \dfrac{Q_{gd}}{I_{g,peak}}$ | s | $Q_{gd}$ = Miller charge |
| 3 | Average Gate Drive Power | $P_{drive} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$ | W | Per MOSFET, per switching cycle |
| 4 | Total Gate Charge | $Q_{g,total} = Q_{gs} + Q_{gd} + Q_{g(over)}$ | nC | From datasheet (at given $V_{DS}, I_D$) |
| 5 | Energy Stored in $C_{iss}$ | $E_g = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{iss} V_{GS}^2$ | J | Energy dissipated in $R_g$ per transition |
| 6 | Switching Loss (per cycle) | $E_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off})$ | J | Linear current/voltage overlap model |
| 7 | Minimum $R_{g(on)}$ for $dv/dt$ immunity | $R_{g,min} \approx \dfrac{V_{GS(th),min}}{C_{GD} \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}}$ | Ω | Prevents spurious turn-on |
| 8 | Bootstrap Capacitor | $C_{boot} \geq \dfrac{Q_g + I_{qbs} \cdot t_{on,max}}{\Delta V_{boot}}$ | F | For high-side N-MOSFET in half-bridge |
| 9 | Effective Drain-Source Cap | $C_{o(er)} = \dfrac{1}{V_{DS}} \int_0^{V_{DS}} C_{oss}(v)\,dv$ | F | Energy-based equivalent for $E_{oss}$ |
| 10 | Output Capacitor Stored Energy | $E_{oss} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{o(er)} V_{DS}^2$ | J | Lost during turn-ON in ZVS analysis |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Inverters / SMPS:** Every high-frequency converter (50 kHz – 1 MHz) depends on fast gate drives to minimize switching loss.
- **Motor Drives (VFD):** Half-bridge inverters require **high-side + low-side synchronized drives** with dead-time control.
- **EV Traction Inverters:** SiC/GaN MOSFETs demand **+18 V / -3 V** drives with sub-10 ns propagation delay.
- **Class-D Audio Amplifiers:** Pure switching operation requires minimal crossover distortion, governed by gate drive symmetry.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Gate Current Through External Gate Resistor

We model the driver as an **ideal voltage source $V_{drive}$** connected to the gate through external resistor $R_g$, with $C_{iss}$ as the load capacitance.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the gate loop:**

$$
V_{drive} = i_g(t) \cdot R_g + V_{GS}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Express $i_g$ as the capacitor charging current:**

$$
i_g(t) = C_{iss} \cdot \frac{dV_{GS}(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the KVL equation:**

$$
V_{drive} = R_g \cdot C_{iss} \cdot \frac{dV_{GS}}{dt} + V_{GS}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize this is a first-order linear ODE with time constant $\tau = R_g C_{iss}$:**

$$
\frac{dV_{GS}}{dt} + \frac{V_{GS}}{R_g C_{iss}} = \frac{V_{drive}}{R_g C_{iss}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve with initial condition $V_{GS}(0) = 0$:**

$$
V_{GS}(t) = V_{drive}\left(1 - e^{-t / R_g C_{iss}}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate to find the instantaneous gate current:**

$$
i_g(t) = C_{iss} \cdot \frac{dV_{GS}}{dt} = \frac{V_{drive}}{R_g} \cdot e^{-t / R_g C_{iss}}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify the peak current, which occurs at $t = 0$:**

$$
\boxed{I_{g,peak} = \frac{V_{drive}}{R_g}}
$$

> During the Miller plateau, $V_{GS}$ is pinned at $V_{GS,pl}$, so the current driving $C_{GD}$ alone is:
> $I_{g,plat} = \dfrac{V_{drive} - V_{GS,pl}}{R_g}$ — this is what limits the $V_{DS}$ fall time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Gate Drive Power Dissipation

**Step 1 — Each switching cycle transfers a fixed charge $Q_g$ at voltage $V_{GS}$:**

Energy per cycle = $Q_g \cdot V_{GS}$ (this is the work done by the driver source).

**Step 2 — Half of this energy is stored in $C_{iss}$, half is dissipated in $R_g$** during charging:

$$
E_{charge} = \frac{1}{2} Q_g V_{GS} \quad \text{(stored in capacitor)}
$$

$$
E_{R_g,charge} = \frac{1}{2} Q_g V_{GS} \quad \text{(dissipated in } R_g \text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — The same amount of energy is dissipated during the discharge phase** (also in $R_g$):

$$
E_{R_g,discharge} = \frac{1}{2} Q_g V_{GS}
$$

**Step 4 — Total energy per full switching cycle (turn-on + turn-off):**

$$
E_{total} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by switching frequency $f_{sw}$ to obtain average power:**

$$
\boxed{P_{drive} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** For IRF540N, $Q_g = 71$ nC (typ), $V_{GS} = 12$ V, $f_{sw} = 50$ kHz.
> $P_{drive} = 71 \times 10^{-9} \times 12 \times 50 \times 10^3 = 42.6$ mW — negligible, but the **peak** current requirement is what stresses the driver.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Turn-ON Switching Loss (Linear Approximation)

During Phase 3 (Miller plateau), $V_{DS}$ falls linearly from $V_{DD}$ to $R_{DS(on)} I_L$, while $I_D$ stays approximately constant at the load current $I_L$.

**Step 1 — Linear voltage transition:**

$$
V_{DS}(t) = V_{DD} \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{fv}}\right), \quad 0 \le t \le t_{fv}
$$

where $t_{fv}$ is the **fall time** (Miller region duration).

**Step 2 — Instantaneous power dissipated in the MOSFET:**

$$
p(t) = V_{DS}(t) \cdot I_D = V_{DD} \cdot I_D \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{fv}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Energy during turn-ON voltage fall:**

$$
E_{on} = \int_0^{t_{fv}} p(t)\,dt = V_{DD} \cdot I_D \int_0^{t_{fv}}\left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{fv}}\right)dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
\int_0^{t_{fv}}\left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{fv}}\right)dt = \left[t - \frac{t^2}{2 t_{fv}}\right]_0^{t_{fv}} = t_{fv} - \frac{t_{fv}}{2} = \frac{t_{fv}}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Final turn-ON loss per cycle:**

$$
\boxed{E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DD} \cdot I_D \cdot t_{fv}}
$$

**Step 6 — By symmetric reasoning, turn-OFF loss:**

$$
\boxed{E_{off} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DD} \cdot I_D \cdot t_{rv}}
$$

**Step 7 — Total switching power loss:**

$$
\boxed{P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DD} \cdot I_D \cdot (t_{fv} + t_{rv}) \cdot f_{sw}}
$$

> **Design Insight:** Halving $t_{fv}$ and $t_{rv}$ (by halving $R_g$ or increasing driver current) **linearly halves** the switching loss — the central engineering trade-off of MOSFET drive design.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Drive Circuit Topology Comparison

| Topology | Isolation | Level Shifting | Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Non-isolated totem-pole)** | None | Not needed | ₹ Low | Low-side switch, common-ground load |
| **Push-Pull (BJT Totem-Pole)** | None | Not needed | ₹ Low | Discrete lab driver (e.g., 2N2222 + 2N2907) |
| **Bootstrapped Half-Bridge (IR2110)** | Only low-side logic | High-side via bootstrap diode + cap | ₹ Medium | Half-bridge VFD, synchronous buck |
| **Opto-Isolated (TLP250, HCPL-3120)** | ✅ 2.5 – 5 kV | Built-in | ₹ Medium | High-side drive, safety isolation |
| **Pulse Transformer** | ✅ Galvanic | Inherent | ₹ Medium-High | High-frequency, low duty cycle |
| **Gate-Drive Transformer (GDT)** | ✅ Galvanic | Inherent | ₹ Medium | SiC/GaN half-bridge with negative bias |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Complete MOSFET Drive Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[PWM MCU 3.3V] -->|HIN, LIN| LC[Logic Conditioning AND RC Filter]
    LC -->|PWM_Pulse| ISO[Isolation Barrier Optocoupler TLP250]
    ISO -->|Differential Drive Signal| TP[Totem Pole Driver BJT Pair]
    TP -->|Boosted Vgs 0 to 12V| RG[Gate Resistor Rg 10 to 100 ohm]
    RG -->|Drive Pulse| GMOSFET[Power MOSFET Gate]
    GMOSFET -->|Drain Current Id| LOAD[Inductive Load]
    BOOT[Bootstrap Capacitor 1uF] -->|Floating Supply| TP
    VCC[12V Isolated DC DC] --> TP
    GND[Isolated Ground] --> TP
```

### 4.2 Sequence of Events During a Switching Cycle (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF_State
    OFF_State --> Phase1_Charging: Driver sources 12V
    Phase1_Charging --> Phase2_CurrentRise: Vgs crosses Vth
    Phase2_CurrentRise --> Phase3_MillerPlateau: Id reaches IL
    Phase3_MillerPlateau --> Phase4_FullEnhancement: Vds collapses to Rdson x IL
    Phase4_FullEnhancement --> ON_State
    ON_State --> Phase4b_Discharging: Driver pulls gate to 0V
    Phase4b_Discharging --> Phase3b_MillerFall: Vgs falls to Vpl
    Phase3b_MillerFall --> Phase2b_CurrentFall: Vds rises to VDD
    Phase2b_CurrentFall --> Phase1b_Discharging: Vgs falls below Vth
    Phase1b_Discharging --> OFF_State
    OFF_State --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Discrete Totem-Pole Driver Circuit (Lab Implementation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VIN[PWM Input from MCU 0 to 5V] --> R1[R1 1k]
    R1 --> Q1BASE[Base of Q1 NPN BC547]
    Q1COL[Collector of Q1] --> Q2BASE[Base of Q2 PNP BC557]
    Q1EMIT[Emitter of Q1] --> GND[GND]
    VCC12[+12V Drive Rail] --> Q2EMIT[Emitter of Q2]
    Q2COL[Collector of Q2] --> RgOUT[Gate Resistor Rg 47 ohm]
    RgOUT --> GATE[MOSFET Gate]
    Q1COL --> Q2BASE
    D1[Anti-parallel Diode] -->|Reverse across Q1 B-E| Q1BASE
```

### 4.4 Half-Bridge with Bootstrap Driver (IR2110 Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HIN[HIN High-side Input] --> HLEV[Level Shifter 500V]
    LIN[LIN Low-side Input] --> LDVR[Low-side Driver]
    HLEV --> HDRV[High-side Driver]
    LDVR --> HO_L[LO Output]
    HDRV --> HO_H[HO Output]
    VCC[15V Vcc] --> LDVR
    VB[Bootstrap 15V] --> HDRV
    DBOOT[Bootstrap Diode] --> VB
    CBOOT[Bootstrap Cap 1uF] --> VB
    VS[VS Source of High-side FET] --> CBOOT
    HO_H --> QG_HIGH[Gate of High-side MOSFET]
    HO_L --> QG_LOW[Gate of Low-side MOSFET]
```

### 4.5 Gate Drive Timing Diagram (Conceptual Waveform)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as MCU PWM
    participant DRV as Gate Driver
    participant G as MOSFET Gate
    participant D as MOSFET Drain
    Note over MCU,G: t = 0 — Driver commanded ON
    MCU->>DRV: Logic HIGH (3.3V)
    DRV->>G: Source 12V through Rg
    Note over G: Phase 1-2: Vgs rises 0 -> Vpl
    Note over D: Drain voltage still = Vdd
    Note over G: Phase 3: Miller plateau
    G->>D: Vds collapses Vdd -> Rdson x Id
    Note over G: Phase 4: Vgs reaches 12V
    Note over D: Fully ON
    Note over MCU,D: After dead time, complementary cycle repeats
```
