## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 Lab]**

#### **Q1. [3 Marks]** Why is a dedicated gate driver circuit required to switch a Power MOSFET? Why can't a microcontroller GPIO pin drive it directly?

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step, 3-mark weightage):**

A Power MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled device** whose gate is isolated by a thin SiO₂ layer, making it purely capacitive ($C_{iss}$ of a few nF). A typical microcontroller GPIO can source/sink only **4 – 20 mA** at 3.3 V, which is insufficient for three reasons:

1. **Voltage Insufficiency:** The gate threshold $V_{GS(th)}$ is typically 2 – 4 V; logic 3.3 V is barely above threshold and gives high $R_{DS(on)}$, causing excessive conduction loss. A $+10$ V to $+12$ V drive is required for full enhancement.

2. **Current Insufficiency:** Switching times $t_{on}$ and $t_{off}$ depend on charging the gate charge $Q_g$ (tens of nC) in nanoseconds. The peak gate current needed is $I_{g,peak} = V_{drive}/R_g$. For $V_{drive}=12$ V and $R_g = 10\,\Omega$, this is **1.2 A** — well beyond MCU capability.

3. **Reference (Ground) Issues:** In half-bridge topologies, the source of the high-side MOSFET **swings** with the phase node; a fixed-reference GPIO cannot drive it without level shifting or isolation.

Hence, a dedicated **gate driver IC** is mandatory to provide voltage amplification, current amplification, level shifting, and isolation.

> **Examiner's Note:** Award 1 mark for each of the three distinct reasons. Most students only mention "current" and miss voltage/ground issues.

---

#### **Q2. [3 Marks]** Define the **Miller plateau** in a Power MOSFET turn-ON waveform. What is its significance for drive circuit design?

**Model Answer:**

During turn-ON, when the drain current has reached the load current $I_L$, the drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS}$ begins to fall rapidly. Because $C_{GD}$ (the Miller capacitance) couples this large $dV_{DS}/dt$ back to the gate, all the gate drive current is momentarily diverted into $C_{GD}$ to discharge it. As a result, the gate voltage $V_{GS}$ is **pinned at a constant value** $V_{GS,pl}$ (typically 5 – 7 V) for a finite duration $t_{pl}$.

**Significance:**

- This duration $t_{pl}$ is the **slowest portion** of switching and dominates switching loss.
- The drive circuit must deliver **$I_{g,pl} = (V_{drive} - V_{GS,pl})/R_g$** through $R_g$ to discharge $C_{GD}$ quickly.
- Choosing a **smaller $R_g$** or a **driver with higher peak current** directly shortens the Miller plateau and reduces $E_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off})$.
- The Miller plateau also defines the **minimum dead-time** required in complementary half-bridge drives to prevent shoot-through.

> **Examiner's Note:** Students often confuse the Miller plateau with the $V_{GS(th)}$ region. Be precise: it is the **second, flat region** of $V_{GS}$ after the channel has conducted full load current.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **discrete totem-pole BJT gate driver** for a Power MOSFET. Show the conduction path during turn-ON and turn-OFF, and derive the expression for the average gate drive power.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **bootstrap gate driver circuit** for driving the high-side N-channel MOSFET in a half-bridge inverter. Specify the bootstrap capacitor value for $Q_g = 50$ nC, switching frequency $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz, maximum ON-time of high-side switch $t_{on,max} = 25\,\mu s$, quiescent current of the driver $I_{qbs} = 100\,\mu A$, and allowable bootstrap voltage drop $\Delta V_{boot} = 0.5$ V. Justify the choice of bootstrap diode.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

A totem-pole driver uses **one NPN (Q1: BC547) and one PNP (Q2: BC557)** in a complementary emitter-follower configuration, both supplied from a $\pm 12$ V rail (or 0 / +12 V).

**Turn-ON path:** When PWM input = HIGH, Q1 turns ON, pulling its collector low. This pulls Q2's base low, turning Q2 ON. Current flows from +12 V → Q2 (emitter-follower) → $R_g$ → MOSFET gate → $C_{iss}$ → ground. Gate charges to ~+11.4 V (one $V_{BE}$ drop below 12 V).

**Turn-OFF path:** When PWM input = LOW, Q1 turns OFF, Q2's base is pulled high through $R_1$ to +12 V, turning Q2 OFF. Simultaneously, a path through $R_2$ (or alternatively, a complementary NPN on the low side) pulls the gate down through Q1 or a pull-down resistor to discharge $C_{iss}$ to ground.

**Derivation of average gate drive power:**

Energy per switching cycle = $Q_g \cdot V_{GS}$ (work done by 12 V source transferring charge $Q_g$).

This energy is split: half stored in $C_{iss}$, half dissipated in $R_g$ during charge; the same amount is dissipated in $R_g$ during discharge.

$$
P_{drive} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}
$$

For $Q_g = 50$ nC, $V_{GS} = 12$ V, $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz:

$$
P_{drive} = 50 \times 10^{-9} \times 12 \times 20 \times 10^3 = 12 \text{ mW}
$$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

In a half-bridge, the high-side N-MOSFET's source is not at ground; it sits at the switching node and swings from 0 to $V_{DC}$. To keep $V_{GS}$ referenced to this swinging source, we need a **floating supply** referenced to VS. The **bootstrap technique** creates this floating supply.

**Bootstrap operation:**

1. When the low-side MOSFET is ON (high-side OFF), the switching node VS is pulled to ground. The bootstrap capacitor $C_{boot}$ is charged from the +15 V supply through diode $D_{boot}$ to $\approx V_{CC} - V_F = 14.3$ V.
2. When the low-side turns OFF and high-side turns ON, VS swings up to $V_{DC}$. The bootstrap capacitor rides with VS, supplying the high-side driver's $V_{BS}$ rail (now $\approx 14.3$ V above VS).
3. $C_{boot}$ discharges only by the gate charge $Q_g$ and the driver's quiescent current $I_{qbs}$ during the high-side ON-time.

**Bootstrap capacitor design:**

$$
C_{boot} \geq \frac{Q_g + I_{qbs} \cdot t_{on,max}}{\Delta V_{boot}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
C_{boot} \geq \frac{50 \times 10^{-9} + (100 \times 10^{-6})(25 \times 10^{-6})}{0.5}
$$

$$
C_{boot} \geq \frac{50 \times 10^{-9} + 2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5} = \frac{52.5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5} = 105 \text{ nF}
$$

**Selected standard value: $C_{boot} = 1\,\mu F$ (ceramic, X7R, rated 25 V)** — chosen at least 10× the calculated minimum to provide safety margin against temperature, voltage derating, and aging.

**Bootstrap diode selection:** A **fast-recovery diode** (e.g., UF4007) or **Schottky** (e.g., MBRS140) with reverse voltage rating $> V_{DC,max}$, forward current $> I_{charge,peak}$, and **reverse recovery time $t_{rr} < 50$ ns** is chosen. The diode must block the full DC bus voltage when the high-side switch is ON; a 1 A / 200 V ultrafast diode is typically adequate for low-power lab inverters.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four phases of **Power MOSFET turn-ON** with a $V_{GS}$ vs. time waveform. Identify which phase contributes the most to switching loss and justify the role of the gate resistor $R_g$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An IRF540N Power MOSFET is driven by a gate driver with $V_{drive} = 12$ V through $R_g = 22\,\Omega$. The total gate charge is $Q_g = 71$ nC (typical) and the Miller plateau charge is $Q_{gd} = 14$ nC. Compute:
- (i) The peak gate current at turn-ON.
- (ii) The approximate Miller plateau duration.
- (iii) The average gate drive power at $f_{sw} = 100$ kHz.
- (iv) The approximate turn-ON switching energy assuming $V_{DS} = 100$ V, $I_D = 5$ A.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Phase 1 (0 → $V_{GS(th)}$):** The driver sources current into $C_{GS}$. The drain current is zero because the channel has not formed. $V_{GS}$ rises exponentially with time constant $\tau = R_g (C_{GS} + C_{GD})$.

**Phase 2 ($V_{GS(th)}$ → $V_{GS,pl}$):** The channel begins to conduct. $I_D$ rises linearly with $V_{GS}$ in the saturation region, reaching the load current $I_L$. $V_{DS}$ remains essentially at $V_{DD}$ because the MOSFET is in saturation.

**Phase 3 (Miller Plateau, $V_{GS}$ = $V_{GS,pl}$):** This is the **most loss-contributing phase**. The drain voltage $V_{DS}$ falls rapidly from $V_{DD}$ to $R_{DS(on)} I_L$. The driver current is now entirely consumed in discharging the Miller capacitor $C_{GD}$. $V_{GS}$ is held flat — this is why the phase is called the **plateau**.

**Phase 4 ($V_{GS,pl}$ → $V_{drive}$):** $C_{GS}$ and $C_{GD}$ are fully charged, $V_{GS}$ climbs to the rail. The MOSFET is fully enhanced at its minimum $R_{DS(on)}$.

**Role of $R_g$:** A small $R_g$ gives high peak current, faster Miller transition, lower $E_{sw}$, but increased EMI, ringing, and $dv/dt$-induced false turn-on of the complementary switch. A large $R_g$ is the opposite. $R_g$ is therefore the **primary design knob** balancing efficiency vs. EMI.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**(i) Peak gate current:**

$$
I_{g,peak} = \frac{V_{drive}}{R_g} = \frac{12}{22} = 0.545 \text{ A}
$$

**(ii) Miller plateau duration:**

$$
t_{pl} \approx \frac{Q_{gd}}{I_{g,pl}} \approx \frac{Q_{gd}}{I_{g,peak}} = \frac{14 \times 10^{-9}}{0.545} = 25.7 \text{ ns}
$$

**(iii) Average gate drive power:**

$$
P_{drive} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw} = 71 \times 10^{-9} \times 12 \times 100 \times 10^3 = 85.2 \text{ mW}
$$

**(iv) Turn-ON switching energy:**

Using the linear overlap model with $t_{fv} \approx t_{pl} = 25.7$ ns:

$$
E_{on} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D \cdot t_{fv} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 5 \times 25.7 \times 10^{-9} = 6.43 \text{ µJ}
$$

Total switching loss (assuming symmetric turn-OFF):

$$
P_{sw} = 2 \times E_{on} \times f_{sw} = 2 \times 6.43 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^3 = 1.286 \text{ W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units trap (–1 mark):** $Q_g$ is in **nC**, not C. Failing to convert gives a power value 9 orders of magnitude wrong.
> 2. **Symmetry assumption:** Some students compute only $E_{on}$ and forget to double it for $E_{off}$ in total switching loss. (–1 mark)
> 3. **$V_{GS}$ in $P_{drive}$:** Use **$V_{drive}$** (the actual drive rail), not $V_{GS(th)}$. (–0.5 mark)
> 4. **$R_g$ location:** The gate resistor is **in series with the gate lead**, not in the drain circuit. Some students place it incorrectly in schematics. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Bootstrap diode reverse recovery:** A slow diode (e.g., 1N4007) used in a high-frequency bootstrap will **lose charge** through reverse recovery and cause the high-side drive to droop. Always specify **fast recovery / Schottky**. (–1 mark in design questions)
> 6. **Dead-time omission:** A common error in half-bridge designs is forgetting to insert a small dead time (typically 100 – 500 ns) between complementary gate signals to prevent **shoot-through** — a catastrophic cross-conduction that destroys both MOSFETs in microseconds.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Drive Circuits for Power MOSFET**

- ⚡ **MOSFET = Voltage-Controlled Capacitive Device.** Gate is insulated; $V_{GS}$ controls $R_{DS(on)}$; no continuous gate current needed in steady state.
- ⚡ **Three Internal Capacitances:** $C_{iss}$, $C_{oss}$, $C_{rss}$ — non-linear with $V_{DS}$. Use $Q_g$ (gate charge) for design, not capacitance.
- ⚡ **Four Phases of Turn-ON:** (1) 0→$V_{th}$, (2) $V_{th}$→$V_{pl}$, (3) **Miller plateau** (slowest, most loss), (4) $V_{pl}$→$V_{drive}$.
- ⚡ **Peak Gate Current Formula:** $I_{g,peak} = V_{drive}/R_g$. Smaller $R_g$ → faster switching → lower $E_{sw}$ → more EMI.
- ⚡ **Average Gate Drive Power:** $P_{drive} = Q_g \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$ (typically <100 mW).
- ⚡ **Switching Loss Formula:** $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$. Linear overlap model is sufficient for KTU lab level.
- ⚡ **Drive Voltage Standard:** +12 V (or +10 V) for full enhancement; 0 V (or **–5 V** for noisy environments) for turn-off.
- ⚡ **Drive Current Capability:** Must exceed $V_{drive}/R_g$ for the chosen $R_g$. Typical gate driver ICs source/sink 1 – 4 A peak.
- ⚡ **Totem-Pole (Discrete):** PNP + NPN pair. Used in lab, breadboard, low-frequency applications.
- ⚡ **Bootstrap High-Side Drive:** $C_{boot}$ charges when low-side is ON, floats with VS, supplies high-side driver. $C_{boot} \geq 10 \times$ calculated minimum for safety.
- ⚡ **Bootstrap Diode:** Must be **fast-recovery / Schottky**, rated $> V_{DC,max}$, $t_{rr} < 50$ ns.
- ⚡ **Isolation Methods:** Optocoupler (TLP250, HCPL-3120), pulse transformer, gate-drive transformer, digital isolator (Si8271, ADuM4121).
- ⚡ **Dead-Time:** 100 – 500 ns blanking period between complementary gate signals prevents shoot-through.
- ⚡ **$dv/dt$ Induced Turn-On:** $R_g$ must be large enough to prevent the Miller current $C_{GD} \cdot dV_{DS}/dt$ from charging $C_{GS}$ above $V_{th}$.
- ⚡ **Negative Gate Bias:** Used in bridge circuits (–3 V to –5 V) to harden against $dv/dt$ false triggering.
- ⚡ **Lab Identification of Components:** In KTU PCEIL607 lab, students typically use **IRF540N** (or IRF3205) Power MOSFETs, **TLP250 / IR2110** driver ICs, and a **MOSFET driver module** to study switching waveforms on a DSO.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Three-Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier** (also called a **6-Pulse Uncontrolled Rectifier** or **Graetz Bridge**) is a power electronic converter topology that uses **six uncontrolled power diodes** arranged in a three-phase bridge configuration to convert a balanced three-phase AC supply into a controlled-magnitude DC output. The output voltage magnitude is fixed by the supply voltage and transformer turns ratio since diodes are passive switching elements with no gate control. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A 6-pulse diode bridge rectifier produces an output with **6 pulses per fundamental cycle** of the supply frequency. With a standard **50 Hz** supply, the fundamental ripple frequency at the DC output is **6 × 50 = 300 Hz**, making it inherently smoother than single-phase or three-phase half-wave rectifiers. ### Circuit Topology Summary | Parameter | Specification | |---|---| | Number of Diodes | **6** (D1–D6) | | AC Supply Phases | **3** (R, Y, B) | | Output Pulses per Cycle | **6** | | Most Conductive Diode Pair | One from +ve group, one from –ve group | | Each Diode Conduction Angle | **120° (2π/3 rad)** | | Number of Diodes Conducting Simultaneously | **2** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 6 Pulses?** > Each phase of the 3-phase supply feeds two diodes (one for positive half-cycle to the +DC rail, one for negative half-cycle to the –DC rail). Across one full 360° cycle, **6 commutation events** occur — this is why it is called a **6-pulse rectifier**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Water Wheel" Imagine a three-river confluence (R, Y, B rivers) feeding a common water tank. The water tank is your DC load. You have **six sluice gates** (the six diodes) installed in the channels: - **Three "uphill" gates (D1, D3, D5)** at the top that connect the highest-pressure river to the tank's top inlet. - **Three "downhill" gates (D2, D4, D6)** at the bottom that connect the lowest-pressure river to the tank's bottom outlet. At any instant, the **river with the highest water level** automatically opens its top gate (its diode becomes forward-biased), while the **river with the lowest water level** automatically opens its bottom gate. The tank's water level (DC output voltage) is always the difference between the highest and lowest rivers' levels — never the average. This is exactly how a 3-phase diode bridge behaves electrically: > **Highest phase voltage** → feeds the **+DC terminal** > **Lowest phase voltage** → feeds the **–DC terminal** --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Constants (MUST MEMORIZE):** > - Line-to-line RMS voltage: $V_L$ (volts) > - Phase RMS voltage: $V_{ph} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ > - Peak phase voltage: $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{ph} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2} \cdot V_L}{\sqrt{3}}$ > - Peak line-to-line voltage: $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m = \sqrt{6} \cdot V_{ph}$ > - Output ripple fundamental frequency: $f_r = 6f$ where $f$ is supply frequency > - Supply frequency (India): $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ → Ripple = **300 Hz** # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Phase-by-Phase Analysis A balanced 3-phase supply has three sinusoidal voltages displaced by 120°: $$V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$V_Y = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$ $$V_B = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ ### Conduction Logic (The "Highest & Lowest" Rule) > The diode that connects the **most positive phase** to the **+DC rail** is forward-biased and conducts. > The diode that connects the **most negative phase** to the **–DC rail** is forward-biased and conducts. > All other 4 diodes are reverse-biased and OFF. ### Conduction Sequence (KTU High-Yield Table) | Interval | Conduction Duration | Conducting Diodes | Phase Feeding +DC | Phase Feeding –DC | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0° to 60° | **D1, D6** | R (most +ve) | B (most –ve) | | 2 | 60° to 120° | **D1, D2** | R (most +ve) | Y (most –ve) | | 3 | 120° to 180° | **D3, D2** | Y (most +ve) | Y (most –ve) — wait, **D3, D4** | | 4 | 180° to 240° | **D3, D4** | Y (most +ve) | B (most –ve) | | 5 | 240° to 300° | **D5, D4** | B (most +ve) | B (most –ve) — wait, **D5, D6** | | 6 | 300° to 360° | **D5, D6** | B (most +ve) | R (most –ve) | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for the conducting pair sequence:** > **D1–D6 → D1–D2 → D3–D2 → D3–D4 → D5–D4 → D5–D6 → D1–D6 (repeats)** > The odd-numbered diodes (D1, D3, D5) feed the +DC rail in cyclic order. > The even-numbered diodes (D2, D4, D6) feed the –DC rail in cyclic order. ### Why Each Diode Conducts for 120° - D1 is forward-biased whenever $V_R > V_Y$ **AND** $V_R > V_B$. This condition is satisfied for a span of **120°** per cycle (centered around the peak of $V_R$). - Same for D3 around $V_Y$ peak, D5 around $V_B$ peak. - Each pair conduction lasts 60°, but since the "highest phase" stays highest for 120°, each diode conducts for 120°. --- ## 2.2 Output Voltage — Line-to-Line Behavior The output voltage $V_o$ at any instant is the difference between the most positive and most negative phase voltages — i.e., the **line-to-line voltage** at that instant. $$V_o(\omega t) = V_{line-to-line}(\omega t)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the heart of KTU 3-phase rectifier problems:** > The output waveform is **NOT** a phase voltage waveform — it is a **line-to-line voltage waveform** with 6 humps per cycle. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below contains **every formula** you need for KTU 2024 lab viva and university exam on this topic. | # | Parameter | Formula | Derivation Basis | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Average DC Output Voltage (No Load) | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} V_m = \dfrac{3V_{mL}}{\pi}$ | Average of $V_{mL}\cos(\theta)$ over 60° | | 2 | In terms of Line RMS | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_{ph,RMS} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{L,RMS}$ | Substituting $V_m = \sqrt{2}V_{ph}$ | | 3 | Numerical Constant | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{\pi} \approx 1.654$ | Pure math | | 4 | Numerical Constant | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \approx 1.350$ | Pure math | | 5 | RMS Output Voltage | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{3} V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi}}$ | RMS of $V_{mL}\cos(\theta)$ | | 6 | Numerical Value of $V_{rms}/V_m$ | $V_{rms} \approx 1.6554 \, V_m$ | Computed | | 7 | Ripple Factor (Unfiltered) | $\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1} \approx 0.0418$ | Definition | | 8 | Rectification Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2} \approx 99.83\%$ | For purely resistive load | | 9 | Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) per diode | $V_{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \, V_m$ | Line-to-line peak | | 10 | Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) | $TUF = \dfrac{V_{dc} I_{dc}}{V_{L,RMS} I_{L,RMS}} = 0.9552$ | For resistive load | | 11 | Output Frequency (ripple) | $f_o = 6f$ | 6 pulses per supply cycle | | 12 | Diode Conduction Angle | $\theta_c = 120° = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ rad | Topology | | 13 | Form Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} \approx 1.0009$ | Definition | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications 1. **Industrial DC Motor Drives** — large DC drives in steel rolling mills, paper mills, and printing presses (historically). 2. **HVDC Converter Stations** — first stage of conversion before inversion in High-Voltage DC transmission lines. 3. **Battery Chargers** — for high-power EV charging stations and telecom backup systems. 4. **DC Welding Power Supplies** — provides near-constant DC current. 5. **Electrochemical & Electroplating Industries** — aluminum smelting, electroplating baths. 6. **Front-End of Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** — uncontrolled diode bridge + DC link capacitor + inverter. 7. **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** — initial AC-to-DC conversion stage. 8. **Traction Substations** — DC supply for electric trains and metros. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Industry Relevance:** > In modern designs, the uncontrolled 3-phase diode bridge is often used as the **input stage of an AC-DC-AC converter** (VFD), where the DC link capacitor then feeds an IGBT-based inverter. Understanding the diode bridge behavior is essential for analyzing input current harmonics, power factor, and DC link ripple design. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convention Used:** > - $V_m$ = Peak phase voltage (peak of $V_R$, $V_Y$, or $V_B$) > - $V_{mL}$ = Peak line-to-line voltage = $\sqrt{3} V_m$ > - All derivations are for a **highly inductive load** or **no-load (capacitor-fed)** depending on context, but the **average DC voltage formula is the same for all load types** because it is determined solely by source voltages. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average DC Output Voltage ($V_{dc}$) ### Step 1: Set Up the Output Waveform Equation Between $\omega t = 0°$ and $\omega t = 60°$, the most positive phase is R and the most negative phase is B. The output voltage is the line-to-line voltage $V_{RB} = V_R - V_B$. $$V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$ $$V_B = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)$$ Using the identity: $\sin A - \sin B = 2 \cos\left(\dfrac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$ $$V_{RB} = 2 V_m \cos\left(\dfrac{2\omega t - 240°}{2}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{240°}{2}\right)$$ $$V_{RB} = 2 V_m \cos(\omega t - 120°) \sin(120°)$$ Since $\sin(120°) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$: $$V_{RB} = 2 V_m \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos(\omega t - 120°) = \sqrt{3} V_m \cos(\omega t - 120°)$$ This is the line-to-line peak voltage: $$V_{RB} = V_{mL} \cos(\omega t - 120°)$$ By symmetry, each of the 6 humps has the form $V_{mL} \cos(\theta)$ where $\theta$ is measured from the center of the hump. ### Step 2: Express in Standard Form Shifting the time origin to the center of the first hump (where $V_o$ is maximum), the output over one hump (from $-30°$ to $+30°$ relative to the peak) is: $$V_o(\theta) = V_{mL} \cos(\theta), \quad -\dfrac{\pi}{6} \le \theta \le +\dfrac{\pi}{6}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Average Over One Hump $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} V_{mL} \cos(\theta) \, d\theta$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator: $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_{mL}}{\pi/3} \left[ \sin(\theta) \right]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6}$$ $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[ \sin(\pi/6) - \sin(-\pi/6) \right]$$ $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \left[ \dfrac{1}{2} - \left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \right]$$ $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cdot 1$$ $$\boxed{V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi} \approx 1.654 V_m}$$ ### Step 4: Express in Terms of Line RMS Since $V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{L,RMS}$: $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2} V_{L,RMS}}{\pi} \approx 1.350 \, V_{L,RMS}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Output Voltage ($V_{rms}$) ### Step 1: Square the Output Voltage $$V_o^2(\theta) = V_{mL}^2 \cos^2(\theta)$$ ### Step 2: Integrate Over One Hump $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} V_{mL}^2 \cos^2(\theta) \, d\theta$$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}^2}{\pi} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} \cos^2(\theta) \, d\theta$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Power-Reduction Identity $$\cos^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}^2}{\pi} \int_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} \dfrac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2} \, d\theta$$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}^2}{2\pi} \left[ \theta + \dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{2} \right]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6}$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate the Bounds At $\theta = +\pi/6$: $$\theta = \pi/6, \quad \sin(2\theta) = \sin(\pi/3) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ At $\theta = -\pi/6$: $$\theta = -\pi/6, \quad \sin(2\theta) = \sin(-\pi/3) = -\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ Subtracting: $$\left[ \theta + \dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{2} \right]_{-\pi/6}^{+\pi/6} = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{6} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) - \left(-\dfrac{\pi}{6} - \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{3} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$ ### Step 5: Final Expression for $V_{rms}^2$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}^2}{2\pi} \left( \dfrac{\pi}{3} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right)$$ $$V_{rms}^2 = \dfrac{V_{mL}^2}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3} V_{mL}^2}{4\pi}$$ ### Step 6: Take the Square Root $$V_{rms} = V_{mL} \sqrt{ \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi} }$$ $$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 1.6554 \, V_m = 0.9551 \, V_{mL}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ripple Factor ($\gamma$) ### Step 1: Define Ripple Factor $$\gamma = \dfrac{V_{ac,rms}}{V_{dc}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2}}{V_{dc}} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1}$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Ratio $$\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}} = \dfrac{0.9551 \, V_{mL}}{0.9549 \, V_{mL}} \approx 1.0009$$ (using $V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} = 0.9549 V_{mL}$ and $V_{rms} = 0.9551 V_{mL}$) ### Step 3: Final Numerical Value $$\gamma = \sqrt{(1.0009)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{1.0018 - 1} = \sqrt{0.0018}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma \approx 0.04218 \text{ or } 4.218\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Significance:** The ripple factor of 0.042 (4.2%) is extremely low compared to single-phase full-wave (0.482) and three-phase half-wave (0.183) rectifiers. This is why 3-phase diode bridges are preferred for high-power DC applications. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rectification Efficiency ($\eta$) ### Step 1: Definition $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2 / R}{V_{rms}^2 / R} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2}$$ ### Step 2: Compute $$\eta = \dfrac{(0.9549 \, V_{mL})^2}{(0.9551 \, V_{mL})^2} = \dfrac{0.9118}{0.9122} \approx 0.9995$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 99.83\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This near-100% efficiency assumes an **ideal lossless rectifier** (no diode drop, no source impedance). With practical silicon diodes (drop ≈ 0.7 V each, and 2 diodes in series with the load), the efficiency drops to about 95–97% for low-voltage outputs. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) Consider diode D1 connected to phase R. When D1 is OFF, the most negative potential at the –DC terminal is set by whichever even-numbered diode is conducting. The maximum reverse voltage across D1 occurs when D1 is OFF and the most positive phase is Y or B, but the load is fed through D3 or D5. At that moment: - The +DC rail is at the peak of, say, $V_Y$, i.e., $V_m$. - The cathode of D1 is connected to phase R. If $V_R = -V_m/2$ (worst case, when R is at the bottom of its sinusoid), then the reverse voltage across D1 is $V_m - (-V_m/2) = 1.5 V_m$. But this analysis neglects line-to-line contributions. The rigorous result is: $$\boxed{V_{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} \, V_m = \sqrt{6} \, V_{ph,RMS}}$$ > [!NOTE] > KTU students must remember: **PIV per diode in 3-phase bridge = peak line-to-line voltage**, not the peak phase voltage. This is a frequent exam trap. --- ## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A 3-phase diode bridge rectifier is supplied from a 400 V, 50 Hz, balanced 3-phase source through a transformer. Compute: (a) Average DC output voltage, (b) RMS output voltage, (c) Ripple factor, (d) PIV per diode, (e) Output ripple frequency. ### Given: $$V_{L,RMS} = 400 \text{ V}, \quad f = 50 \text{ Hz}$$ ### Step (a): Average DC Output Voltage $$V_{dc} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{L,RMS} = \dfrac{3 \times 1.4142}{3.1416} \times 400$$ $$V_{dc} = 1.350 \times 400 = \boxed{540 \text{ V}}$$ ### Step (b): RMS Output Voltage $$V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 400 = 565.69 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{rms} = V_{mL} \sqrt{ \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{4\pi} } = 565.69 \times 0.9551$$ $$V_{rms} = \boxed{540.31 \text{ V}}$$ ### Step (c): Ripple Factor $$\gamma = \sqrt{ \left( \dfrac{540.31}{540} \right)^2 - 1 } = \sqrt{1.00115 - 1} = \sqrt{0.00115}$$ $$\gamma \approx \boxed{0.0339 \text{ (or 3.39\%)}}$$ ### Step (d): PIV per Diode $$V_{PIV} = V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 400 = \boxed{565.69 \text{ V}}$$ ### Step (e): Output Ripple Frequency $$f_o = 6f = 6 \times 50 = \boxed{300 \text{ Hz}}$$ # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Circuit Block Diagram — Three-Phase Diode Bridge ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AC ["3-PHASE AC SUPPLY (50 Hz)"] R["PHASE R
