## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1.a]**

**Q1.a.** Define *state* and *state variable* of a control system. Why is the state-space representation preferred over the transfer function approach for MIMO systems?

**Model Answer:**
The **state** of a dynamic system at time $t_0$ is the smallest set of numbers (together with the input for $t \ge t_0$) that is sufficient to determine uniquely the future behaviour of the system for all $t \ge t_0$. The independent variables that constitute this set are called **state variables**.

For a system with $n$ energy-storage elements, $n$ state variables are needed. Typical choices are inductor currents ($i_L$) and capacitor voltages ($v_C$).

State-space representation is preferred over transfer functions for MIMO systems because:
1. Transfer functions inherently relate **one input to one output** and become unwieldy for many inputs/outputs.
2. State-space naturally handles **multiple inputs and outputs** through matrix dimensions.
3. It includes **initial conditions** explicitly.
4. It exposes **internal states** useful for control design.
5. It is the natural framework for **modern control** methods (pole placement, observer design, optimal control).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Q1.b]**

**Q1.b.** What is the **state transition matrix**? State any two of its important properties.

**Model Answer:**
The **State Transition Matrix (STM)**, denoted $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t)$ or $e^{\mathbf{A}t}$, is defined as the matrix that maps the initial state $\mathbf{x}(0)$ to the state at any time $t$ in the unforced system ($\mathbf{u}(t) = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x}(t) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t)\,\mathbf{x}(0), \quad \text{where}\ \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t) = e^{\mathbf{A}t} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two important properties:**
1. **Initial condition:** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(0) = \mathbf{I}$ (the identity matrix).
2. **Inverse symmetry:** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{-1}(t) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(-t)$, i.e., the matrix is invertible and the inverse is obtained by negating the time argument.
3. *(Bonus property)* $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t_1 + t_2) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t_1)\,\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t_2)$ — semi-group property.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a single-input single-output (SISO) system from its state-space representation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the system described by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{\mathbf{x}} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -8 & -6 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{x} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{u} \\
\mathbf{y} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\mathbf{x}
\end{aligned}
$$

find the transfer function $G(s)$ and the state transition matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t)$. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**
*(Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above. Key steps for marks:)*

1. State the assumptions: **zero initial conditions** $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{0}$, **LTI system**.
2. Take Laplace transform of the state equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}(s) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{U}(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

3. Rearrange and solve:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{X}(s) = (s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}\mathbf{U}(s)
\end{aligned}
$$

4. Take Laplace transform of the output equation, substitute $\mathbf{X}(s)$, and obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

5. Conclude: poles of $G(s)$ = eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ = roots of $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}) = 0$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} s & -1 \\ 8 & s + 6 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute determinant and inverse:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(s) &= s(s+6) - (-1)(8) = s^{2} + 6s + 8 = (s+2)(s+4) \\
(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1} &= \dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+4)}\begin{bmatrix} s + 6 & 1 \\ -8 & s \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $G(s)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(s) &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+4)}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ s \end{bmatrix} = \dfrac{1}{s^{2} + 6s + 8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t)$ via partial fractions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1} &= \dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+4)}\begin{bmatrix} s + 6 & 1 \\ -8 & s \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Perform partial fraction on each entry. For the (1,1) entry $\dfrac{s+6}{(s+2)(s+4)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{s+6}{(s+2)(s+4)} &= \dfrac{A}{s+2} + \dfrac{B}{s+4} \\
A &= \left.\dfrac{s+6}{s+4}\right\vert_{s=-2} = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2 \\
B &= \left.\dfrac{s+6}{s+2}\right\vert_{s=-4} = \dfrac{2}{-2} = -1 \\
\Rightarrow \dfrac{s+6}{(s+2)(s+4)} &= \dfrac{2}{s+2} - \dfrac{1}{s+4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Inverse Laplace: $2e^{-2t} - e^{-4t}$.

For the (1,2) entry $\dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+4)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+4)} &= \dfrac{1/2}{s+2} - \dfrac{1/2}{s+4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2}\left(e^{-2t} - e^{-4t}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For the (2,1) entry $\dfrac{-8}{(s+2)(s+4)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{-8}{(s+2)(s+4)} &= \dfrac{-4}{s+2} + \dfrac{4}{s+4} \Rightarrow -4e^{-2t} + 4e^{-4t}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the (2,2) entry $\dfrac{s}{(s+2)(s+4)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{s}{(s+2)(s+4)} &= \dfrac{-1}{s+2} + \dfrac{2}{s+4} \Rightarrow -e^{-2t} + 2e^{-4t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final state transition matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t) &= \begin{bmatrix} 2e^{-2t} - e^{-4t} & \dfrac{1}{2}(e^{-2t} - e^{-4t}) \\[0.6em] -4e^{-2t} + 4e^{-4t} & -e^{-2t} + 2e^{-4t} \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{I}$ ✓ $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the procedure to obtain the state-space model in **Controllable Canonical Form (CCF)** from a given transfer function. Apply it to convert

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(s) = \dfrac{5s + 2}{s^{3} + 4s^{2} + 3s + 7}
\end{aligned}
$$

to CCF. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **controllability** and **observability**. Test the system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

for both properties. Comment on the suitability of the system for state-feedback design. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — CCF Derivation and Application (7 Marks):**

**Procedure** (write in 5 steps):
1. Express the transfer function in standard form: $G(s) = \dfrac{b_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + b_0}{s^n + a_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0}$ (ensure denominator is monic).
2. Identify the coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_{n-1}$ and $b_0, b_1, \ldots, b_{n-1}$.
3. Construct $\mathbf{A}_{CCF}$ — a matrix with **1's on the super-diagonal** and the **last row containing $-a_0, -a_1, \ldots, -a_{n-1}$**:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}_{CCF} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -a_0 & -a_1 & -a_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$
4. Set $\mathbf{B}_{CCF} = [0,\ 0,\ \ldots,\ 1]^{T}$.
5. Set $\mathbf{C}_{CCF} = [b_0,\ b_1,\ \ldots,\ b_{n-1}]$ and $\mathbf{D}_{CCF} = 0$ (for strictly proper systems).

**Application to $G(s) = \dfrac{5s + 2}{s^{3} + 4s^{2} + 3s + 7}$:**

Here $n = 3$, $a_0 = 7$, $a_1 = 3$, $a_2 = 4$, $b_0 = 2$, $b_1 = 5$, $b_2 = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ -7 & -3 & -4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 5 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{D} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Controllability & Observability (7 Marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Controllability:** A system is completely state controllable if there exists an input $\mathbf{u}(t)$ that transfers any initial state $\mathbf{x}(0)$ to any desired final state $\mathbf{x}(t_f)$ in a finite time.
- **Observability:** A system is completely observable if the initial state $\mathbf{x}(0)$ can be uniquely determined from the knowledge of $\mathbf{u}(t)$ and $\mathbf{y}(t)$ over a finite interval $0 \le t \le t_f$.

**Controllability test (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{A}\mathbf{B} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \\[0.8em]
\mathcal{C} = [\mathbf{B}\ \ \mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}] &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathcal{C}) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = 1 < 2 = n$. The system is **NOT completely state controllable**.

**Observability test (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C}\mathbf{A} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \\[0.8em]
\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{C} \\ \mathbf{C}\mathbf{A} \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(\mathcal{O}) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = 1 < 2 = n$. The system is **NOT completely observable**.

**Comment:** Since the system is neither fully controllable nor fully observable, it is **not suitable** for arbitrary state-feedback pole placement using the full state vector. The designer must either:
- Use a reduced-order model (only the controllable/observable subspace),
- Apply output feedback (limited but possible),
- Redesign the system by adding actuators or sensors to recover the missing properties.

$\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the initial condition in the state equation's Laplace transform.** Many students write $s\mathbf{X}(s) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}(s) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{U}(s)$ without the $\mathbf{x}(0)$ term, losing 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Mixing up CCF and OCF** — the matrices look very similar but are *transposes* of each other in a specific way. In CCF, the input matrix is a column with a 1 at the bottom; in OCF, the output matrix is a row with $b_0$ first. Always draw the matrices side-by-side when revising.
>
> 3. **Confusing eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ with poles of $G(s)$** — they are the *same* only when the realisation is **minimal** (i.e., controllable and observable). For non-minimal realisations, pole-zero cancellation can occur.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the feed-through term $\mathbf{D}$** — when computing the transfer function, students often drop the $+\mathbf{D}$ at the end. If $\mathbf{D} \neq 0$, the system is *proper but not strictly proper*, and the term must be included.
>
> 5. **In controllability/observability tests, computing the wrong power of $\mathbf{A}$** — for an $n$-th order system you need terms up to $\mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}$, not $\mathbf{A}^{n}\mathbf{B}$.
>
> 6. **Not verifying $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(0) = \mathbf{I}$** — this is a free 1-mark check in any state-transition-matrix problem. Always verify!

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — State-Space Analysis**

- **State-space form:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{u}$.
- **State variables** = the minimum independent set describing system energy storage; pick inductor currents and capacitor voltages in electrical systems.
- **Three canonical realisations:** CCF (controller design), OCF (observer design), DCF (decoupled dynamics).
- **Transfer function from SS:** $G(s) = \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B} + \mathbf{D}$.
- **State transition matrix:** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t) = e^{\mathbf{A}t} = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\}$.
- **Forced solution (Variation of Parameters):** $\mathbf{x}(t) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t)\mathbf{x}(0) + \int_0^t \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t - \tau)\mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(\tau)\,d\tau$.
- **Stability:** All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ must lie in the **open left half-plane** (negative real parts).
- **Controllability:** $\text{rank}[\mathbf{B}\ \ \mathbf{A}\mathbf{B}\ \ \mathbf{A}^{2}\mathbf{B}\ \ \cdots\ \ \mathbf{A}^{n-1}\mathbf{B}] = n$.
- **Observability:** $\text{rank}[\mathbf{C}^{T}\ \ \mathbf{A}^{T}\mathbf{C}^{T}\ \ \cdots]^{T} = n$.
- **Minimal realisation** = controllable **and** observable; in this case, $\text{poles of } G(s) = \text{eigenvalues of } \mathbf{A}$.
- **Duality principle:** A system $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C})$ is controllable **iff** the dual system $(\mathbf{A}^{T}, \mathbf{C}^{T}, \mathbf{B}^{T})$ is observable.
- **Power Electronics Application:** Buck/Boost converters are modelled with two state variables ($i_L$ and $v_C$) — CCF realisation leads directly to voltage-mode controller design.
- **Always verify** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(0) = \mathbf{I}$ as a sanity check.
- **State-space to TF uses matrix inverse**; the denominator is always $\det(s\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of State Feedback and State Observer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design of State Feedback and State Observer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is State Feedback Control?

In modern control theory, a system is described in the **state space** form using a set of first-order differential equations. Unlike classical control (which uses transfer functions and works only for single-input single-output systems), state space methods can handle **Multi-Input Multi-Output (MIMO)** systems and provide complete internal information about the system.

The general linear time-invariant (LTI) state space model is:

$$\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t)$$
$$y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$$

where:
- $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **state vector** (n-dimensional)
- $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the **input vector**
- $y(t) \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is the **output vector**
- $A$ is the $n \times n$ system matrix
- $B$ is the $n \times m$ input matrix
- $C$ is the $p \times n$ output matrix
- $D$ is the $p \times m$ feedthrough matrix

**State feedback** is a control strategy where the control input $u$ is computed as a linear combination of the system states:

$$u(t) = -Kx(t) + r(t)$$

Here, $K$ is the **state feedback gain matrix** (of dimension $m \times n$), and $r(t)$ is the reference input. The negative sign ensures negative feedback for stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why State Feedback?**
> State feedback allows us to **arbitrarily relocate the closed-loop poles** of a system (provided the system is **controllable**). This is known as **Pole Placement**, and is the core of modern control design.

### 1.2 What is a State Observer?

In many real-world systems, we **cannot measure all states directly**. For example, in a DC motor, we can easily measure speed (with a tachometer) and current (with a current sensor), but rotor flux inside a motor is difficult to measure. In such cases, we estimate the unmeasurable states using a **State Observer**.

A **Luenberger State Observer** is a dynamical system that estimates the states of the actual plant. For a system $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$, $y = Cx$, the observer is given by:

$$\dot{\hat{x}}(t) = A\hat{x}(t) + Bu(t) + L(y(t) - C\hat{x}(t))$$

where:
- $\hat{x}(t)$ is the **estimated state vector**
- $L$ is the **observer gain matrix** (of dimension $n \times p$)
- $(y - C\hat{x})$ is the **innovation** or output estimation error
- $L$ multiplies this error to correct the observer dynamics

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** The observer runs in parallel with the plant. When there is a difference between actual output $y$ and estimated output $C\hat{x}$, the term $L(y - C\hat{x})$ "pushes" the estimated state $\hat{x}$ towards the true state $x$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1: State Feedback = Driving a Car with Eyes Open**
> Imagine you are driving a car on a winding road. Your brain continuously measures the car's position (state), compares it with the desired lane (reference), and turns the steering wheel (control input) by an amount proportional to the deviation. Here, your eyes are the **sensors** providing the state, your brain computes the **feedback gain $K$**, and the steering wheel is the **control input $u$**. The "gain" $K$ determines how aggressively you correct — too little and you wander off; too much and you overshoot and oscillate!

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2: State Observer = A Smart Guesser Watching from the Sidelines**
> Imagine a drone (the plant) flying in a room, but you are blindfolded and can only see its shadow on the wall (the output $y$). You cannot see the drone's exact position, velocity, and orientation, but based on the shadow and the control commands you sent, you can **guess** (estimate) the drone's true state. Your guess improves over time as you compare your predicted shadow with the actual shadow. This is exactly what a **state observer** does — it "guesses" the full state from the available output and the control input.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Closed-loop poles** must be in the **left half of the s-plane** (negative real part) for stability.
> - **Observer poles** are typically placed **5 to 10 times faster** (further left) than the controller poles so that state estimation errors decay quickly.
> - **Controllability matrix rank** must equal $n$ (the order of the system) for full state feedback design.
> - **Observability matrix rank** must equal $n$ for full state observer design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Prerequisites: Controllability and Observability

Before designing state feedback or observers, we must verify two key properties.

#### 2.1.1 Controllability

A system is **completely state controllable** if there exists a control input $u(t)$ that transfers any initial state $x(0)$ to any final state $x(t_f)$ in a finite time interval.

**Controllability Matrix:**

$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB & A^2B & \cdots & A^{n-1}B \end{bmatrix}$$

The system is **completely controllable** if and only if:

$$\text{rank}(\mathcal{C}) = n$$

> [!NOTE]
> Controllability is a property of the pair $(A, B)$ — it does not depend on $C$ or $D$.

#### 2.1.2 Observability

A system is **completely observable** if the initial state $x(0)$ can be uniquely determined from the output $y(t)$ measured over a finite time interval.

**Observability Matrix:**

$$\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \\ CA^2 \\ \vdots \\ CA^{n-1} \end{bmatrix}$$

The system is **completely observable** if and only if:

$$\text{rank}(\mathcal{O}) = n$$

### 2.2 Closed-Loop System Dynamics with State Feedback

With $u = -Kx$, the closed-loop system becomes:

$$\dot{x} = Ax + B(-Kx) = (A - BK)x$$

The **closed-loop characteristic equation** is:

$$\det(sI - A + BK) = 0$$

Our goal is to choose $K$ so that the roots of this polynomial match the **desired closed-loop poles**.

### 2.3 Ackermann's Formula for State Feedback

**Ackermann's formula** provides a direct, algebraic method to compute $K$ from the desired characteristic polynomial. For an $n$-th order SISO system:

$$\boxed{K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathcal{C}^{-1} \cdot \alpha_c(A)}$$

where $\alpha_c(A)$ is the **desired characteristic polynomial** evaluated at matrix $A$:

$$\alpha_c(s) = s^n + \alpha_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1 s + \alpha_0$$

$$\alpha_c(A) = A^n + \alpha_{n-1}A^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1 A + \alpha_0 I_n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the system is **uncontrollable**, $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$ does not exist, and Ackermann's formula **cannot be applied**. Always verify $\det(\mathcal{C}) \neq 0$ first.

### 2.4 Observer Error Dynamics

Define the **estimation error**: $e(t) = x(t) - \hat{x}(t)$

Differentiating and using the observer equation:

$$\dot{e} = \dot{x} - \dot{\hat{x}} = Ax + Bu - [A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})]$$
$$\dot{e} = A(x - \hat{x}) - LC(x - \hat{x}) = (A - LC)e$$

The **observer error dynamics** is governed by $(A - LC)$. The error decays to zero (i.e., $\hat{x} \to x$) if the eigenvalues of $(A - LC)$ are in the LHP.

### 2.5 Ackermann's Formula for State Observer

To make the observer poles match a desired polynomial $\alpha_o(s)$, we use:

$$\boxed{L = \alpha_o(A) \cdot \mathcal{O}^{-1} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

where $\alpha_o(A)$ is the desired observer characteristic polynomial evaluated at $A$, and $\mathcal{O}$ is the observability matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **duality** between the two Ackermann's formulas! The observer design is mathematically equivalent to a state feedback design for the **dual system** $(A^T, C^T)$.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Dimensions** | **Condition/Note** |
|---|---|---|---|
| State Space Model | $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu, \; y = Cx$ | $A: n \times n$, $B: n \times m$, $C: p \times n$ | LTI system |
| State Feedback Law | $u = -Kx + r$ | $K: m \times n$ | $r$ is reference input |
| Closed-Loop Matrix | $A_{cl} = A - BK$ | $n \times n$ | Determines closed-loop poles |
| Desired Characteristic Polynomial | $\alpha_c(s) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(s - p_i)$ | Scalar polynomial | $p_i$ are desired poles |
| Controllability Matrix | $\mathcal{C} = [B \; AB \; A^2B \; \cdots \; A^{n-1}B]$ | $n \times nm$ | Full rank $n$ required |
| Observability Matrix | $\mathcal{O} = [C^T \; (CA)^T \; \cdots \; (CA^{n-1})^T]^T$ | $np \times n$ | Full rank $n$ required |
| Ackermann (Feedback) | $K = [0 \; 0 \; \cdots \; 1] \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_c(A)$ | $1 \times n$ (for SISO) | For SISO systems only |
| Observer Equation | $\dot{\hat{x}} = A\hat{x} + Bu + L(y - C\hat{x})$ | $n \times 1$ | $L: n \times p$ |
| Error Dynamics | $\dot{e} = (A - LC)e$ | $n \times 1$ | Eigenvalues must be in LHP |
| Ackermann (Observer) | $L = \alpha_o(A) \mathcal{O}^{-1} [0 \; 0 \; \cdots \; 1]^T$ | $n \times 1$ (for SISO) | For SISO systems only |
| Separation Principle | Controller + Observer = Independent design | — | Eigenvalues of $A-BK$ and $A-LC$ can be placed independently |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students often confuse the Ackermann formulas. The feedback formula has $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$ on the **left**, while the observer formula has $\mathcal{O}^{-1}$ on the **right** of $\alpha_o(A)$. Note carefully that the row vector $[0 \; 0 \; \cdots \; 1]$ is at the **end** of $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$ in the feedback formula but at the **end** of $\mathcal{O}^{-1}$ in the observer formula.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Robotics:** Manipulator arm joint angles are controlled via state feedback, and unmeasured velocities are estimated using observers.
> - **Power Electronics:** Inverters and DC-DC converters use state feedback to regulate output voltage, with observer-estimated inductor currents and capacitor voltages.
> - **Aerospace:** Aircraft autopilot systems use Luenberger/Kalman observers to estimate unmeasured states like angle of attack.
> - **Electric Vehicles:** Motor drives use observers (sliding mode, Luenberger) to estimate rotor flux/position in sensorless control.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ackermann's Formula for State Feedback

**Step 1 — Setup.** We want the closed-loop matrix $A - BK$ to have a desired characteristic polynomial $\alpha_c(s)$.

By the **Cayley-Hamilton Theorem**, every matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation. The closed-loop matrix $A_{cl} = A - BK$ satisfies:

$$\alpha_c(A_{cl}) = (A - BK)^n + \alpha_{n-1}(A - BK)^{n-1} + \cdots + \alpha_1(A - BK) + \alpha_0 I = 0$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of $K$.** By expanding, this can be written as:

$$\alpha_c(A - BK) = \alpha_c(A) - \beta \mathcal{C} K \quad \text{(for some row vector } \beta \text{)}$$

where $\beta$ is a row vector that depends on the system. Specifically:

$$\beta = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathcal{C}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Cayley-Hamilton to $A - BK$.** Since $\alpha_c(A - BK) = 0$:

$$\alpha_c(A) = \beta \, \mathcal{C} \, K$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $K$.** Multiply both sides on the left by $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$ and recognize the structure:

$$K = \mathcal{C}^{-1} \cdot \beta^T \cdot \alpha_c(A) \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \mathcal{C}^{-1} \alpha_c(A)$$

This is the celebrated Ackermann's formula. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: Ackermann's Formula for Observer

The observer design problem reduces to: place the eigenvalues of $(A - LC)$ at desired locations.

By **duality**, this is equivalent to placing the eigenvalues of $(A^T - C^T L^T)$ via state feedback for the dual system $(A^T, C^T)$. Using Ackermann's formula for the dual system:

$$L^T = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} (\mathcal{O}^T)^{-1} \alpha_o(A^T)$$

Using the identity $(\mathcal{O}^T)^{-1} = (\mathcal{O}^{-1})^T$ and $\alpha_o(A^T) = [\alpha_o(A)]^T$:

$$L = \alpha_o(A) \cdot \mathcal{O}^{-1} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Complete Numerical Example 1 — State Feedback Design

**Problem:** Given the system:
$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Design a state feedback gain $K = [k_1 \; k_2]$ such that the closed-loop poles are at $s = -1, \; s = -2$.

**Step 1 — Verify Controllability.**

$$\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} B & AB \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathcal{C}) = (0)(0) - (1)(1) = -1 \neq 0$$

Since $\det(\mathcal{C}) \neq 0$, the system is **completely controllable**. ✓

**Step 2 — Form the Desired Characteristic Polynomial.**

$$\alpha_c(s) = (s+1)(s+2) = s^2 + 3s + 2$$

So $\alpha_1 = 3$ and $\alpha_0 = 2$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\alpha_c(A)$.**

$$A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\alpha_c(A) = A^2 + 3A + 2I = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\mathcal{C}^{-1}$.**

For $\mathcal{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, swapping diagonal elements gives the inverse:

$$\mathcal{C}^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Ackermann's Formula.**

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \mathcal{C}^{-1} \cdot \alpha_c(A)$$

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Closed-Loop Matrix).**

$$A - BK = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Characteristic polynomial: $\det(sI - (A-BK)) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$ ✓

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{K = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}}$ and the control law is $u = -2x_1 - 3x_2$.

### 3.4 Complete Numerical Example 2 — State Observer Design

**Problem:** For the same system with $C = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, design a Luenberger observer with poles at $s = -5$ and $s = -6$.

**Step 1 — Verify Observability.**

$$\mathcal{O} = \begin{bmatrix} C \\ CA \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det(\mathcal{O}) = 1 \neq 0 \Rightarrow \text{System is completely observable. ✓}$$

**Step 2 — Form the Desired Observer Polynomial.**

$$\alpha_o(s) = (s+5)(s+6) = s^2 + 11s + 30$$

So $\beta_1 = 11$ and $\beta_0 = 30$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\alpha_o(A)$.**

$$\alpha_o(A) = A^2 + 11A + 30I = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 11 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 0 \\ 0 & 30 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 11 \\ 0 & 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\mathcal{O}^{-1}$.**

Since $\mathcal{O} = I$, we have $\mathcal{O}^{-1} = I$.

**Step 5 — Apply Ackermann's Formula for Observer.**

$$L = \alpha_o(A) \cdot \mathcal{O}^{-1} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 11 \\ 0 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$L = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 11 \\ 0 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 11 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Verification (Observer Error Matrix).**

$$A - LC = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 11 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -11 & 1 \\ -30 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Characteristic polynomial: $\det(sI - (A-LC)) = s^2 + 11s + 30 = (s+5)(s+6)$ ✓

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{L = \begin{bmatrix} 11 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}}$

### 3.5 Complete Example 3 — Combined State Feedback with Observer

Combining the two: $u = -K\hat{x}$ (note: we use $\hat{x}$ because $x$ may not be measurable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Separation Principle:** The eigenvalues of the closed-loop system with observer-based control are the union of the eigenvalues of $A - BK$ and $A - LC$. This means we can design $K$ and $L$ **independently**!

The combined closed-loop matrix is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{e} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A - BK & BK \\ 0 & A - LC \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ e \end{bmatrix}$$

The block-triangular form shows the eigenvalues are simply $\{ \lambda_i(A-BK) \} \cup \{ \lambda_i(A-LC) \}$.

For our example: closed-loop poles are $\{-1, -2\}$ from controller and $\{-5, -6\}$ from observer. All in LHP → **stable**. ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 State Feedback Control System

The block diagram below shows a complete state feedback control loop. The plant has state vector $x$, the gain $K$ multiplies the states, and the result is subtracted from the reference $r$ to form the control input $u$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Reference Input r]
    SUM1[Summing Junction]
    PLANT[Plant Dynamics A and B]
    OUT[Output y]
    KGAIN[State Feedback Gain Matrix K]
    XSTATE[State Vector x]

    R --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> |u| PLANT
    PLANT --> OUT
    PLANT --> XSTATE
    XSTATE --> KGAIN
    KGAIN --> |negative K times x| SUM1
```

### 4.2 Luenberger State Observer Structure

The observer runs in parallel with the plant. It uses the same input $u$ as the plant, and compares its predicted output $C \hat{x}$ with the actual output $y$. The error is multiplied by $L$ and fed back to correct the estimate.

```mermaid
graph LR
    UINP[Input u]
    PLANT2[Plant A, B, C]
    YOUT[Actual Output y]
    OBS[Luenberger Observer A, B, C, L]
    XHAT[Estimated State x hat]
    PREDY[Predicted Output C x hat]
    SUMOBS[Summing Junction]
    LGAIN[Observer Gain L]

    UINP --> PLANT2
    PLANT2 --> YOUT
    UINP --> OBS
    OBS --> XHAT
    XHAT --> PREDY
    PREDY --> SUMOBS
    YOUT --> SUMOBS
    SUMOBS --> |innovation y minus C x hat| LGAIN
    LGAIN --> OBS
```

### 4.3 Complete System — State Feedback with Observer

This is the practical implementation where full state $x$ is **not** measurable. We use the estimated state $\hat{x}$ in place of $x$ for the feedback law. The estimate $\hat{x}$ is generated by the Luenberger observer.

```mermaid
graph LR
    REF2[Reference r]
    SUMF[Summing Junction for Feedback]
    PLANT3[Plant A, B, C]
    YFINAL[Output y]
    OBS3[Observer A minus LC]
    XEST[Estimated State x hat]
    KFB[Feedback Gain K]

    REF2 --> SUMF
    SUMF --> |u| PLANT3
    PLANT3 --> YFINAL
    PLANT3 --> |u shares| OBS3
    YFINAL --> |feedback to observer| OBS3
    OBS3 --> XEST
    XEST --> KFB
    KFB --> |negative K x hat| SUMF
```

### 4.4 Flowchart — Design Procedure for Pole Placement

The design process follows a strict sequence. Skipping steps leads to errors, as the observer/feedback cannot be designed if the system fails the controllability/observability test.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST1[Step 1: Obtain State Space Model A, B, C]
    ST2[Step 2: Check Controllability rank of C equals n]
    ST3[Step 3: Choose Desired Closed Loop Poles]
    ST4[Step 4: Form Desired Characteristic Polynomial alpha_c of s]
    ST5[Step 5: Compute alpha_c of A by substitution]
    ST6[Step 6: Compute Inverse of Controllability Matrix]
    ST7[Step 7: Apply Ackermann Formula to get K]
    ST8[Step 8: Verify A minus BK has desired eigenvalues]
    ST9[Step 9: For Observer, Check Observability rank of O equals n]
    ST10[Step 10: Choose Desired Observer Poles 5 to 10 times faster]
    ST11[Step 11: Apply Observer Ackermann Formula to get L]
    ST12[Step 12: Verify A minus LC has desired eigenvalues]

    ST1 --> ST2
    ST2 --> ST3
    ST3 --> ST4
    ST4 --> ST5
    ST5 --> ST6
    ST6 --> ST7
    ST7 --> ST8
    ST1 --> ST9
    ST9 --> ST10
    ST10 --> ST11
    ST11 --> ST12
```
