topics.title: Cascade Control System and Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEIL607 - POWER ELECTRONICS & CONTROL SYSTEM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Cascade Control System and Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cascade Control System** is a multi-loop feedback control strategy in which the output of a *primary (master / outer)* controller is used to dynamically adjust the *setpoint* of a *secondary (slave / inner)* controller. The two controllers operate in series, forming a hierarchical control structure that improves disturbance rejection and dynamic response for processes exhibiting measurable *secondary variables* which respond faster than the *primary variable*.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Control System Lab terminology, cascade control is classified under **Multi-Loop Control Systems** and is experimentally verified through **MATLAB/Simulink** simulation of two interacting feedback loops operating over a common plant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cruise Control of a Car with a Clutch Sensor"

Imagine driving a car where you want to maintain a constant speed (**primary variable** = car speed). A naive approach would directly press the accelerator based on speed error. However, if the road becomes hilly, the engine load (a *secondary variable*) changes, and the car speed dips before the speed controller reacts.

In a **cascade** structure:
- The **outer (master) loop** watches the **road speed** and commands an *engine load setpoint*.
- The **inner (slave) loop** watches the *actual engine load* (torque) and adjusts the **accelerator pedal** rapidly to meet that setpoint.
- The inner loop reacts to disturbances (hills, wind) **before they propagate** to the outer loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** The *secondary loop must be faster* (higher bandwidth) than the *primary loop* — this is the fundamental design rule of cascade control.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Scheme Evaluation Parameters)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value for Well-Tuned Cascade |
|---|---|---|
| **Settling Time** | $t_s$ | **40 % to 60 % reduction** vs. single-loop |
| **Maximum Overshoot** | $M_p$ | **$\leq 5\%$** for step setpoint |
| **Steady-State Error** | $e_{ss}$ | **$\leq 2\%$** |
| **Integral Absolute Error** | IAE | **Reduced by 30 %–70 %** |
| **Disturbance Rejection Bandwidth** | $\omega_{bw}$ | **Increased** in inner loop |

## 1.4 Physical Interpretation

The plant is partitioned into two sections:
- **$G_{p2}(s)$** — the *fast* secondary section (e.g., flow, current, pressure)
- **$G_{p1}(s)$** — the *slow* primary section (e.g., temperature, level, composition)

Two controllers operate:
- **$G_{c2}(s)$** — Slave controller (fast, often P or PI)
- **$G_{c1}(s)$** — Master controller (slower, often PI or PID)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU experiments, the **inner loop is tuned FIRST** (closed), then the **outer loop** is tuned with the inner loop in operation. This sequential tuning is a board-favourite viva question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Cascade Control Works

The cascade control loop functions through the following sequential logic:

1. **Primary Setpoint Arrival** — A reference value $R_1(s)$ enters the *outer loop* summing junction.
2. **Primary Error Generation** — Error $E_1(s) = R_1(s) - Y_1(s)$ is computed.
3. **Master Controller Action** — $G_{c1}(s)$ processes $E_1(s)$ and produces an output that becomes the *variable* setpoint $R_2(s)$ for the slave loop.
4. **Secondary Error Generation** — Inner error $E_2(s) = R_2(s) - Y_2(s)$ is formed.
5. **Slave Controller Action** — $G_{c2}(s)$ drives the final control element (valve, actuator).
6. **Process Partitioning** — Manipulated variable $U(s)$ enters $G_{p2}(s)$, whose output $Y_2(s)$ feeds $G_{p1}(s)$, which finally produces $Y_1(s)$.
7. **Dual Feedback** — Both $Y_1(s)$ and $Y_2(s)$ are measured and fed back to their respective controllers.

> [!NOTE]
> The slave loop typically includes **only disturbances that enter $G_{p2}(s)$** — these are rejected *before* they propagate to the slow primary variable.

## 2.2 Design Rules (Why & How)

- **Rule 1 — Bandwidth Separation:** Inner loop natural frequency $\omega_{n2} \geq 3 \cdot \omega_{n1}$ outer loop natural frequency.
- **Rule 2 — Secondary Variable Selection:** Must be (a) measurable, (b) faster than the primary, (c) directly affected by the manipulated variable.
- **Rule 3 — Sequential Tuning:** Tune inner loop first using standard methods (Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, Tyreus-Luyben), then tune the outer loop with the inner loop closed.
- **Rule 4 — Slave Controller Complexity:** Often P-only or PI — derivative action in the fast loop amplifies measurement noise.
- **Rule 5 — Master Controller Authority:** Master must be slower; use PI or PID here to eliminate steady-state error on the primary variable.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Open-Loop Secondary Transfer Function** | $G_{OL2}(s) = G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s) \cdot H_2(s)$ | dimensionless | Inner loop alone |
| 2 | **Closed-Loop Secondary Transfer Function** | $G_{CL2}(s) = \dfrac{G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) H_2(s)}$ | dimensionless | Reduces to equivalent block $G_{eq}(s)$ |
| 3 | **Overall Cascade Transfer Function** | $\dfrac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \dfrac{G_{c1}(s) \cdot G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s) \cdot G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s) \cdot H_1(s)}$ | dimensionless | Equivalent single-loop form |
| 4 | **Equivalent Inner Block** | $G_{eq}(s) = \dfrac{G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) H_2(s)}$ | dimensionless | Replaces inner loop |
| 5 | **Secondary Disturbance Rejection** | $\dfrac{Y_2(s)}{D_2(s)} = \dfrac{G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) H_2(s)}$ | — | $D_2$ enters inner loop |
| 6 | **Primary Disturbance Transfer** | $\dfrac{Y_1(s)}{D_1(s)} = \dfrac{G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s) G_{eq}(s) G_{p1}(s) H_1(s)}$ | — | $D_1$ enters $G_{p1}$ |
| 7 | **Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Gain** | $K_u = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{co}) \vert}$ | — | At phase crossover $\omega_{co}$ |
| 8 | **Inner Loop Time Constant (1st order approx.)** | $\tau_{eq} = \dfrac{\tau_2}{1 + K_{c2} K_{p2}}$ | seconds | Faster than $\tau_2$ |
| 9 | **Steady-State Error (Type 1 system)** | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | — | $K_p$ = position error constant |
| 10 | **ITAE Performance Index** | $J_{ITAE} = \displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} t \,\vert e(t) \vert\, dt$ | — | Minimised for optimal tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** Cascade control is the workhorse in **thermal power plants** (drum level–flow), **petrochemical reactors** (temperature–flow), **HVAC systems** (temperature–airflow), and **motor drives** (speed–current). It is the foundational structure behind every industrial DCS (Distributed Control System).

## 2.4 Comparative Insight — Single-Loop vs. Cascade

| Property | Single-Loop | Cascade |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Controllers | 1 | 2 (or more) |
| Disturbance Rejection | Slower | **Much faster** for inner-loop disturbances |
| Sensor Count | 1 | **2** (primary + secondary) |
| Tuning Complexity | Easier | **Sequential — moderately complex** |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (extra sensor + controller) |
| Robustness | Lower | **Higher** for measurable secondary variables |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Equivalent Inner-Loop Transfer Function

**Starting point:** Consider the inner (secondary) loop with feedback $H_2(s)$ and controller $G_{c2}(s)$ driving plant $G_{p2}(s)$.

The forward path of the inner loop is:

$$ G_{fwd,2}(s) = G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s) $$

By the **standard negative-feedback closed-loop formula**:

$$ G_{CL2}(s) = \frac{G_{fwd,2}(s)}{1 + G_{fwd,2}(s) \cdot H_2(s)} $$

Substituting the forward path expression:

$$ G_{CL2}(s) = \frac{G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s) \cdot H_2(s)} $$

**Logic step:** The inner loop's closed-loop transfer function *replaces* the cascade of $G_{c2}(s)$ and $G_{p2}(s)$ from the perspective of the outer loop. We define this as $G_{eq}(s)$:

$$ G_{eq}(s) \triangleq \frac{G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s) \cdot H_2(s)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This $G_{eq}(s)$ is the *mathematical contract* between the inner and outer loops. From the master's view, it appears as a *new* plant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Overall Cascade Transfer Function $Y_1(s)/R_1(s)$

**Step 1 — Identify the outer loop components:**
- Controller: $G_{c1}(s)$
- "Plant" (from master view): $G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s)$
- Feedback: $H_1(s)$

**Step 2 — Apply the closed-loop formula again:**

$$ \frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{G_{c1}(s) \cdot G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s) \cdot G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s) \cdot H_1(s)} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute $G_{eq}(s)$:**

$$
\frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{ G_{c1}(s) \cdot \left[ \dfrac{G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) H_2(s)} \right] \cdot G_{p1}(s) }
{ 1 + G_{c1}(s) \cdot \left[ \dfrac{G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) H_2(s)} \right] \cdot G_{p1}(s) \cdot H_1(s) }
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $\left[1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) H_2(s)\right]$:**

$$
\frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{ G_{c1}(s) G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) G_{p1}(s) }
{ 1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) H_2(s) + G_{c1}(s) G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) G_{p1}(s) H_1(s) }
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logic commentary:** Notice the characteristic polynomial has *two* feedback terms — one from the inner loop and one from the outer loop. This nested structure is the **mathematical signature** of cascade control. The denominator's inner-loop term $G_{c2} G_{p2} H_2$ provides the *extra disturbance rejection*.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 2024 Style

**Given the cascade system:**

$$ G_{c1}(s) = K_1 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_{i1} s} \right), \quad G_{c2}(s) = K_2 $$

$$ G_{p1}(s) = \frac{1}{(s+1)(2s+1)}, \quad G_{p2}(s) = \frac{1}{5s+1} $$

$$ H_1(s) = 1, \quad H_2(s) = 1 $$

**Step 1 — Compute the inner-loop equivalent (slave controller is P-only, $G_{c2}=K_2$):**

$$ G_{eq}(s) = \frac{K_2 \cdot \frac{1}{5s+1}}{1 + K_2 \cdot \frac{1}{5s+1} \cdot 1} = \frac{K_2}{5s + 1 + K_2} $$

**Step 2 — Simplify the equivalent first-order form:**

$$ G_{eq}(s) = \frac{K_2}{(5s + (1+K_2))} = \frac{\dfrac{K_2}{1+K_2}}{\dfrac{5}{1+K_2} s + 1} $$

Let $K_{eq} = \dfrac{K_2}{1+K_2}$ and $\tau_{eq} = \dfrac{5}{1+K_2}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the outer loop with $G_{c1}(s) = K_1 + \dfrac{K_1}{T_{i1} s}$:**

$$
G_{c1}(s) \cdot G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s) = \left( K_1 + \frac{K_1}{T_{i1} s} \right) \cdot \frac{K_{eq}}{\tau_{eq} s + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{(s+1)(2s+1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Inner-loop bandwidth check (assume $K_2 = 4$):**

$$ \tau_{eq} = \frac{5}{1+4} = 1 \text{ s} $$

The inner loop time constant **dropped from 5 s to 1 s** — a 5× speed-up. This validates the design rule.

**Step 5 — Calculate closed-loop characteristic equation:**

$$ 1 + G_{c1}(s) G_{eq}(s) G_{p1}(s) H_1(s) = 0 $$

After substitution and clearing denominators, the order of the overall closed-loop system becomes **4th order** (original 3rd order plant + 1st order PI integrator), confirming that cascade control preserves system order from the master's perspective while gaining inner-loop speed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Tuning Strategy

| Tuning Stage | Method Applied | Goal | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1** | Put master in **MANUAL** (open outer loop) | Isolate slave loop | Slave acts as a single-loop |
| **Stage 2** | Apply **Ziegler-Nichols P-only** on slave | Find $K_{u2}$ and $P_{u2}$ | Compute $K_{c2}, T_{i2}, T_{d2}$ |
| **Stage 3** | Close inner loop, **put master in MANUAL** | Test inner-loop disturbance rejection | Verify $\omega_{n2} \geq 3 \omega_{n1,\text{desired}}$ |
| **Stage 4** | Switch master to **AUTO** | Tune master using Z-N or Cohen-Coon | Optimise $K_{c1}, T_{i1}, T_{d1}$ |
| **Stage 5** | Apply **setpoint filter** to master output | Prevent sudden slave setpoint shocks | Smooths $R_2(s)$ |
| **Stage 6** | Verify with **step + disturbance** test | Validate ISE, IAE, ITAE | Compare with single-loop baseline |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Detailed Block Diagram — Cascade Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[R1 PRIMARY SETPOINT] --> SUM1{{SUM1 OUTER LOOP}}
    Y1[Y1 PRIMARY OUTPUT] --> FBM1[FEEDBACK PATH H1]
    FBM1 --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> E1[E1 PRIMARY ERROR]
    E1 --> GC1[GC1 MASTER CONTROLLER PI or PID]
    GC1 --> R2[R2 VARIABLE SLAVE SETPOINT]
    R2 --> SUM2{{SUM2 INNER LOOP}}
    Y2[Y2 SECONDARY OUTPUT] --> FBM2[FEEDBACK PATH H2]
    FBM2 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> E2[E2 SECONDARY ERROR]
    E2 --> GC2[GC2 SLAVE CONTROLLER P or PI]
    GC2 --> U[U MANIPULATED VARIABLE TO VALVE]
    U --> GP2[GP2 FAST SECONDARY PLANT]
    GP2 --> Y2
    Y2 --> GP1[GP1 SLOW PRIMARY PLANT]
    GP1 --> Y1
    D2[D2 INNER LOOP DISTURBANCE] --> GP2
    D1[D1 OUTER LOOP DISTURBANCE] --> GP1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the signal flow clockwise. The output of $G_{c1}$ is *not* a physical actuator signal — it is a *time-varying setpoint* $R_2(s)$ for the inner loop. The inner loop's output $U(s)$ is the *only* signal that physically drives the plant valve.

---

## 4.2 Simulink Implementation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUTER_LOOP[OUTER CONTROL LOOP]
        STEP[STEP INPUT R1] --> SSUM1[SUMMING JUNCTION WITH MINUS]
        SCOPE1[SCOPE PRIMARY Y1] --> FBACK1[FEEDBACK GAIN H1]
        FBACK1 --> SSUM1
        SSUM1 --> ERR1[ERROR SIGNAL E1]
        ERR1 --> PID1[PID BLOCK MASTER TUNING]
        PID1 --> SETPT2[VARIABLE SETPOINT R2]
    end
    subgraph INNER_LOOP[INNER CONTROL LOOP]
        SETPT2 --> SSUM2[SUMMING JUNCTION WITH MINUS]
        SCOPE2[SCOPE SECONDARY Y2] --> FBACK2[FEEDBACK GAIN H2]
        FBACK2 --> SSUM2
        SSUM2 --> ERR2[ERROR SIGNAL E2]
        ERR2 --> P2[P OR PI BLOCK SLAVE]
        P2 --> PLANT2[TRANSFER FUNCTION GP2]
        PLANT2 --> PLANT1[TRANSFER FUNCTION GP1]
        PLANT1 --> SCOPE1
        PLANT2 --> SCOPE2
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Signal Path for Disturbance Rejection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DISTURBANCE D2 ENTERS GP2] --> B[GP2 OUTPUT DEVIATES Y2]
    B --> C[INNER FEEDBACK H2 SENSES CHANGE]
    C --> D[INNER ERROR E2 GROWS]
    D --> E[SLAVE CONTROLLER GC2 ACTS FAST]
    E --> F[U COMPENSATES BEFORE D2 REACHES GP1]
    F --> G[Y1 ALMOST UNAFFECTED]
    G --> H[OUTER LOOP DOES NOT NEED TO ACT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight visualised:** The inner loop's $G_{c2}$ corrects the disturbance *locally* inside $G_{p2}$ — the disturbance never propagates to $G_{p1}$. This is why cascade control is *so effective* in heat exchangers, where flow disturbances (cold water pressure) would otherwise devastate temperature control.

---

## 4.4 Cascade Control Tuning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[STEP 1 IDENTIFY PLANT TRANSFER FUNCTIONS GP1 AND GP2] --> S2[STEP 2 OPEN OUTER LOOP MANUAL MODE]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3 APPLY STEP TEST ON GP2]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4 COMPUTE SLAVE TUNING KU2 PU2]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5 SET GC2 PARAMETERS]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6 CLOSE INNER LOOP AND VERIFY STABILITY]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7 OPEN OUTER LOOP AND STEP TEST EQUIVALENT PLANT]
    S7 --> S8[STEP 8 COMPUTE MASTER TUNING KU1 PU1]
    S8 --> S9[STEP 9 SET GC1 PARAMETERS]
    S9 --> S10[STEP 10 CLOSE BOTH LOOPS AND RECORD RESPONSES]
    S10 --> S11[STEP 11 COMPARE WITH SINGLE LOOP BASELINE]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define cascade control system. State any two advantages over single-loop control.**

**Model Answer:**

Cascade control is a multi-loop control strategy in which the output of the primary (master) controller is used as the *variable setpoint* of a secondary (slave) controller operating in series with the primary loop.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Faster disturbance rejection** — Disturbances entering the inner (secondary) plant are compensated by the slave controller *before* they reach the primary variable, dramatically reducing IAE/ITAE indices.
2. **Improved dynamic response** — The inner loop reduces the effective time constant of the plant, allowing the outer loop to achieve tighter tracking with lower overshoot.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid advantages: better robustness to plant parameter variations, ability to handle multiple measurable disturbances, smoother control valve action.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is the inner loop tuned first in a cascade control system? List the design rule for selecting the secondary variable.**

**Model Answer:**

The inner loop is tuned first because:
- It is the *fastest* loop and must be stabilised before being *cascaded* into the outer loop.
- An unstable or sluggish inner loop will cause the outer loop to see a poorly-behaved equivalent plant $G_{eq}(s)$, making the master controller impossible to tune.

**Design rule for secondary variable:**
The secondary variable must (i) be **measurable online**, (ii) respond **faster** than the primary variable ($\omega_{n2} \geq 3 \omega_{n1}$), and (iii) be **directly affected** by the manipulated variable.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**A cascade control system has the following transfer functions:**

$$ G_{c1}(s) = 2\left(1 + \frac{1}{5s}\right), \quad G_{c2}(s) = 4, \quad G_{p1}(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}, \quad G_{p2}(s) = \frac{1}{0.5s+1}, \quad H_1 = H_2 = 1 $$

**(a)** Derive the equivalent closed-loop transfer function of the inner (secondary) loop. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the result of part (a), derive the *overall* closed-loop transfer function $Y_1(s)/R_1(s)$ and comment on the order of the resulting system. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Identify inner-loop components:**
The forward path of the inner loop is:

$$ G_{fwd,2}(s) = G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s) = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{0.5s+1} = \frac{4}{0.5s+1} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the closed-loop formula** with $H_2(s) = 1$:

$$ G_{eq}(s) = \frac{G_{fwd,2}(s)}{1 + G_{fwd,2}(s) \cdot H_2(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{4}{0.5s+1}}{1 + \dfrac{4}{0.5s+1}} $$

**Step 3 — Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by $(0.5s+1)$:**

$$ G_{eq}(s) = \frac{4}{0.5s + 1 + 4} = \frac{4}{0.5s + 5} $$

**Step 4 — Normalise to standard first-order form** by dividing numerator and denominator by 5:

$$ G_{eq}(s) = \frac{4/5}{(0.5/5)s + 1} = \frac{0.8}{0.1s + 1} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commentary:** The original $G_{p2}$ had time constant $\tau_2 = 0.5$ s and DC gain $K_{p2} = 1$. After closing the inner loop with $G_{c2} = 4$, the equivalent has $\tau_{eq} = 0.1$ s and $K_{eq} = 0.8$. The loop is **5× faster** at the cost of a small reduction in steady-state gain — the master controller compensates this.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Express the master controller in standard PI form:**

$$ G_{c1}(s) = 2\left(1 + \frac{1}{5s}\right) = \frac{2(5s + 1)}{5s} = \frac{10s + 2}{5s} = 2 + \frac{2}{5s} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the outer closed-loop formula:**

$$ \frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{G_{c1}(s) \cdot G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s) \cdot G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s)} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the forward path:**

$$ G_{c1}(s) \cdot G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s) = \left(2 + \frac{2}{5s}\right) \cdot \frac{0.8}{0.1s+1} \cdot \frac{1}{s+1} $$

Combining the PI terms over common denominator $5s$:

$$ = \frac{(10s + 2)}{5s} \cdot \frac{0.8}{(0.1s+1)(s+1)} $$

$$ = \frac{0.8(10s + 2)}{5s(0.1s+1)(s+1)} = \frac{0.8 \cdot 2(5s+1)}{5s(0.1s+1)(s+1)} $$

$$ = \frac{1.6(5s+1)}{5s(0.1s+1)(s+1)} $$

**Step 4 — Form the closed-loop transfer function:**

$$ \frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{1.6(5s+1)}{5s(0.1s+1)(s+1)}}{1 + \dfrac{1.6(5s+1)}{5s(0.1s+1)(s+1)}} $$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $5s(0.1s+1)(s+1)$:**

$$ \frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{1.6(5s+1)}{5s(0.1s+1)(s+1) + 1.6(5s+1)} $$

**Step 6 — Expand the denominator characteristic polynomial:**

First expand $5s(0.1s+1)(s+1)$:

$$ (0.1s+1)(s+1) = 0.1s^2 + 1.1s + 1 $$

$$ 5s(0.1s^2 + 1.1s + 1) = 0.5s^3 + 5.5s^2 + 5s $$

Add $1.6(5s+1) = 8s + 1.6$:

$$ \text{Denominator} = 0.5s^3 + 5.5s^2 + 5s + 8s + 1.6 = 0.5s^3 + 5.5s^2 + 13s + 1.6 $$

Multiplying by 2 to clear decimals:

$$ s^3 + 11s^2 + 26s + 3.2 $$

**Step 7 — Final transfer function:**

$$ \boxed{ \frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{1.6(5s+1)}{0.5s^3 + 5.5s^2 + 13s + 1.6} } $$

**Order commentary:** The overall closed-loop system is **3rd order**. The original plant was 2nd order (1st order in $G_{p1}$ + 1st order in $G_{p2}$), and the PI action in the master controller added one integrator pole → net **3rd order**. The inner loop was *absorbed* into $G_{eq}$ and did not increase the visible system order from the outer perspective.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *(Alternative Choice)*

**A temperature control system uses cascade control where the primary variable is *reactor temperature* and the secondary variable is *cooling-water flow rate*. The plant transfer functions are:**

$$ G_{p1}(s) = \frac{2}{10s+1}, \quad G_{p2}(s) = \frac{0.5}{s+1} $$

The master controller is $G_{c1}(s) = 1.5\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{8s}\right)$ and the slave controller is $G_{c2}(s) = 3$.

**(a)** Simulate this cascade system in MATLAB/Simulink and explain the **sequential tuning procedure**. Write the MATLAB code to obtain the closed-loop step response. (7 marks)

**(b)** A flow disturbance of magnitude $D_2(s) = \dfrac{0.2}{s}$ enters the inner loop. Derive the transfer function $\dfrac{Y_2(s)}{D_2(s)}$ and compute the steady-state deviation in $Y_2$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Sequential tuning procedure — five steps:**

1. **Open the outer loop** (set $G_{c1}$ output to manual/constant). The cascade reduces to a single-loop system with $G_{c2}$ controlling $G_{p2}$.
2. **Apply a step test** to identify the inner plant dynamics: $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{0.5}{s+1}$ (first order, $\tau = 1$ s, $K = 0.5$).
3. **Tune the slave controller** using Ziegler-Nichols: For a first-order plant, the recommended PI is $K_{c2} = 0.5 / (K \cdot \tau) = 0.5/(0.5 \cdot 1) = 1$, but given here $G_{c2} = 3$ for aggressive disturbance rejection.
4. **Close the inner loop** and verify stability using the equivalent plant:

$$ G_{eq}(s) = \frac{3 \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{s+1}}{1 + 3 \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{s+1}} = \frac{1.5}{s + 1 + 1.5} = \frac{1.5}{s + 2.5} = \frac{0.6}{0.4s + 1} $$

5. **Tune the master controller** with the inner loop closed. The "plant" from master's view is $G_{eq}(s) \cdot G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{0.6}{0.4s+1} \cdot \dfrac{2}{10s+1} = \dfrac{1.2}{(0.4s+1)(10s+1)}$.

**MATLAB Code:**

```matlab
% MATLAB Code for Cascade Control Simulation
s = tf('s');
Gp1 = 2/(10*s+1);     % Primary plant (reactor temperature)
Gp2 = 0.5/(s+1);      % Secondary plant (flow)
Gc1 = 1.5*(1 + 1/(8*s));  % Master PI
Gc2 = 3;              % Slave P

% Compute equivalent inner loop
Geq = (Gc2*Gp2)/(1 + Gc2*Gp2);

% Overall closed-loop transfer function
T_cascade = (Gc1*Geq*Gp1)/(1 + Gc1*Geq*Gp1);

% Compare with single-loop
G_single = Gc1*Gp1*Gp2;
T_single = G_single/(1 + G_single);

% Step response comparison
figure;
step(T_cascade, 'b', T_single, 'r--', 50);
legend('Cascade Control','Single-Loop Control');
title('Step Response Comparison');
xlabel('Time (s)'); ylabel('Output');
grid on;
```

**Output discussion:** The cascade response will show **smaller overshoot**, **faster settling time**, and **lower IAE** than the single-loop response.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Inner-loop disturbance transfer function:**

The disturbance $D_2(s)$ enters at the output of $G_{p2}$. By inspection of the inner loop block diagram:

$$ \frac{Y_2(s)}{D_2(s)} = \frac{G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) H_2(s)} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given transfer functions** with $H_2 = 1$:

$$ \frac{Y_2(s)}{D_2(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{0.5}{s+1}}{1 + 3 \cdot \dfrac{0.5}{s+1}} $$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(s+1)$:**

$$ \frac{Y_2(s)}{D_2(s)} = \frac{0.5}{s+1 + 1.5} = \frac{0.5}{s + 2.5} $$

**Step 4 — Apply the disturbance input** $D_2(s) = 0.2/s$:

$$ Y_2(s) = \frac{0.5}{s+2.5} \cdot \frac{0.2}{s} = \frac{0.1}{s(s+2.5)} $$

**Step 5 — Apply the Final Value Theorem** for steady-state:

$$ y_{2,ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot Y_2(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{0.1}{s(s+2.5)} = \frac{0.1}{2.5} = 0.04 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A step disturbance of magnitude **0.2** in the flow loop produces a *steady-state* secondary deviation of only **0.04** — an **80 % attenuation**. The slave controller $G_{c2} = 3$ (high gain) provides strong disturbance rejection. Without the inner loop (single-loop), the same disturbance would propagate fully to the primary variable, causing a steady-state deviation of $0.1$ in temperature.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes costing marks in Cascade Control questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply** the inner-loop denominator when expanding the overall closed-loop form. The denominator is $1 + G_{c2}G_{p2}H_2 + G_{c1}G_{c2}G_{p2}G_{p1}H_1$, *not* just $1 + G_{c1}G_{c2}G_{p1}G_{p2}H_1$. **Lose 2 marks** for missing the inner-loop term.
>
> 2. **Tuning order error:** Writing "tune master first" — this is **wrong**. Always tune *slave first*. **Lose 1–2 marks** in viva.
>
> 3. **Units mismatch in IAE/ITAE calculation:** $J_{ITAE} = \int t \cdot \vert e \vert \, dt$ — the time-domain integral must be computed numerically using `trapz` in MATLAB, not analytically. **Lose 1 mark** if formula is written without units.
>
> 4. **Confusing $G_{eq}$ with $G_{p2}$:** Students often write $G_{eq}(s) = G_{p2}(s)$, forgetting the controller action. The correct form includes $G_{c2}$ in the loop.
>
> 5. **Stability check omission:** Always verify Routh-Hurwitz or Nyquist stability for the final 3rd/4th order closed-loop system. **Lose 2 marks** for skipping this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Cascade control = hierarchical multi-loop with *one controller feeding the setpoint* of another.
- **Structure:** Master (outer) $\rightarrow$ Slave (inner) $\rightarrow$ Plant. Two feedback paths.
- **Design Rule 1:** Inner loop *must be faster* — $\omega_{n2} \geq 3 \omega_{n1}$.
- **Design Rule 2:** Secondary variable *must be measurable*, faster, and directly affected by MV.
- **Tuning Order:** **Slave first, then Master** (sequential, never simultaneous).
- **Inner loop controller:** P or PI only (avoid D in fast loop — amplifies noise).
- **Outer loop controller:** PI or PID (use I-action to eliminate steady-state error on primary).
- **Key formula — Inner equivalent:** $G_{eq} = \dfrac{G_{c2}G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2}G_{p2}H_2}$.
- **Key formula — Overall closed loop:** $\dfrac{Y_1}{R_1} = \dfrac{G_{c1}G_{eq}G_{p1}}{1 + G_{c1}G_{eq}G_{p1}H_1}$.
- **Characteristic equation:** $1 + G_{c2}G_{p2}H_2 + G_{c1}G_{c2}G_{p2}G_{p1}H_1 = 0$ — has *two feedback terms*.
- **Disturbance rejection:** $D_2$ (inner) is rejected by slave *before* it reaches primary. $D_1$ (outer) reaches primary but is handled by master.
- **System order:** Order of overall closed-loop = (Plant order) + (number of integrating controllers in master) = typically 3rd or 4th order for industrial cascades.
- **Steady-state gain:** $K_{eq} = \dfrac{K_{c2}K_{p2}}{1 + K_{c2}K_{p2}}$ — *less than 1*, master compensates.
- **Time constant reduction:** $\tau_{eq} = \dfrac{\tau_2}{1 + K_{c2}K_{p2}}$ — the inner loop speeds up the plant.
- **MATLAB verification:** Use `feedback(Gc2*Gp2, 1)` to compute $G_{eq}$, then cascade with `Gc1*Gp1`.
- **Industrial examples:** Temperature–flow, level–flow, composition–flow, pressure–flow, speed–current (in motor drives).
- **Comparison with single-loop:** Cascade gives 30 %–70 % IAE reduction and 2×–5× faster disturbance rejection — *when secondary variable is well chosen*.
- **Common pairing mistakes:** Do NOT cascade (a) two slow variables, (b) two fast variables, (c) primary faster than secondary — *violates bandwidth rule*.
- **Viva favourite:** "What happens if the secondary sensor fails?" → The inner loop opens, the master output is no longer trimmed, control degrades to single-loop. **Answer: install backup / interlock.**
